key_verses.title: One Who Breaks the Sacred Cycle
key_verses.sanskrit:
एवं प्रवर्तितं(ञ्) चक्रं(न्) नानुवर्तयतीह यः ।
अघायुरिन्द्रियारामो(मः) मोघं पार्थ स जीवति ॥
key_verses.transliteration:
evaṁ pravartitaṁ(ñ) cakraṁ(n) nānuvartayatīha yaḥ ।
aghāyurindriyārāmo(aḥ) moghaṁ pārtha sa jīvati ॥
key_verses.one_line_meaning: O Pārtha, one who does not follow this divinely initiated cycle of giving and receiving, and lives only in sense enjoyment, lives a sinful and wasted life.
key_verses.concise_journey: Bhagavān Kṛṣṇa now concludes the yajña-cycle teaching. He has shown the chain: beings depend on food, food depends on rain, rain depends on yajña, yajña depends on karma, karma is taught by the Veda, and the Veda comes from Bhagavān. This is not merely a poetic chain. It is the order by which life is sustained.
key_verses.meaning_detail:
Bhagavān Kṛṣṇa now concludes the yajña-cycle teaching. He has shown the chain: beings depend on food, food depends on rain, rain depends on yajña, yajña depends on karma, karma is taught by the Veda, and the Veda comes from Bhagavān. This is not merely a poetic chain. It is the order by which life is sustained.

The word cakram means cycle. This is the universal cycle of harmony, maintained through proper giving and receiving. Human beings receive from nature, devatās, society, parents, teachers, ancestors, animals, plants, rivers, and Bhagavān’s order. Therefore, human beings must also give back through yajña, dānam, service, sharing, duty, and reverential living.

A karma-yogī is giving-oriented. He measures success by how much he can contribute. If he has wealth, he sees it as an opportunity for dānam. If he has knowledge, he shares it. If he has strength, he serves. If he has time, he helps. For him, life is fulfilled by contribution.

A mere karmī is taking-oriented. He measures success by how much he can grab, hoard, possess, and enjoy. He receives from the cycle but does not support the cycle. He consumes more and shares less. Such a person breaks the yajña-spirit.

Therefore, Bhagavān uses strong words: aghāyuḥ and indriyārāmaḥ. Aghāyuḥ means one whose life is made of pāpa. Indriyārāmaḥ means one who revels only in sense pleasures. This does not condemn normal enjoyment. The problem is a life centered only on personal enjoyment, without gratitude, contribution, or sacrifice.

Such a life is called mogham jīvati — living in vain. A person may be materially successful, but if the life is only grabbing and enjoying, it has missed its spiritual purpose. Human birth becomes meaningful when it participates in yajña, grows in purity, and moves toward mokṣa.
key_verses.story:
King Śibi Protects the Dove (Mahābhārata / Purāṇic Tradition)
King Śibi was known for dharma and compassion. One day, while he was seated in his court, a frightened dove flew into the palace and fell into his lap. The little bird trembled. Behind it came a hawk, hungry and sharp-eyed.
The hawk said, “O King, that dove is my food. By protecting it, you are taking away my rightful meal.”
Śibi faced a real dharma-tension. If he gave up the dove, the helpless creature would die. If he protected the dove, the hawk would go hungry. The king could not simply choose comfort or sentiment. He had to uphold the harmony of life.
Śibi said, “I will protect the dove, but I will not deny your need. I will give you flesh from my own body equal to the weight of the dove.”
A balance was brought. The dove was placed on one side. Śibi cut flesh from his own body and placed it on the other. But the dove seemed heavier and heavier. Finally, Śibi himself climbed onto the scale, offering his whole body to keep his word and protect dharma.
The gods, who had come in disguise, revealed themselves and blessed him. Śibi’s greatness was not measured by what he possessed, but by what he was willing to give.
This story connects directly to 3.16. Bhagavān criticizes the person who refuses to follow the cycle of giving and lives only for sense enjoyment. Śibi is the opposite. He participates in the yajña-cycle through sacrifice, protection, and giving. His life is not mogham, wasted; it becomes meaningful because it is offered for dharma.
key_verses.real_life_example:
This shloka asks us to examine how we measure success. Do we measure life by salary, property, comfort, status, and possessions? Or do we measure life by contribution, sharing, service, maturity, and dharma?

Modern life often praises the taking-oriented person: more income, more upgrades, more consumption, more personal comfort. But Bhagavān asks a different question: “How much are you giving back to the cycle that supports you?”

In daily Indian life, this giving can take many forms: feeding guests, supporting parents, doing dānam, helping temples and gurukulas, teaching children, sharing food, protecting water, supporting farmers, caring for animals, serving society, and doing one’s profession honestly. Even a household becomes sacred when it is not only a place of consumption but a place of offering.

The verse also warns against indriyārāmatā, living only for sense enjoyment. Entertainment, food, comfort, and pleasure are not wrong in themselves. But if life becomes only eating, watching, buying, traveling, comparing, and enjoying, then the higher purpose of human birth is forgotten.

The practical message is: take what you need, enjoy with moderation, and give back generously. A karma-yogī does not ask only, “What did I get?” He asks, “What did I contribute?” That shift makes life meaningful.
key_verses.reflection:
Do I measure success more by what I possess or by what I contribute?
Where am I receiving from the cycle of life but not giving back proportionately?
Is there an area where sense enjoyment has become the center of my life instead of dharma and spiritual growth?

kids_content.explanation_script:
Kṛṣṇa says, “Life works when everyone gives and receives.”
We receive many things. Parents care for us. Teachers teach us. Farmers grow food. Trees give fruits and shade. Rivers give water. Bhagavān’s world gives us so much.
So we should not only take. We should also give.
We can help at home. We can share snacks. We can say thank you. We can avoid wasting food and water. We can help a friend. We can be kind to animals and plants.
A person who only grabs and never shares is not living well. A person who receives and gives back makes life beautiful.
kids_content.story:
Aarav and the Classroom Supplies
Aarav’s classroom had a shelf with pencils, erasers, glue, crayons, and colored paper. The teacher said, “These are for everyone. Use what you need, and keep them nicely for the next person.”
At first, Aarav loved the shelf. He took the sharpest pencils, the biggest eraser, and extra colored paper. He kept some in his bag “just in case.” When other children came, the best supplies were missing.
One day, his friend Mira needed red paper for a project. There was none left. She looked sad. Aarav remembered the red paper in his bag. He felt uncomfortable.
He quietly brought it out and said, “You can use this.” Then he returned the extra pencils and erasers to the shelf. After that, he took only what he needed and helped arrange the supplies after class.
The classroom became happier. Everyone had enough because everyone used and returned things properly.
Kṛṣṇa teaches the same idea. The world is like a big classroom shelf. We receive from it, but we should not only grab. We should share, care, and give back.
kids_content.reflection:
What is one thing I use that I should share or take care of better?
Did I help the people or things that helped me today?

key_verses.detailed_meaning:
Step 1: The divinely initiated cycle
Phrase: evaṁ pravartitaṁ cakram = this cycle set in motion in this way This refers to the yajña-cycle taught in the previous verses: karma, yajña, rain, food, and beings. Bhagavān has initiated a life-order based on giving and receiving.
Step 2: Not following the cycle
Phrase: na anuvartayati iha yaḥ = whoever does not follow it here A person may receive from the world but refuse to participate in giving back. Such a person does not live by yajña, dānam, sharing, and contribution.
Step 3: A life made of pāpa
Phrase: aghāyuḥ = one whose life is sinful Aghāyuḥ means a life filled with pāpa because the person consumes more than he contributes. The life becomes spiritually unhealthy because it violates the yajña-order.
Step 4: One who revels in the senses
Phrase: indriyārāmaḥ = one who delights in sense pleasures This is a person whose life is centered on sense enjoyment. Pleasure itself is not condemned, but a life devoted only to pleasure becomes spiritually wasteful.
Step 5: Living in vain
Phrase: moghaṁ sa jīvati = he lives in vain A human life used only for grabbing, hoarding, and enjoying does not fulfill its purpose. Life becomes meaningful when it is used for karma-yoga, inner purification, and movement toward mokṣa.
Step 6: Address to Arjuna
Phrase: pārtha = O Pārtha By addressing Arjuna directly, Bhagavān makes the teaching practical. Arjuna must not avoid his duty. He must participate in the dhārmic cycle through karma-yoga.
key_verses.more_stories:
1. Mahābali Offers Three Steps to Vāmana (Bhāgavata Purāṇa)
King Mahābali was powerful, generous, and respected. During a great sacrifice, many came to receive gifts from him. The yajña hall was full of activity, priests, guests, and offerings.
Then Vāmana, a small brāhmaṇa boy, entered the assembly. His form was simple, but his presence was radiant. Mahābali welcomed him and asked what he desired. Vāmana requested only three steps of land.
Mahābali’s guru Śukrācārya warned him. This was no ordinary boy. If Mahābali granted the request, he would lose everything. The tension became sharp: should he protect his possessions, or keep his word and give?
Mahābali chose giving. He said that a promise made in yajña must be honored. Vāmana then grew into the cosmic form of Trivikrama. With one step he covered the earth, with another the heavens. There was no place left for the third step. Mahābali bowed and offered his own head.
This story connects to 3.16 because Mahābali did not live as one who only grabs and hoards. Even when giving meant losing everything, he remained in the spirit of yajña. The shloka condemns the sense-centered taker; Mahābali shows the giving-oriented life that makes human birth meaningful.

2. Dadhīci Gives His Bones for the Devas (Bhāgavata Purāṇa / Purāṇic Tradition)
The devas were once threatened by a terrible force they could not defeat. They needed a weapon powerful enough to restore balance, but such a weapon could be made only from the bones of Sage Dadhīci.
The request was not small. It was not a request for wealth, food, or land. It was a request for his very body. The devas approached Dadhīci with reverence and hesitation.
Dadhīci understood the situation. His body had been used for tapas and dharma. Now it could serve one more sacred purpose. He did not cling to the body as an object of private enjoyment.
He gave up his life willingly. From his bones, the thunderbolt weapon was made, and the devas were able to restore order.
This story connects directly to 3.16. The universal cycle is maintained by sacrifice and contribution. Dadhīci’s life was not mogham, wasted in sense pleasure. It became meaningful because he gave even the body for the protection of dharma and the welfare of the world.

3. Karṇa Gives His Kavaca and Kuṇḍala (Mahābhārata)
Karṇa was born with divine armor and earrings, the kavaca and kuṇḍala, which protected him in battle. They were not decorations; they were part of his strength and security.
Before the great war, Indra came to Karṇa disguised as a brāhmaṇa. He asked for the kavaca and kuṇḍala as dānam. Karṇa recognized the danger. If he gave them away, he would become vulnerable. If he refused, his reputation for generosity would be broken.
The tension was severe: personal safety on one side, dāna-dharma on the other. Karṇa chose giving. He cut the armor and earrings from his own body and gave them to the brāhmaṇa.
Indra, moved by Karṇa’s generosity, gave him a powerful weapon in return. But the greatness of Karṇa’s act lay in the sacrifice itself. He gave what was precious, not what was convenient.
This story connects to 3.16 because the verse contrasts the taker and the giver. The indriyārāmaḥ lives for personal enjoyment and security. Karṇa’s dānam shows the opposite movement: willingness to give even what protects oneself. Such giving reflects the yajña-spirit.

4. Puru Gives His Youth to Yayāti (Mahābhārata)
King Yayāti was cursed with old age before his desire for enjoyment had ended. He still longed for youth, pleasure, and worldly enjoyment. He asked his sons if one of them would exchange youth for his old age.
One by one, the sons refused. The request was difficult. To give youth is to give vitality, beauty, energy, and the very season of enjoyment.
Puru, the youngest, agreed. He gave his youth to his father and accepted old age. Yayāti regained youth and enjoyed worldly pleasures for a long time. But eventually he discovered that desire is never exhausted by enjoyment. Desire only grows when fed.
Yayāti returned the youth to Puru and blessed him. Puru’s sacrifice became the foundation of his greatness.
This story connects to 3.16 in two ways. Yayāti’s endless pursuit of enjoyment shows the danger of indriyārāmatā, living for the senses. Puru’s sacrifice shows the yajña-spirit of giving. The shloka warns that a life devoted only to enjoyment is wasted; Puru’s life becomes meaningful through contribution and sacrifice.

5. The Generosity of King Raghū (Raghuvaṁśa / Rāmāyaṇa Tradition)
King Raghū performed a great sacrifice and gave away his wealth in dānam. After the sacrifice, his treasury was nearly empty, but his heart was full because the wealth had served dharma.
Soon after, a student named Kautsa came seeking wealth to offer as guru-dakṣiṇā. Raghū did not say, “I have already given enough.” A king committed to dharma does not measure giving by convenience. He promised to help.
To fulfill that promise, Raghū prepared to obtain wealth even if it required confronting Kubera, the lord of wealth. Seeing his determination and generosity, Kubera caused wealth to rain down. Raghū gave Kautsa what he needed.
This story connects to 3.16 because Raghū measured success by giving, not hoarding. He did not live as an indriyārāmaḥ, centered on enjoyment and possession. His life followed the yajña-cycle: wealth received from the order was returned into dharma through dānam.

rich_grammar.padacchedah: एवम् प्रवर्तितम् चक्रम् न अनुवर्तयति इह यः । अघायुः इन्द्रियारामः मोघम् पार्थ सः जीवति ॥३.१६॥
rich_grammar.pratipadarthah: एवम् | (evam) = in this manner प्रवर्तितम् | (pravartitam) = set in motion, initiated चक्रम् | (cakram) = cycle न | (na) = not अनुवर्तयति | (anuvartayati) = follows, continues in accordance with इह | (iha) = here, in this world यः | (yaḥ) = who अघायुः | (aghāyuḥ) = one whose life is sinful / made of pāpa इन्द्रियारामः | (indriyārāmaḥ) = one who delights in the senses मोघम् | (mogham) = vainly, fruitlessly पार्थ | (pārtha) = O Pārtha सः | (saḥ) = he जीवति | (jīvati) = lives
rich_grammar.anvayah: हे पार्थ, यः इह एवम् प्रवर्तितं चक्रं न अनुवर्तयति, सः अघायुः इन्द्रियारामः मोघं जीवति ।
rich_grammar.sandhi: न + अनुवर्तयति = नानुवर्तयति | अनुवर्तयति + इह = अनुवर्तयतीह | अघायुः + इन्द्रियारामः = अघायुरिन्द्रियारामः | मोघम् + पार्थ = मोघं | सः + जीवति = स
rich_grammar.samasa: अघायुः = अघम् आयुः यस्य सः Samāsa name: बहुव्रीहि समासः; one whose life is made of sin or pāpa. | इन्द्रियारामः = इन्द्रियेषु आरामः यस्य सः Samāsa name: बहुव्रीहि समासः; one whose delight is in the senses.
rich_grammar.other: प्रवर्तितम् is a क्तप्रत्ययान्त-कृदन्तम् from प्र + वृत्, meaning “set in motion” or “initiated.” | चक्रम् is the object of अनुवर्तयति. | अनुवर्तयति is लट्-लकारः, प्रथमपुरुष एकवचनम्: “he follows.” | यः and सः form the relative-correlative structure: “whoever… he…” | मोघम् functions adverbially: “vainly” or “fruitlessly.” | पार्थ is सम्बोधनप्रथमा, directly addressing Arjuna.

key_verses.final_takeaway: One who breaks the sacred cycle of yajña by living only for personal enjoyment lives a wasted life. Participating in the cosmic order of giving and receiving is not optional — it is the foundation of a meaningful human life.

key_verses.title: The Self-Fulfilled Being
key_verses.sanskrit:
यस्त्वात्मरतिरेव स्यात् आत्मतृप्तश्च मानवः ।
आत्मन्येव च सन्तुष्टः(स्) तस्य कार्यं(न्) न विद्यते ॥
key_verses.transliteration:
yastvātmaratireva syāt ātmatṛptaśca mānavaḥ ।
ātmanyeva ca santuṣṭaḥ(s) tasya kāryaṁ(n) na vidyate ॥
key_verses.one_line_meaning: But for the one who delights in the Self, is fulfilled by the Self, and is content in the Self alone, there is no duty to be accomplished for fulfillment.
key_verses.concise_journey: Bhagavān Kṛṣṇa now gives an important exception. Up to the previous verse, he strongly taught that human beings must participate in the yajña-cycle through karma-yoga. A person who only takes, enjoys, hoards, and refuses to contribute lives in vain. That teaching applies to those who are still seeking fulfillment through the world.
key_verses.meaning_detail:
Bhagavān Kṛṣṇa now gives an important exception. Up to the previous verse, he strongly taught that human beings must participate in the yajña-cycle through karma-yoga. A person who only takes, enjoys, hoards, and refuses to contribute lives in vain. That teaching applies to those who are still seeking fulfillment through the world.

But now Bhagavān speaks of a rare person: the jñānī, the one who has discovered fullness in the Self. Such a person is ātma-ratiḥ — one whose delight is in the Self. He is ātma-tṛptaḥ — one who is satisfied by the Self. He is ātmani eva santuṣṭaḥ — one who is content in the Self alone.

This does not mean he hates the world or refuses all activity. It means he no longer depends on the world for inner fullness. Before knowledge, a person often acts for fulfillment: “If I get this, I will be complete; if people love me, I will be secure; if I succeed, I will be happy.” Such action has pressure, anxiety, and dependence.

The jñānī has discovered that fullness is not produced by external acquisition. Fullness is his own nature. Therefore, he does not approach the world with a begging bowl, emotionally asking, “Will this complete me? Will this make me secure?” He is already inwardly full.

So Bhagavān says: tasya kāryaṁ na vidyate — for such a person, there is no duty to be done. This does not mean he becomes lazy or useless. It means there is no obligatory action required for his personal completion. If he acts, he acts out of fullness, not for fullness. Such action is like līlā — free, unpressured, and whole.

This verse must not be misused by ordinary seekers. Until self-knowledge is firm, karma-yoga remains necessary for purification. Only the one who is truly ātma-ratiḥ, ātma-tṛptaḥ, and ātmani eva santuṣṭaḥ is free from the sense of compulsory action for self-fulfillment.
key_verses.story:
Śuka Leaves Home Established in the Self (Bhāgavata Purāṇa / Mahābhārata Tradition)
Śuka, the son of Vyāsa, was inwardly free from a very young age. He was not restless for wealth, marriage, status, praise, or possession. The world that usually pulls the mind outward did not bind him.
One day, Śuka left home to wander as a renunciate. Vyāsa, the great sage and father, felt the pain of separation. Seeing his son walking away, he called after him, “O my son!”
Śuka did not turn back. It was not because he was cold or disrespectful. His heart was not bound by the usual dependence that says, “I am incomplete without this relationship.” He was established in the Self.
A traditional telling says that as Vyāsa called out, the trees echoed back, as though all of nature answered. Śuka had become one with the fullness of Being; he was no longer living as a needy individual seeking completion from the world.
The tension in the story is deep: a father calls, a son walks away, and worldly attachment is tested. Śuka’s action reveals inner fullness. He was not rejecting love; he was free from dependence.
This story connects directly to 3.17. Śuka is an example of one who is ātma-ratiḥ, delighting in the Self, and ātma-tṛptaḥ, fulfilled in the Self. For such a person, there is no action required for personal completion. If he moves, teaches, or lives, it is not to become full; it is from fullness already discovered.
key_verses.real_life_example:
This shloka helps us understand the difference between two kinds of action: action for fulfillment and action out of fulfillment.

Most of us act because we feel incomplete. We may think, “If I get this job, I will be complete. If my child succeeds, I will be complete. If people respect me, I will be complete. If I buy this house, I will be secure. If someone loves me, I will be happy.” Because the action is born from inner insufficiency, it carries anxiety.

This shloka does not tell us to stop acting. It asks us to understand the direction of spiritual growth. Karma-yoga purifies the mind. Jñāna-yoga reveals that the Self is the source of fullness. Then life changes. We may still work, love, serve, teach, cook, study, earn, or help society, but the inner pressure reduces.

In current life, we can use this teaching as a diagnostic. Am I acting because I must prove myself? Am I serving because I want recognition? Am I loving because I am afraid of being alone? Am I working because I believe success alone will make me secure? These questions show whether action is still driven by inner incompleteness.

For most seekers, the practical message is not “I have no duty.” The practical message is: continue karma-yoga, purify the mind, study Vedānta, and gradually discover inner fullness. The freedom described in this shloka belongs to the jñānī. Until then, duty remains a blessing and a discipline.
key_verses.reflection:
Am I acting mainly for inner completion, or can I slowly learn to act as an offering while seeking fullness through Self-knowledge?
What object, relationship, achievement, or role am I treating as the source of my security or happiness?
Do I understand that “no duty” applies only to the one who is truly fulfilled in the Self, not to a mind still seeking fulfillment from the world?

kids_content.explanation_script:
Kṛṣṇa says that a wise person is happy inside.
Most of us want many things to feel happy. We may think, “I will be happy only if I get this toy,” or “I will be happy only if everyone praises me.” But that happiness does not stay for long.
A wise person has found a deeper happiness inside. Such a person can still play, help, study, and talk with others. But the person does not feel empty inside.
For us, this means we should still do our duties. We should study, help, share, and pray. Slowly, we learn that real happiness is not only in toys, marks, praise, or winning. Kṛṣṇa teaches that the deepest happiness is within.
kids_content.story:
Mira and the Star Sticker
Mira loved getting star stickers from her teacher. Whenever she finished her work neatly, the teacher placed a shiny star on her notebook. Mira would smile the whole day.
One day, Mira worked very carefully. Her letters were neat. Her drawing was colored beautifully. But the teacher was busy helping another child and forgot to give her a star.
Mira’s face fell. She thought, “My work is not good unless I get a star.”
At home, her grandmother looked at the notebook and said, “Mira, did you do your best?”
Mira nodded. “Yes, Paati. But I did not get a star.”
Grandmother smiled. “The star is nice. But your careful work is already good. Can you feel happy that you did it well?”
Mira looked again at her page. She had written slowly, stayed patient, and finished the work. A small smile came back.
The next day, she still liked getting stars. But she also felt happy when she knew she had done her best.
Kṛṣṇa teaches something deeper like this. We can enjoy praise, toys, and winning, but we should not depend only on them for happiness. A wise person has happiness inside and acts from that fullness.
kids_content.reflection:
Do I feel happy only when someone praises me?
Can I feel glad when I do something well, even if nobody gives me a prize?

key_verses.detailed_meaning:
Step 1: The contrast
Phrase: yaḥ tu = but the one who The word tu marks a contrast with the previous verse. In 3.16, Bhagavān criticized the person who lives only for sense enjoyment and does not follow the yajña-cycle. Now he speaks of the rare jñānī who is inwardly full.
Step 2: Delight in the Self
Phrase: ātma-ratiḥ eva syāt = one who delights in the Self alone Ātma-ratiḥ means one whose joy is in the Self. The person does not depend on external objects, people, or achievements for inner delight.
Step 3: Fulfilled by the Self
Phrase: ātma-tṛptaḥ ca mānavaḥ = a person who is satisfied by the Self Ātma-tṛptaḥ means one who is fulfilled by the Self. He does not feel emotionally bankrupt or incomplete. He does not seek the world as the source of fullness.
Step 4: Content in the Self alone
Phrase: ātmani eva ca santuṣṭaḥ = content in the Self alone This repeats and strengthens the teaching. The jñānī’s contentment is not dependent on circumstances. It is rooted in Self-knowledge.
Step 5: No duty to be accomplished
Phrase: tasya kāryaṁ na vidyate = for him, there is no duty There is no action that must be done for his personal fulfillment. He may act, but his action is not driven by incompleteness. His action is out of fullness, like līlā.
Step 6: Teaching movement
Phrase: whole shloka The verse does not cancel karma-yoga for ordinary seekers. It only describes the one who has completed the journey through Self-knowledge. Until that fullness is discovered, karma-yoga remains necessary.
key_verses.more_stories:
1. Jaḍa Bharata Teaches King Rahūgaṇa (Bhāgavata Purāṇa)
Jaḍa Bharata lived quietly, appearing dull and ordinary to others. People did not recognize his wisdom because he did not display learning, status, or social importance. He had discovered inner freedom and did not seek recognition from the world.
One day, King Rahūgaṇa’s attendants needed someone to carry the royal palanquin. They saw Jaḍa Bharata and forced him to join the bearers. As he walked, Jaḍa Bharata carefully avoided stepping on tiny living beings. Because of this, the palanquin moved unevenly.
The king became irritated and mocked him. From the outside, it looked as though Jaḍa Bharata was only a poor carrier being scolded by a king. But Jaḍa Bharata was inwardly untouched. He replied with deep wisdom, teaching that the Self is not the body and that the real “I” is not the carrier, the king, or the servant.
Rahūgaṇa was shaken. He realized that the man he had insulted was a knower of truth. He got down from the palanquin, bowed, and sought instruction.
Here we see the teaching of 3.17 in living form. Jaḍa Bharata was not acting for recognition, comfort, or status. Even when forced into action, he was inwardly full and unattached. His action was not for self-completion; he represents the one who is content in the Self.

2. Dattātreya Teaches King Yadu as an Avadhūta (Bhāgavata Purāṇa)
King Yadu once saw a wandering avadhūta moving freely, without anxiety, possessions, or visible dependence. The king was surprised. Most people are worried about wealth, family, status, comfort, and protection. This person appeared inwardly happy without any of these supports.
Yadu approached him and asked, “How are you so joyful and free while living in the world without possessions?” The avadhūta, Dattātreya, answered that he had learned from many teachers in nature — earth, air, sky, water, fire, birds, animals, and many others.
He did not speak as a person trying to impress a king. He spoke from fullness. He had discovered how to live without inner dependence on external objects. The world had become a teacher, not a chain.
The king listened with reverence. The meeting revealed that true freedom is not produced by possessions. It comes from wisdom and inner contentment.
This episode illustrates the verse by showing a person who moves through the world without seeking completion from it. Dattātreya is satisfied within and not dependent on worldly acquisition. He is a living example of ātma-tṛptaḥ and ātmani eva santuṣṭaḥ.

3. Ṛṣabha Walks Away from Royal Life (Bhāgavata Purāṇa)
Ṛṣabha was a great king and father. He ruled, guided, and fulfilled his responsibilities. But after instructing his sons, especially Bharata, he renounced royal life and walked away as an avadhūta.
The scene is striking. A king who could have enjoyed power, comfort, praise, and royal pleasures left them behind. He wandered without concern for public opinion. Some people misunderstood him. Some mocked him. Some saw only an unconventional wanderer.
But Ṛṣabha was not acting out of disappointment or failure. He was inwardly free. He had no need to prove himself through a throne, palace, or public honor.
His life after renunciation showed that fullness was not in kingship. Kingship had been a role; the Self was his reality. Having fulfilled his duties, he moved without dependence.
The shloka’s message becomes clear through this moment. Ṛṣabha shows the state of one who is no longer compelled by worldly roles for fulfillment. He may act while duties remain, but once established in inner fullness, there is no action required for personal completion.

4. Sanatkumāra Leads Nārada Beyond Name and Learning (Chāndogya Upaniṣad)
Nārada approached Sanatkumāra with great learning. He knew many branches of knowledge: Vedas, grammar, rituals, sciences, arts, and sacred disciplines. From the outside, he seemed highly accomplished.
But he was honest. He said, “I know the mantras, but I do not know the Self. I am sorrowful. Please take me beyond sorrow.”
Sanatkumāra began teaching him step by step, taking him beyond name, speech, mind, will, thought, meditation, understanding, strength, food, water, fire, space, memory, hope, and prāṇa. Finally, the teaching led toward Bhūmā, the Infinite.
The tension in the story is powerful: vast learning had not given Nārada complete fulfillment. He still knew that something was missing. Sanatkumāra guided him toward the fullness that is not dependent on limited objects.
Through this incident, we understand 3.17 more deeply. External learning and accomplishment do not by themselves give the contentment described in the verse. The goal is Self-knowledge, by which one becomes inwardly complete and no longer seeks fulfillment from the finite.

5. Vāmadeva Knows the Self Even in the Womb (Bṛhadāraṇyaka Upaniṣad Tradition)
The Upaniṣadic tradition speaks of Vāmadeva, who realized the truth of the Self even while still in the womb. This is presented as an extraordinary case, showing the power of knowledge carried from deep spiritual maturity.
Before birth itself, he is said to have known, “I was Manu and Sūrya.” This does not mean ordinary egoistic pride. It expresses recognition of the Self as not limited to one body or one individual role.
The tension is between ordinary identity and true identity. Most people are born into identification with body, name, family, and status. Vāmadeva’s knowledge breaks that limitation at once.
Such realization is rare and exceptional. It is not a model for ordinary seekers to imitate externally. It shows what inner freedom means when Self-knowledge is complete.
This final episode highlights the highest sense of the verse. One who knows the Self as fullness is not waiting for later achievements, roles, or possessions to become complete. For such a knower, there is no duty required to become whole; fullness is already recognized as the Self.

rich_grammar.padacchedah: यः तु आत्मरतिः एव स्यात् आत्मतृप्तः च मानवः । आत्मनि एव च सन्तुष्टः तस्य कार्यम् न विद्यते ॥३.१७॥
rich_grammar.pratipadarthah: यः | (yaḥ) = who तु | (tu) = but, however आत्मरतिः | (ātmaratiḥ) = one who delights in the Self एव | (eva) = alone, indeed स्यात् | (syāt) = may be, would be, is आत्मतृप्तः | (ātmatṛptaḥ) = fulfilled by the Self च | (ca) = and मानवः | (mānavaḥ) = human being, person आत्मनि | (ātmani) = in the Self एव | (eva) = alone च | (ca) = and सन्तुष्टः | (santuṣṭaḥ) = contented, satisfied तस्य | (tasya) = for him / of him कार्यम् | (kāryam) = duty, action to be done न | (na) = not विद्यते | (vidyate) = exists, is found
rich_grammar.anvayah: यः तु मानवः आत्मरतिः एव स्यात्, आत्मतृप्तः च, आत्मनि एव च सन्तुष्टः, तस्य कार्यं न विद्यते ।
rich_grammar.sandhi: यः + तु = यस्तु | तु + आत्मरतिः = त्वात्मरतिः | आत्मरतिः + एव = आत्मरतिरेव | स्यात् + आत्मतृप्तः = स्यादात्मतृप्तः | आत्मतृप्तः + च = आत्मतृप्तश्च | आत्मनि + एव = आत्मन्येव | सन्तुष्टः + तस्य = सन्तुष्टस्तस्य | कार्यम् + न = कार्यं
rich_grammar.samasa: आत्मरतिः = आत्मनि रतिः यस्य सः Samāsa sense: बहुव्रीहि / locative-based adjectival sense; one whose delight is in the Self. | आत्मतृप्तः = आत्मना तृप्तः Samāsa sense: तृतीया-तत्पुरुष-like expression; satisfied by the Self.
rich_grammar.other: स्यात् is विधिलिङ् from अस्, used here in the sense “may be” or “suppose there is.” | सन्तुष्टः is a क्तप्रत्ययान्त-कृदन्तम् from सम् + तुष्, meaning “contented” or “satisfied.” | तस्य is षष्ठी विभक्ति, used in the sense “for him.” | कार्यम् is the subject of विद्यते: “duty exists.” With न, it means “no duty exists.” | विद्यते is लट्-लकारः, आत्मनेपदम्, meaning “exists” or “is found.” | The three expressions आत्मरतिः, आत्मतृप्तः, and आत्मनि सन्तुष्टः all describe the same person and emphasize fullness in the Self from three angles.

key_verses.final_takeaway: The jñānī who is fulfilled in the Self has no obligatory duty. This is not laziness or escapism — it is the natural state of one whose mind rests in completeness, free from the need to gain or protect anything through action.

key_verses.title: Beyond Action and Inaction
key_verses.sanskrit:
नैव तस्य कृतेनार्थो(:) नाकृतेनेह कश्चन ।
न चास्य सर्वभूतेषु कश्चिदर्थव्यपाश्रयः ॥३.१८॥
key_verses.transliteration:
naiva tasya kṛtenārtho(aḥ) nākṛteneha kaścana ।
na cāsya sarvabhūteṣu kaścidarthavyapāśrayaḥ ॥3.18॥
key_verses.one_line_meaning: For the Self-fulfilled jñānī, there is nothing to gain through action, nothing to lose through inaction, and no dependence on any being for any purpose.
key_verses.concise_journey: Bhagavān Kṛṣṇa continues the description of the jñānī from the previous shloka. In 3.17, he described the one who delights in the Self, is fulfilled in the Self, and is content in the Self alone. Now he explains the practical consequence of that fullness.
key_verses.meaning_detail:
Bhagavān Kṛṣṇa continues the description of the jñānī from the previous shloka. In 3.17, he described the one who delights in the Self, is fulfilled in the Self, and is content in the Self alone. Now he explains the practical consequence of that fullness.

For such a person, naiva tasya kṛtena arthaḥ — there is nothing to be gained through action. This does not mean that action is bad. It means the jñānī does not depend on action for happiness, security, worth, or completion. He may act, but action is not a psychological necessity.

Nor does he depend on inaction. Na akṛtena iha kaścana — there is nothing gained by not acting either. Some people are addicted to action; they cannot sit quietly. Others are addicted to inaction; they avoid every responsibility. The jñānī is free from both addictions. He can act when action is appropriate, and he can remain quiet when action is not required.

This is because he does not look upon the world as a necessity for inner fullness. Whatever is around him may be enjoyed as a luxury, but not depended upon as a necessity. If it is present, fine. If it is absent, nothing essential is lost. This is inner freedom.

The last part says: na ca asya sarvabhūteṣu kaścid artha-vyapāśrayaḥ — he has no dependence on any being for any purpose. This does not mean he hates people or avoids relationships. It means he does not emotionally lean on anyone for self-completion. Relationships may continue, duties may continue, compassion may continue, but dependence is gone.

For ordinary seekers, this verse is not a license to avoid duties. It describes the result of Self-knowledge. Until such fullness is discovered, karma-yoga and jñāna-yoga remain necessary. The jñānī is free because he has discovered fullness as himself.
key_verses.story:
King Janaka Remains Free While Ruling Mithilā (Mahābhārata / Vedāntic Tradition)
King Janaka ruled Mithilā with wisdom and attention. He was not careless about his kingdom. Ministers came to him with matters of law, agriculture, protection, learning, and public welfare. He acted fully as a king.
Yet Janaka was known as a jñānī. His inner freedom was tested in a striking way. While he was in an assembly of sages, news came that Mithilā was burning. For most people, such news would bring panic: palace, wealth, property, status, and security could all disappear.
Janaka remained calm. He is traditionally remembered as saying, “If Mithilā burns, nothing of mine is burnt.” This was not indifference toward people or negligence of duty. It was freedom from inner dependence. He knew that his real Self was not the palace, treasury, land, or royal identity.
He could rule when ruling was required. He could also remain inwardly undisturbed when possessions were threatened. His action as king did not come from incompleteness; nor did his freedom require running away from action.
Here the teaching of 3.18 becomes visible. Janaka had nothing to gain through action for self-completion, nothing to gain through inaction, and no dependence on external possessions or beings for his inner fullness. The kingdom was a field of duty, not the source of his Self.
key_verses.real_life_example:
This shloka helps us examine our dependencies. Many of us depend on action for identity. We feel valuable only when we are busy, productive, praised, needed, or successful. If a day is quiet, we feel useless. This is dependence on action.

Others depend on inaction. They avoid effort, responsibility, discomfort, and commitment. They call it peace, but often it is tamas or fear. This is dependence on inaction.

The jñānī is free from both. He can act without becoming a workaholic, and he can rest without becoming lazy. He can relate without clinging, and he can be alone without loneliness. He can enjoy what comes without being shattered when it goes.

For most of us, this is not yet our state. But the verse gives us a direction. We can slowly convert necessities into luxuries. A luxury means: “When it is present, I enjoy it; when it is absent, I do not collapse.” A necessity means: “Without this, I am incomplete.” Spiritual growth is the journey from dependence to inner freedom.

In practical life, we can ask: Is my phone a tool or a necessity? Is praise a pleasant bonus or a requirement? Is work a contribution or my only identity? Are relationships sacred connections or emotional crutches? These questions show us where karma-yoga and jñāna-yoga must continue.

The practical message is: act when action is needed, rest when rest is appropriate, love people without clinging, and seek fullness through Self-knowledge, not through compulsive doing or avoiding.
key_verses.reflection:
Am I more dependent on action, always needing to be busy, or on inaction, avoiding what needs to be done?
What person, role, object, or result have I turned from a luxury into a necessity?
Can I begin to enjoy what is present without depending on it for my sense of completeness?

kids_content.explanation_script:
Kṛṣṇa says that a very wise person is happy inside and does not need outside things to feel complete.
Some children feel happy only when they are playing. If there is no game, they become upset. Some children feel happy only when they get praise. If nobody praises them, they feel sad. Some children feel happy only when they win. If they lose, they feel terrible.
A wise person can enjoy games, praise, and winning, but does not depend on them. If they come, nice. If they do not come, the person is still okay inside.
For us, this means we should do our duties, enjoy good things, and also learn to be peaceful when things do not go exactly our way.
kids_content.story:
Neel and the Rainy Day
Neel loved playing cricket after school. Every day he waited for the last bell. He would run home, change clothes, and rush outside with his bat.
One afternoon, dark clouds filled the sky. By the time Neel reached home, rain was pouring. The cricket ground was full of puddles.
Neel threw his bag down. “This is the worst day! I can’t play cricket. Now there is nothing to do.”
His older sister said, “Cricket is fun, but is cricket the only way to have a good day?”
Neel frowned. At first he did not want to listen. Then his sister brought out a board game. After that, they helped Amma make pakoras. Later, Neel read a comic and watched the rain from the window.
By evening, he smiled. “I still like cricket best. But today was not ruined.”
The next time rain came, Neel felt disappointed, but not destroyed. He learned that a game can be enjoyed without becoming the only source of happiness.
Kṛṣṇa’s teaching is much deeper, but this is the beginning. We can enjoy things without depending on them completely. A wise person is peaceful inside whether action happens or does not happen.
kids_content.reflection:
What is one thing I enjoy a lot but should not depend on for all my happiness?
Can I stay calm when my favorite plan changes?

key_verses.detailed_meaning:
Step 1: Nothing to gain through action
Phrase: na eva tasya kṛtena arthaḥ = for him, there is indeed nothing to gain through action For the jñānī, action is not a means of becoming complete. He may act, but he does not expect action to give him inner fullness.
Step 2: Nothing to gain through inaction
Phrase: na akṛtena iha kaścana = nor is there anything here through inaction The jñānī is not dependent on inaction either. He does not think, “Only if I withdraw and remain inactive will I be fulfilled.” He is free whether acting or not acting.
Step 3: No dependence on beings
Phrase: na ca asya sarvabhūteṣu kaścit artha-vyapāśrayaḥ = and for him there is no dependence on any being for any purpose The jñānī does not depend on people, possessions, roles, or results for inner completion. He may love, serve, teach, and relate, but without emotional dependence for self-worth.
Step 4: Teaching movement
Phrase: whole shloka This verse completes the description begun in 3.17. The jñānī is full in the Self; therefore, neither action nor inaction nor any being is required for his fullness. This is not laziness. It is freedom born of Self-knowledge.
key_verses.more_stories:
1. Aṣṭāvakra Awakens King Janaka to Inner Freedom (Aṣṭāvakra Gītā Tradition)
King Janaka sat in a royal assembly surrounded by scholars, priests, ministers, and seekers. He was a ruler of great power, but he was not satisfied with power alone. Deep within, he wanted direct knowledge of the Self.
Into that assembly came Aṣṭāvakra, a young sage whose body was bent in eight places. Many in the court saw only his unusual form and quietly laughed. Aṣṭāvakra saw their reaction and laughed in return. When Janaka asked why he laughed, Aṣṭāvakra replied that he had come expecting wise people, but found people who judged only by skin and shape.
The court fell silent.
Janaka recognized that this was no ordinary boy. Here was someone who saw beyond the body. The king humbled himself and asked for teaching. Aṣṭāvakra then unfolded the truth of the Self: the Self is unattached, actionless, free, and untouched by the body, mind, kingdom, or role.
Janaka listened deeply. The teaching struck him like lightning. He saw that he was not merely a king, not merely a doer, not merely a ruler of Mithilā. The Self was ever free. After this, he could continue ruling, but the throne no longer defined him. He could act, but action was no longer needed to complete him.
This episode brings out 3.18 with great clarity. The jñānī has nothing to gain through action and nothing to gain through inaction. Janaka did not need to abandon the throne to become full, nor did he need the throne to remain full. His fullness was in the Self.

2. Prahlāda Remains Fearless Before Hiraṇyakaśipu (Bhāgavata Purāṇa)
Prahlāda was only a child, but he lived in the palace of a terrifying father. Hiraṇyakaśipu wanted everyone to bow to his power. The palace walls echoed with command, fear, and pride. Servants trembled. Ministers obeyed. Teachers tried to shape Prahlāda according to the king’s will.
But Prahlāda’s heart rested in Bhagavān.
Again and again, Hiraṇyakaśipu asked him, “Who is supreme?” Prahlāda did not flatter him. He calmly spoke of Nārāyaṇa. The king’s anger grew. Persuasion failed. Threats failed. Punishments failed. Prahlāda was placed before elephants, poison, fire, weapons, and danger, but his mind did not collapse.
He was not protected by worldly strength. He did not have an army. He did not have political power. Even his father stood against him. Yet he was not inwardly helpless, because his security was not borrowed from any person.
Finally, Hiraṇyakaśipu pointed to a pillar and demanded, “Is your Lord in this pillar too?” Prahlāda said yes. The pillar split, and Bhagavān appeared as Narasiṁha. The king’s arrogance ended, and Prahlāda’s steadiness was revealed to all.
This moment shows the meaning of na cāsya sarvabhūteṣu kaścidarthavyapāśrayaḥ. Prahlāda did not depend on any being for inner security. Approval did not complete him; threat did not destroy him. His support was not external, and therefore his heart remained free.

3. Bhīṣma Teaches Dharma from the Bed of Arrows (Mahābhārata)
The war of Kurukṣetra had ended, but peace had not yet entered Yudhiṣṭhira’s heart. The battlefield was silent, the warriors were gone, and the burden of kingship felt unbearable. Yudhiṣṭhira wondered how he could rule after so much destruction.
Bhīṣma lay on the bed of arrows, waiting for the proper time to leave the body. His body was pierced, but his mind was clear like a still flame. Around him stood Kṛṣṇa, the Pāṇḍavas, sages, and elders. The scene was solemn: one of the greatest warriors of the age was leaving, but before leaving, he still had wisdom to give.
Yudhiṣṭhira approached him with humility and grief. Bhīṣma did not say, “I have suffered enough; I owe nothing more.” He also did not teach to gain fame, power, or reward. His life’s ambitions were over. There was nothing left for him to achieve.
Yet when dharma required speech, he spoke. He taught Yudhiṣṭhira about kingship, duty, ethics, devotion, social order, and the path of a ruler. His body was helpless, but his wisdom nourished a kingdom.
The teaching of 3.18 is visible here. Bhīṣma had nothing personal to gain through action. He also had nothing to gain through refusal or silence. His teaching did not arise from need; it flowed from clarity. Such action is not bondage, because it is not performed for self-completion.

4. The Buddha Sits Unmoved Beneath the Bodhi Tree (Buddhist Tradition)
Siddhārtha had already seen the limits of palace pleasure. He had seen sickness, old age, death, and the sorrow of human life. He had also practiced severe austerity and discovered that tormenting the body did not give final freedom. Having seen both indulgence and extreme hardship, he sat beneath the Bodhi tree with a firm resolve.
He would not rise until he understood.
As the night deepened, Māra appeared. Temptation came. Fear came. Doubt came. The pull of desire said, “Return to pleasure.” The pressure of fear said, “You are alone.” The voice of doubt said, “Who are you to seek awakening?” The world seemed to throw every inner obstacle before him.
Siddhārtha remained still.
He did not run toward action to escape silence. He did not run toward inaction out of dullness. He simply remained awake, steady, and clear. At dawn, awakening arose. The search that had driven him through palace life, renunciation, austerity, and meditation came to completion.
After awakening, he did not need the world to complete him. Yet he walked, taught, guided, and served for many years. His teaching activity was not born from lack; it came from realization.
This story reflects the principle of 3.18. The awakened one is not dependent on action for fulfillment, nor on inaction. Sitting silently or teaching the world, he remains free because fulfillment is no longer sought from outside.

5. Ramaṇa Maharṣi Sits in Arunācala’s Stillness (Modern Vedāntic Tradition)
As a young boy, Venkataraman was suddenly seized by the fear of death. Instead of calling for help or running away, he turned completely inward. He lay still and asked, “What dies? The body may become stiff and silent, but am I the body?”
In that intense inquiry, he recognized the deathless Self.
Soon after, he left home for Arunācala. He did not go there to build an institution, gather followers, or become famous. In the early years, he sat absorbed in silence. People sometimes ignored him, sometimes disturbed him, sometimes cared for him. His body was there, but his identity was no longer centered on the body.
Later, seekers began to gather. Some came with questions. Some came with devotion. Some came only to sit in silence. Ramaṇa answered when needed, remained silent when silence was enough, and lived with extraordinary simplicity.
He did not depend on teaching to be fulfilled. He did not depend on silence to be fulfilled. He did not depend on disciples, āśrama, fame, or recognition. Whether people came or did not come, the inner fullness remained untouched.
This life beautifully illustrates 3.18. For the jñānī, action is not a necessity, inaction is not a necessity, and people are not emotional supports for self-completion. Teaching may happen, silence may happen, relationships may happen, solitude may happen — but fullness belongs to the Self alone.

rich_grammar.padacchedah: न एव तस्य कृतेन अर्थः न अकृतेन इह कश्चन । न च अस्य सर्वभूतेषु कश्चित् अर्थव्यपाश्रयः ॥३.१८॥
rich_grammar.pratipadarthah: न | (na) = not एव | (eva) = indeed, at all तस्य | (tasya) = for him / of him कृतेन | (kṛtena) = by action done, through action अर्थः | (arthaḥ) = purpose, gain, benefit न | (na) = not अकृतेन | (akṛtena) = by inaction, through non-performance इह | (iha) = here, in this world कश्चन | (kaścana) = anything whatsoever न | (na) = not च | (ca) = and अस्य | (asya) = for him / of him सर्वभूतेषु | (sarvabhūteṣu) = among all beings कश्चित् | (kaścit) = any अर्थव्यपाश्रयः | (arthavyapāśrayaḥ) = dependence for any purpose or gain
rich_grammar.anvayah: तस्य कृतेन अर्थः न एव । इह अकृतेन कश्चन अर्थः न । अस्य सर्वभूतेषु कश्चित् अर्थव्यपाश्रयः न च ।
rich_grammar.sandhi: न + एव = नैव | कृतेन + अर्थः = कृतेनार्थः | अर्थः + न = अर्थो | न + अकृतेन = नाकृतेन | अकृतेन + इह = अकृतेनेह | च + अस्य = चास्य | कश्चित् + अर्थव्यपाश्रयः = कश्चिदर्थव्यपाश्रयः
rich_grammar.samasa: अकृतेन = न कृतेन Samāsa sense: नञ्-तत्पुरुष; through what is not done, through inaction. | सर्वभूतेषु = सर्वेषु भूतेषु Samāsa name: कर्मधारय / adjective-noun compound sense; among all beings. | अर्थव्यपाश्रयः = अर्थस्य व्यपाश्रयः Samāsa sense: षष्ठी-तत्पुरुष; dependence for a purpose, gain, or need.
rich_grammar.other: कृतेन and अकृतेन are तृतीया एकवचनम् forms, indicating means: “through action” and “through inaction.” | अर्थः is the subject in the first clause: “gain/purpose does not exist.” | कश्चन and कश्चित् are indefinite pronouns used with negation to mean “nothing whatsoever” or “no dependence whatsoever.” | सर्वभूतेषु is सप्तमी बहुवचनम्, indicating the field in which dependence is absent: “among all beings.” | अर्थव्यपाश्रयः is the subject of the implied “exists” in the final clause: “no dependence exists.” | The verse uses repeated negation to show total independence: no dependence on action, no dependence on inaction, and no dependence on any being.

key_verses.final_takeaway: For the Self-realized person, there is nothing to gain through action and nothing to lose through inaction. Such a being has no dependence on anything external — not people, not results, not circumstances — for peace and fulfillment.

key_verses.title: Act Without Attachment
key_verses.sanskrit:
तस्मादसक्तः(स्) सततं(ङ्) कार्यं(ङ्) कर्म समाचर ।
असक्तो ह्याचरन्कर्म परमाप्नोति पूरुषः ॥
key_verses.transliteration:
tasmādasaktaḥ(s) satataṁ(ṅ) kāryaṁ(ṅ) karma samācara ।
asakto hyācarankarma paramāpnoti pūruṣaḥ ॥
key_verses.one_line_meaning: Therefore, always perform obligatory action without attachment; for by performing action without attachment, a person attains the Supreme.
key_verses.concise_journey: Having described the jñānī who is fulfilled in the Self (3.17) and who has nothing to gain through action or inaction (3.18), Bhagavān Kṛṣṇa now draws the practical conclusion for the seeker. Since most people are not yet established in Self-knowledge, the path forward is niṣkāma karma — performing one's duties without attachment to results. This is the direct instruction to Arjuna and to every sincere seeker.
key_verses.meaning_detail:
This verse is the practical conclusion of the teaching that began in 3.9. Bhagavān Kṛṣṇa has explained that the world functions through the yajña-cycle (3.10–16), that the jñānī is free from obligation (3.17), and that such a person has no dependence on anything external (3.18). Now he turns to Arjuna and says: therefore — tasmāt — since you are not yet a jñānī, act without attachment.

The word asaktaḥ is the key to karma-yoga. Attachment here means the psychological grip of doership, ownership, and result-dependence. When a person acts while thinking 'I am the doer, this result must come to me, and my happiness depends on this outcome,' the action binds. When the same action is performed as an offering — with the attitude of Īśvara-arpaṇa (dedication to the Lord) and prasāda-buddhi (accepting results as the Lord's grace) — the action purifies.

Bhagavān uses two words: kāryam and karma. Kāryam means 'what ought to be done' — obligatory, contextually appropriate action. It is not whimsical activity. It includes svadharma (one's duties based on station and stage of life), nitya-karma (daily disciplines like prayer and study), and naimittika-karma (occasional duties). Karma samācara means 'perform action well.' The prefix sam- indicates thoroughness — not half-hearted, grudging effort, but wholehearted engagement without the disease of attachment.

The second line gives the promise: asakto hi ācaran karma param āpnoti pūruṣaḥ — performing action without attachment, a person attains the Supreme. 'Param' here means mokṣa, liberation. But how does action lead to liberation? Not directly. Action performed without attachment purifies the mind (cittaśuddhi). A purified mind becomes fit for Self-knowledge (jñāna-yogyatā). Through śravaṇa, manana, and nididhyāsana, knowledge arises. Through knowledge, liberation is recognized. So the chain is: niṣkāma karma → cittaśuddhi → jñāna-yogyatā → jñānam → mokṣa.

Śaṅkarācārya emphasizes that action itself does not produce mokṣa. Mokṣa is not a product of action but of knowledge. However, action without attachment removes the mental impurities that obstruct knowledge. This is why karma-yoga is called an indirect means (sādhana) to liberation. The verse beautifully bridges the practical and the ultimate: act now, purify now, and through that purification, the Supreme becomes accessible through knowledge.
key_verses.story:
Vidura Serves Dharma Without Personal Ambition (Mahābhārata)
In the royal court of Hastināpura, Vidura held a unique position. He was the son of Vyāsa, born through a servant, and therefore could never sit on the throne. Despite possessing wisdom equal to or greater than many kings, the circumstances of his birth placed him permanently outside the line of royal succession.
Yet Vidura did not become bitter or withdrawn. He served as the chief minister and advisor to the Kuru dynasty. When Dhṛtarāṣṭra made decisions driven by attachment to Duryodhana, Vidura spoke the truth. When the court conspired against the Pāṇḍavas, Vidura warned Yudhiṣṭhira. When Draupadī was dishonored, Vidura protested.
His actions were not performed for reward. He knew that speaking truth in Dhṛtarāṣṭra's court would not bring him power or recognition. Indeed, Duryodhana despised him. Yet Vidura continued to act because it was the right thing to do — kāryaṁ karma.
When the situation became intolerable, Vidura left the court. He did not leave in anger or resentment. He walked away knowing that he had done what was required. His departure, like his service, was without attachment.
Vidura's life illustrates 3.19 perfectly. He performed his duty thoroughly (samācara), without attachment to results (asaktaḥ), consistently (satatam). His inner freedom did not come from avoiding action but from acting without the disease of self-interest. Through that purity of action, he remained one of the wisest and most respected figures in the entire Mahābhārata.
key_verses.real_life_example:
Consider a teacher who has been teaching for twenty years. In the early years, she worked hard because she wanted recognition — the best teacher award, praise from parents, promotion. Every lesson was subtly oriented toward 'What will this get me?' When results came, she was elated; when they did not come, she was crushed.

Over the years, something shifted. She began to see teaching as her dharma, not as a transaction. She still prepared carefully, still explained with passion, still cared deeply about her students. But the inner calculation — 'What do I get from this?' — gradually weakened. She taught because teaching needed to be done, because these children needed guidance, because this was her place in the world.

This shift from result-driven action to duty-driven action is what 3.19 describes. The external action may look identical. The quality of engagement may even improve. But the inner orientation changes from 'I act to gain' to 'I act because it is right.' That change of orientation is the difference between binding karma and liberating karma-yoga.

In our own lives, whether we are students, professionals, parents, or volunteers, we can begin this practice. Do the work thoroughly. Do not do it carelessly. But slowly release the tight grip on outcomes. Let results come as they will — as prasāda. This does not mean apathy. It means freedom from the anxiety that makes us slaves to outcomes.
key_verses.reflection:
Am I performing my current duties wholeheartedly, or am I doing them half-heartedly because the results I want have not come?
What attachment — to praise, success, control, or recognition — makes my action pressured rather than free?
Can I act today with full effort and full surrender of the result?

kids_content.explanation_script:
Kṛṣṇa tells Arjuna something very important here. He says: do your work properly, but do not keep worrying about what you will get from it.
Imagine you are helping your mother clean the house. If you help only because she promised you ice cream, then your mind is on the ice cream, not on the cleaning. If the ice cream does not come, you feel cheated. But if you help because it is the right thing to do and because you love your family, then even without ice cream, you feel good inside.
Kṛṣṇa says that when we do our work without being greedy about results, something wonderful happens. Our heart becomes clean and peaceful. And a clean, peaceful heart can learn the deepest truths about life.
kids_content.story:
Riya and the Science Project
Riya loved science. Her teacher announced a science fair. Riya was excited — not because she loved science, but because the winner would get a big shiny trophy.
She worked on her project every day. But her mind was always on the trophy. She kept imagining her name being called. She imagined holding the trophy in front of everyone. She even practiced her winning smile.
On the day of the fair, another student won. Riya was heartbroken. She threw her project notes in the dustbin and said, "I wasted my time."
Her father sat with her that evening. He said, "Riya, did you learn anything while working on your project?"
Riya thought about it. She had learned how plants drink water. She had learned how to use a magnifying glass. She had built something with her own hands.
"Yes," she said slowly. "I learned a lot."
"Then was it really wasted?" her father asked.
Riya picked her notes out of the dustbin. She realized that the real treasure was what she had learned, not the trophy.
The next year, Riya worked on her project with full effort, but this time she enjoyed the process. She still wanted to do well, but the trophy did not control her happiness.
Kṛṣṇa teaches exactly this. Do your best work, but do not let the result control your peace. When you work without being greedy, you grow in a beautiful way.
kids_content.reflection:
Do I enjoy doing my work, or do I only enjoy the prize at the end?
Can I do something kind today without expecting anything in return?

key_verses.detailed_meaning:
Step 1 — Therefore (tasmāt)
The word tasmāt means 'therefore' and draws a conclusion from 3.17–18. Since the jñānī is free and you (Arjuna) are not yet a jñānī, the practical path is karma-yoga. This word connects the ideal (jñāna-niṣṭhā) with the practical discipline (karma-niṣṭhā).

Step 2 — Without attachment (asaktaḥ)
Asaktaḥ means 'without attachment.' This is the defining quality of karma-yoga. Attachment here includes attachment to doership (kartṛtva), enjoyership (bhoktṛtva), and results (phala). The karma-yogī acts with Īśvara-arpaṇa-buddhi (dedicating action to the Lord) and prasāda-buddhi (receiving results as grace).

Step 3 — Always (satatam)
Satatam means 'constantly, always.' Karma-yoga is not a part-time practice. It is a full-time orientation. Whether in comfortable or uncomfortable situations, the attitude of non-attachment must be maintained. Swami Paramarthananda teaches that satatam indicates this is a way of life, not an occasional spiritual exercise.

Step 4 — Obligatory action (kāryaṁ karma)
Kāryam means 'what ought to be done.' It refers to obligatory duties (nitya-karma, naimittika-karma, and svadharma). Karma without kāryam could mean any random activity. But Kṛṣṇa specifies that the action must be contextually appropriate — one's duty. This prevents the misunderstanding that any action done casually qualifies as karma-yoga.

Step 5 — Perform well (samācara)
Samācara is an imperative from sam + ā + car, meaning 'perform thoroughly, engage properly.' The prefix sam- indicates completeness. The karma-yogī does not act carelessly or reluctantly. He acts with full engagement, skill, and sincerity — but without the binding pressure of result-dependence.

Step 6 — A person attains the Supreme (param āpnoti pūruṣaḥ)
Param means 'the Supreme' — mokṣa. Āpnoti means 'attains.' Pūruṣaḥ means 'a person.' The promise is clear: through unattached action, a person reaches the highest goal. But Śaṅkarācārya clarifies this is an indirect path: niṣkāma karma → cittaśuddhi → jñāna-yogyatā → jñānam → mokṣa. Action does not directly produce liberation; it purifies the mind, making it fit for Self-knowledge.

Step 7 — The complete teaching chain
This verse bridges chapters 2 and 3. In 2.47, Kṛṣṇa said 'your right is to action alone, never to the results.' In 3.9, he said 'perform action for yajña.' Now in 3.19, he gives the complete practical instruction: act without attachment, always, performing your duties, and through that purification, attain the Supreme. The entire karma-yoga sādhana is condensed in this one verse.
key_verses.more_stories:
1. Hanumān Serves Rāma Without Seeking Reward (Rāmāyaṇa)
Hanumān's service to Rāma is one of the purest examples of niṣkāma karma in all of Indian literature. When Rāma needed someone to cross the ocean and find Sītā, Hanumān leaped without hesitation. He did not ask, 'What will I receive in return?' He did not negotiate terms or conditions.
In Laṅkā, Hanumān faced Rāvaṇa's army, fire, and danger. He found Sītā and delivered Rāma's message of hope. He burned Laṅkā as a warning. He returned and laid the news at Rāma's feet.
At no point did Hanumān say, 'Now give me my reward.' His action was kāryaṁ karma — what needed to be done. His attitude was asaktaḥ — free from personal ambition. This is why Hanumān is considered the ideal karma-yogī.

2. Karṇa Gives His Armor Even When Warned (Mahābhārata)
Karṇa was born with divine armor (kavaca) and earrings (kuṇḍala) that made him virtually invulnerable. Before the Kurukṣetra war, Indra came disguised as a brāhmaṇa and asked for them. Kṛṣṇa and Sūrya had already warned Karṇa that this visitor was Indra in disguise, seeking to weaken him.
Karṇa knew the consequences. Giving away his armor meant losing his greatest protection in battle. Yet when Indra asked, Karṇa gave without hesitation. His commitment to dāna (generosity) was not transactional. He did not say, 'Let me calculate the cost-benefit.' He gave because giving was his dharma.
This act of selfless giving illustrates the spirit of 3.19. Karṇa performed what he believed was his duty (kāryam) without attachment to personal safety (asaktaḥ). Though his overall story is complex, this particular moment shows the power of acting from principle rather than calculation.

3. Nachiketas Refuses Yama's Temptations (Kaṭha Upaniṣad)
Young Nachiketas arrived at the door of Yama, the Lord of Death. Due to a technicality, Yama owed him three boons. For his third boon, Nachiketas asked about the truth of the Self: 'What happens after death? Does the Self exist or not?'
Yama tried to distract him. He offered long life, children, grandchildren, wealth, kingdom, celestial pleasures, beautiful attendants, and anything the world could provide. 'Ask for anything else,' Yama urged.
Nachiketas refused every temptation. He said that all these pleasures are impermanent and wear out the senses. He wanted only the knowledge that liberates. His refusal was not born from depression or world-hatred. It was born from viveka — discrimination between the eternal and the temporary.
This episode shows what 3.19 ultimately points to: the person who acts without attachment to finite results is the one who eventually reaches the Supreme (param). Nachiketas was not attached to worldly results; therefore, he was fit for the highest teaching.

4. Sudāmā Visits Kṛṣṇa Without Expectation (Bhāgavata Purāṇa)
Sudāmā was a poor brāhmaṇa, a childhood friend of Kṛṣṇa. His wife urged him to visit Kṛṣṇa in Dvārakā and seek help. Sudāmā agreed reluctantly and carried a small handful of beaten rice (powa) as an offering.
When Sudāmā arrived, Kṛṣṇa received him with extraordinary love. He washed Sudāmā's feet, seated him on his own bed, and treated him like the most honored guest. Sudāmā was overwhelmed. He could not bring himself to ask for material help. He simply enjoyed the reunion and left, having given his humble offering.
On the way home, Sudāmā thought, 'I went without asking for anything. I offered what I had without expecting return.' When he reached his village, he found his old hut transformed into a beautiful home, his family well-provided.
Sudāmā's visit illustrates acting without attachment. He did not calculate. He did not demand. He offered what he had and left the results to the Lord. The Lord provided beyond imagination. This is the promise of param āpnoti — when we act without selfish attachment, the Supreme takes care of what is needed.

5. Sage Dadhīci Sacrifices His Bones for Dharma (Purāṇic Tradition)
The gods were being defeated by the demon Vṛtrāsura, who could not be killed by any ordinary weapon. Brahma revealed that only a weapon made from the bones of sage Dadhīci could slay this demon. The gods approached Dadhīci with their request.
Dadhīci did not hesitate. He understood that protecting dharma required this sacrifice. He gave up his body so that his bones could be fashioned into the vajra (thunderbolt) wielded by Indra. Vṛtrāsura was slain, and the cosmic order was restored.
Dadhīci's sacrifice is among the most selfless acts in Purāṇic literature. He did not calculate what he would gain. He did not negotiate for heavenly rewards. He gave everything because dharma required it. This is kāryaṁ karma samācara — performing obligatory action without attachment — taken to its ultimate expression.

rich_grammar.padacchedah: तस्मात् असक्तः सततम् कार्यम् कर्म समाचर । असक्तः हि आचरन् कर्म परम् आप्नोति पूरुषः ॥
rich_grammar.pratipadarthah: तस्मात् | (tasmāt) = therefore असक्तः | (asaktaḥ) = without attachment सततम् | (satatam) = always, constantly कार्यम् | (kāryam) = obligatory, what ought to be done कर्म | (karma) = action समाचर | (samācara) = perform well, engage in असक्तः | (asaktaḥ) = without attachment हि | (hi) = indeed, because आचरन् | (ācaran) = performing, engaging in कर्म | (karma) = action परम् | (param) = the Supreme, mokṣa आप्नोति | (āpnoti) = attains पूरुषः | (pūruṣaḥ) = a person
rich_grammar.anvayah: तस्मात् असक्तः सततम् कार्यं कर्म समाचर । हि असक्तः कर्म आचरन् पूरुषः परम् आप्नोति ।
rich_grammar.sandhi: तस्मात् + असक्तः = तस्मादसक्तः | असक्तः + हि = असक्तो हि | हि + आचरन् = ह्याचरन् | आचरन् + कर्म = आचरन्कर्म | परम् + आप्नोति = परमाप्नोति
rich_grammar.samasa: कार्यम् = कर्तुम् योग्यम् / कर्तव्यम् — कृत्य-प्रत्ययान्त-रूपम्; that which ought to be done. | असक्तः = न सक्तः — नञ्-तत्पुरुषः; one who is not attached.
rich_grammar.other: समाचर is लोट्-लकारः (imperative), मध्यमपुरुष एकवचनम्, from सम् + आ + चर्, meaning 'perform thoroughly.' | आचरन् is शतृ-प्रत्ययान्त-कृदन्तम् (present participle) from आ + चर्, meaning 'while performing.' | आप्नोति is लट्-लकारः, प्रथमपुरुष एकवचनम्, from आप् (5th gaṇa), meaning 'attains.' | तस्मात् is पञ्चमी एकवचनम् of तद्, used as a conjunctive: 'therefore.' | सततम् is an अव्ययम् meaning 'always, constantly.'

key_verses.final_takeaway: Perform your duties thoroughly and without attachment — this is the essence of karma-yoga. Through unattached action, the mind is purified, and through that purification, Self-knowledge and liberation become accessible. The Supreme is not attained by avoiding action but by transforming its inner quality.
