key_verses.title: Perfection Through Action
key_verses.sanskrit:
कर्मणैव हि संसिद्धिम् आस्थिता जनकादयः ।
लोकसङ्ग्रहमेवापि सम्पश्यन्कर्तुमर्हसि ॥
key_verses.transliteration:
karmaṇaiva hi saṁsiddhim āsthitā janakādayaḥ ।
lokasaṅgrahamevāpi sampaśyankartumarhasi ॥
key_verses.one_line_meaning: Indeed, Janaka and others attained perfection through action alone; even considering the welfare of the world, you ought to act.
key_verses.concise_journey: After instructing Arjuna to act without attachment (3.19), Bhagavān Kṛṣṇa now offers historical proof that karma-yoga works. He cites King Janaka and others who attained saṁsiddhi — perfection in the form of Self-knowledge — through the path of action. Kṛṣṇa then introduces a second powerful reason to act: lokasaṅgraha, the welfare and cohesion of society. Even if personal liberation were not the goal, the responsibility of leadership demands action.
key_verses.meaning_detail:
Bhagavān Kṛṣṇa supports his instruction to act without attachment (3.19) with two arguments — one historical and one social.

The historical argument comes first: karmaṇā eva hi saṁsiddhim āsthitāḥ janakādayaḥ — Janaka and others attained perfection through action alone. Janaka was a king who ruled Mithilā with full engagement in royal duties — governance, justice, protection, welfare — yet he was simultaneously established in Self-knowledge. He did not renounce the world to discover the Self. He discovered the Self while living fully in the world. The word āsthitāḥ means 'were established in' or 'attained and remained in.' Saṁsiddhi means complete perfection — which, in the Advaita tradition, refers to cittaśuddhi leading to jñāna-prāpti. The suffix ādayaḥ ('and others') indicates Janaka was not unique. Other royal sages like Aśvapati (Chāndogya Upaniṣad) also attained wisdom through the path of karma-yoga.

Śaṅkarācārya discusses two possible meanings of saṁsiddhi here: (1) Self-knowledge (jñāna-prāpti), meaning Janaka attained knowledge while performing action, or (2) cittaśuddhi (mental purification), meaning Janaka purified his mind through karma-yoga and then attained knowledge through jñāna-yoga. Either way, the point is clear: action does not obstruct the path. In fact, for the vast majority of seekers, action is the necessary gateway.

The second argument is even more far-reaching: lokasaṅgraham eva api sampaśyan kartum arhasi — even merely considering the welfare of the world, you ought to act. Lokasaṅgraha means holding the world together — maintaining social order, dharma, moral example, and collective welfare. Even if Arjuna had already attained personal liberation, he should still act for the sake of others. A leader who withdraws from action sends a dangerous message: that duty can be avoided, that responsibility can be abandoned when it becomes uncomfortable.

Swami Paramarthananda explains that this verse gives two independent reasons for karma-yoga: (1) it leads to personal perfection, and (2) it protects society. Even a jñānī acts for lokasaṅgraha. Therefore, whether one is a seeker or a knower, action with the right attitude is always appropriate.
key_verses.story:
King Janaka's Fire of Wisdom While Ruling (Bṛhadāraṇyaka Upaniṣad / Mahābhārata Tradition)
King Janaka of Mithilā was one of the most remarkable figures in the Vedāntic tradition. He was not a forest-dwelling ascetic. He was a king with a full court, a kingdom to protect, disputes to settle, taxes to manage, and enemies to face. His daily life was filled with worldly responsibility.
Yet Janaka's inner life was extraordinary. The Bṛhadāraṇyaka Upaniṣad records his dialogues with the great sage Yājñavalkya. In one famous assembly, Janaka invited the greatest brāhmaṇas and offered a thousand cows, each with gold coins tied to their horns. He challenged them: 'Let the most learned among you take these cows.' Yājñavalkya boldly accepted the challenge and proceeded to teach profound Vedānta in response to questions from other sages.
Janaka listened with the sharpness of a seeker and the humility of a student. He was not there for entertainment. He wanted truth. Through Yājñavalkya's teaching, Janaka's understanding deepened until he recognized the Self as his own essential nature.
What makes Janaka's story so significant for 3.20 is this: he did not abandon his kingdom after receiving knowledge. He continued to rule, to act, to serve. His action became free action — action without dependence on results. The kingdom was his field of karma-yoga, not his prison.
Later tradition remembers Janaka saying, 'Even if Mithilā burns, nothing of mine is burnt.' This was not indifference. It was the freedom of a mind established in the Self. Action continued; attachment did not.
Bhagavān Kṛṣṇa cites Janaka as proof that action and wisdom are not opposed. The path of duty, when walked with the right attitude, leads to the same destination as the path of renunciation. Janaka attained saṁsiddhi — perfection — through karma-yoga.
key_verses.real_life_example:
In modern society, there is sometimes a false division between 'spiritual life' and 'worldly life.' Some people believe that to be truly spiritual, one must withdraw from responsibilities — quit one's job, live in an āśrama, avoid material engagement. Others believe that worldly success is the only goal and dismiss inner work entirely.

The teaching of 3.20 dissolves this false division. Janaka was fully engaged in worldly duties and fully established in Self-knowledge. He did not choose one at the expense of the other.

Consider a doctor who treats patients with genuine compassion. She does not treat patients to become famous or wealthy. She treats them because healing is her dharma. At the same time, she studies Vedānta, reflects on the nature of the Self, and gradually discovers inner freedom. Her medical practice becomes her karma-yoga — a field where she serves others while purifying her own mind. She attains saṁsiddhi not by abandoning medicine but through medicine performed with the right attitude.

The second teaching of the verse — lokasaṅgraha — is equally relevant. Leaders, teachers, parents, and public figures shape society through their actions. A parent who lives with integrity teaches children integrity without giving a single lecture. A boss who treats employees with fairness inspires fairness in the entire organization. When responsible people act, the fabric of society holds together. When they withdraw, confusion and disorder follow.
key_verses.reflection:
Do I believe that spiritual growth requires abandoning my responsibilities, or can I see my daily work as a field for inner purification?
Whose example has shaped my understanding of duty and dharma — for better or worse?
Am I acting not just for my own benefit but also for the welfare and cohesion of those around me?

kids_content.explanation_script:
Kṛṣṇa tells Arjuna about a great king named Janaka. King Janaka had a big kingdom to take care of. He had to make rules, protect people, solve problems, and do many things every day. He was very busy.
But King Janaka was also very wise. He learned about the Self — the deepest part of who we really are. And here is the amazing thing: he did not stop being a king to become wise. He became wise while being a king.
Kṛṣṇa says: 'See? Janaka did his work and still found wisdom. So you should also do your work. And there is another reason too — when good people work, everyone benefits. The whole world is helped.'
So Kṛṣṇa gives two reasons to act: it helps you grow inside, and it helps everyone around you.
kids_content.story:
The Older Sister Who Changed the Classroom
Ananya was the oldest student in her class. She was not the class monitor, and no teacher had asked her to lead. But she was kind, honest, and hardworking.
One day, a new rule was announced: every student had to bring a healthy snack instead of junk food. Many children grumbled. 'Why can't we eat chips?' they complained.
Ananya did not argue. She simply brought a box of fruit and nuts the next day. She ate her snack quietly and shared some with her friends. She did not lecture anyone.
Slowly, other children noticed. If Ananya could eat fruit happily, maybe it was not so bad. Within a week, half the class was bringing healthier snacks. By the end of the month, even the biggest chip-lovers were trying new things.
Ananya did not force anyone. She simply did what was right. Her example changed the classroom.
Kṛṣṇa teaches that when good people act, others naturally follow. This is called lokasaṅgraha — holding the world together through good example. You do not need a title or a prize. You just need to do the right thing.
kids_content.reflection:
Can I think of someone whose good example has helped me become better?
What is one good habit I can start that might help others around me too?

key_verses.detailed_meaning:
Step 1 — Through action alone (karmaṇā eva)
Karmaṇā is the instrumental case of karma, meaning 'through action.' Eva emphasizes 'alone' or 'indeed.' The combination means that action — specifically karma-yoga — was the means. This counters the idea that one must abandon action to attain perfection.

Step 2 — Indeed attained perfection (hi saṁsiddhim āsthitāḥ)
Hi means 'indeed' and adds certainty. Saṁsiddhim means 'complete perfection' — which, per Śaṅkarācārya, is either jñāna-prāpti (attainment of Self-knowledge) or jñāna-yogyatā through cittaśuddhi (fitness for knowledge through mental purification). Āsthitāḥ means 'established in, attained and remained in.' These were not people who briefly touched wisdom and lost it.

Step 3 — Janaka and others (janakādayaḥ)
Janaka is the famous king of Mithilā, celebrated in the Bṛhadāraṇyaka Upaniṣad as a disciple of Yājñavalkya and a jñānī who continued to rule. Ādayaḥ means 'and others' — including royal sages like Aśvapati (Chāndogya Upaniṣad 5.11) and other karma-yogīs from the tradition. The historical precedent strengthens the argument: this path has worked before.

Step 4 — Even considering the welfare of the world (lokasaṅgraham eva api sampaśyan)
Lokasaṅgraham is a compound: loka (world/society) + saṅgraha (holding together, welfare, cohesion). Eva api means 'even also' — introducing a second, independent reason. Sampaśyan means 'considering, seeing, keeping in view.' Even apart from personal liberation, one should act for the sake of social welfare and moral order.

Step 5 — You ought to act (kartum arhasi)
Kartum is the infinitive of kṛ, meaning 'to do, to act.' Arhasi is second person singular of arh, meaning 'you ought to, you deserve to, you should.' This is a respectful but firm instruction: Arjuna, you should act — both for your own spiritual growth and for the welfare of the world.

Step 6 — Two independent arguments for action
The verse provides two self-sufficient reasons: (1) personal saṁsiddhi through karma-yoga, proven by the example of Janaka, and (2) lokasaṅgraha — the responsibility of maintaining dharma and social cohesion. Either reason alone is sufficient to justify action. Together, they make the case overwhelming. No sincere seeker and no responsible leader can justify withdrawal from duty.
key_verses.more_stories:
1. King Aśvapati Teaches the Pañcāgni-Vidyā (Chāndogya Upaniṣad)
King Aśvapati of Kekaya was another royal sage who combined worldly duties with deep spiritual knowledge. Six brāhmaṇas, each learned in one aspect of Brahman, came to him seeking complete knowledge. Remarkably, they came not to another brāhmaṇa but to a king — a man immersed in governance and worldly duties.
Aśvapati tested each sage and found their knowledge partial. One knew Brahman as heaven, another as the sun, another as space. Each had grasped a fragment. Aśvapati then taught them the complete Vaiśvānara-Vidyā — the knowledge of the cosmic Self as the whole.
This king, like Janaka, had attained saṁsiddhi while ruling. His kingdom was not an obstacle to knowledge; it was the context within which knowledge flourished. The Upaniṣad presents him as proof that karma-yoga and jñāna-yoga can coexist beautifully.

2. Arjuna's Own Dharma on the Battlefield (Mahābhārata)
Arjuna himself is the best illustration of why this verse matters. He stands on the battlefield wanting to withdraw. He has seen his relatives, teachers, and friends on the opposing side. His emotional response is to drop his weapons and refuse to fight.
But Kṛṣṇa systematically dismantles every argument for withdrawal. In chapter 2, he addressed the philosophical truth: the Self cannot be killed. In chapter 3, he addresses the practical truth: action is unavoidable and necessary. Now he provides historical proof: Janaka attained perfection through action. If Janaka could find wisdom while managing a kingdom, surely Arjuna can fight a righteous war while maintaining inner equanimity.
The parallel is direct: Arjuna is a warrior-prince like Janaka was a king. His field of karma-yoga is the battlefield. Running away would not bring him peace; it would bring him guilt, social disorder, and the collapse of dharma.

3. Rāma Rules Ayodhyā After the War (Rāmāyaṇa)
After defeating Rāvaṇa and rescuing Sītā, Rāma returned to Ayodhyā and was crowned king. He did not retire to a forest to meditate. He ruled for many years in what tradition calls Rāma-rājya — the ideal kingdom where dharma flourished.
Rāma's post-war life is a powerful example of lokasaṅgraha. He had already achieved what he set out to do. He had fulfilled his promise to the sages, rescued Sītā, and destroyed adharma. He could have withdrawn. But he chose to rule because the people needed him.
His rule was characterized by fairness, compassion, and attention to duty. Every citizen's welfare was his concern. Rāma-rājya became the benchmark for just governance in Indian civilization. This is lokasaṅgraha in action — a great person continuing to act for the welfare of the world.

4. Yudhiṣṭhira Accepts Kingship After the War (Mahābhārata)
After the devastating war at Kurukṣetra, Yudhiṣṭhira was shattered. He had won the kingdom but lost countless loved ones. He wanted to renounce everything and retire to the forest. The weight of the destruction haunted him.
But Kṛṣṇa, Bhīṣma, Vyāsa, and other elders persuaded him to accept the throne. Their argument was precisely the argument of 3.20: the world needs responsible leaders. If Yudhiṣṭhira — the most dharmic of kings — refuses to rule, who will hold society together? His withdrawal would not serve peace; it would create a vacuum filled by adharma.
Yudhiṣṭhira reluctantly accepted and ruled for many years. His kingship, born not from ambition but from duty, became a form of karma-yoga. He acted not for personal gain but for lokasaṅgraha.

5. Mother Yaśodā Serves Without Seeking Recognition (Bhāgavata Purāṇa)
In the Bhāgavata Purāṇa, Yaśodā is the foster mother of Kṛṣṇa. She raised the Supreme Lord as her own child — feeding him, cleaning him, scolding him when he stole butter, and protecting him from danger. She did not know she was raising the Lord of the universe. She simply did her duty as a mother.
Yaśodā's service had no calculation. She did not think, 'If I serve well, I will attain mokṣa.' She loved and served because that was what a mother does. Her action was pure, selfless, and total.
In the tradition, Yaśodā's devotion is considered among the highest. She attained spiritual perfection not through elaborate rituals or forest renunciation but through the simple, wholehearted performance of her motherly duties. Her story shows that saṁsiddhi can come through the humblest of actions when performed with love and without attachment to personal gain.

rich_grammar.padacchedah: कर्मणा एव हि संसिद्धिम् आस्थिताः जनकादयः । लोकसंग्रहम् एव अपि सम्पश्यन् कर्तुम् अर्हसि ॥
rich_grammar.pratipadarthah: कर्मणा | (karmaṇā) = through action, by action एव | (eva) = alone, indeed हि | (hi) = indeed, certainly संसिद्धिम् | (saṁsiddhim) = perfection, complete attainment आस्थिताः | (āsthitāḥ) = established in, attained जनकादयः | (janakādayaḥ) = Janaka and others लोकसंग्रहम् | (lokasaṁgraham) = welfare of the world, social cohesion एव | (eva) = alone, indeed अपि | (api) = also, even सम्पश्यन् | (sampaśyan) = considering, keeping in view कर्तुम् | (kartum) = to act, to do अर्हसि | (arhasi) = you ought to, you should
rich_grammar.anvayah: जनकादयः कर्मणा एव हि संसिद्धिम् आस्थिताः । लोकसंग्रहम् एव अपि सम्पश्यन् कर्तुम् अर्हसि ।
rich_grammar.sandhi: कर्मणा + एव = कर्मणैव | संसिद्धिम् + आस्थिताः = संसिद्धिमास्थिताः | जनक + आदयः = जनकादयः | लोकसंग्रहम् + एव = लोकसंग्रहमेव | एव + अपि = एवापि | सम्पश्यन् + कर्तुम् = सम्पश्यन्कर्तुम् | कर्तुम् + अर्हसि = कर्तुमर्हसि
rich_grammar.samasa: संसिद्धिम् = सम्यक् सिद्धिम् — उपसर्ग-युक्त-नाम; complete perfection/attainment. | जनकादयः = जनकः आदिः येषाम् ते — बहुव्रीहि; those of whom Janaka is the first, i.e. Janaka and others. | लोकसंग्रहम् = लोकस्य संग्रहम् — षष्ठी-तत्पुरुषः; the holding together/welfare of the world.
rich_grammar.other: कर्मणा is तृतीया एकवचनम् of कर्मन्, indicating the means: 'through action.' | आस्थिताः is क्तप्रत्ययान्त-कृदन्तम् from आ + स्था, meaning 'established in' or 'resorted to.' Plural form indicates multiple persons (Janaka and others). | सम्पश्यन् is शतृ-प्रत्ययान्त-कृदन्तम् from सम् + पश् (दृश्), meaning 'seeing, considering.' Used as a participial adjective modifying the implied 'you' (त्वम्). | कर्तुम् is a तुमुन्-प्रत्ययान्त-कृदन्तम् (infinitive) from कृ, meaning 'to do.' | अर्हसि is लट्-लकारः, मध्यमपुरुष एकवचनम्, परस्मैपदम्, from अर्ह्, meaning 'you deserve to, you ought to.'

key_verses.final_takeaway: Janaka and other great souls attained perfection through karma-yoga, not by renouncing action. Additionally, responsible action holds society together. Whether for personal growth or collective welfare, the wise person engages fully in duty without attachment.

key_verses.title: The Standard Set by the Great
key_verses.sanskrit:
यद्यदाचरति श्रेष्ठः(स्) तत्तदेवेतरो जनः ।
स यत्प्रमाणं(ङ्) कुरुते लोकस्तदनुवर्तते ॥
key_verses.transliteration:
yadyadācarati śreṣṭhaḥ(s) tattadevetaro janaḥ ।
sa yatpramāṇaṁ(ṅ) kurute lokastadanuvartate ॥
key_verses.one_line_meaning: Whatever a great person does, common people follow that. Whatever standard he sets, the world follows.
key_verses.concise_journey: Bhagavān Kṛṣṇa deepens the lokasaṅgraha argument from 3.20. Having told Arjuna to act for the welfare of the world, he now explains the mechanism: great people set the standard, and ordinary people follow. A leader's conduct — whether noble or negligent — ripples outward through society. This verse explains why responsible people cannot afford to withdraw from action even when they personally have nothing to gain.
key_verses.meaning_detail:
This verse reveals a profound truth about human society: people follow examples, not merely instructions. Bhagavān Kṛṣṇa states a universal principle — yad yad ācarati śreṣṭhaḥ tat tad eva itaraḥ janaḥ — whatever a great person does, common people do exactly the same.

The word śreṣṭhaḥ means 'the best, the most distinguished, the one who is looked up to.' This can refer to a leader, elder, teacher, parent, or anyone whose conduct carries influence. Itaraḥ janaḥ means 'the other people, the ordinary person.' The mechanism is imitation: people learn not from sermons but from examples. If a respected person is honest, honesty spreads. If a respected person is corrupt, corruption becomes normalized.

The second line deepens this: sa yat pramāṇaṁ kurute lokas tad anuvartate — whatever standard (pramāṇam) he establishes, the world (lokaḥ) follows (anuvartate). Pramāṇam here means 'measure, standard, authority.' It is not just about visible action but about the implicit norm that the action establishes. When a teacher values understanding over marks, students begin to value understanding. When a parent prioritizes kindness over winning, children grow up kind.

Śaṅkarācārya comments that this verse is addressed specifically to Arjuna because Arjuna is a śreṣṭha — a prince, a warrior of great fame, and a person whose actions will be observed by millions. If Arjuna drops his weapons and walks away, the message to the world would be devastating: when duty is difficult, you may abandon it. If Arjuna fights with dharmic integrity, the message is equally powerful: face your duty, even when it is painful.

Swami Paramarthananda extends this teaching: every person is a śreṣṭha in some circle. A parent is a śreṣṭha for children. A teacher is a śreṣṭha for students. A senior colleague is a śreṣṭha for juniors. Even a friend can be a śreṣṭha among peers. The responsibility of setting the right standard is not limited to kings and saints. It applies to everyone in their sphere of influence.

The verse also carries a warning. If great people act selfishly, irresponsibly, or lazily, the same behavior cascades through society. The decline of dharma does not begin with ordinary people. It begins when leaders compromise, when those who should know better stop acting according to their knowledge.
key_verses.story:
Bhīṣma's Vow Shapes the Kuru Dynasty (Mahābhārata)
Bhīṣma, originally named Devavrata, was the son of King Śāntanu and the river goddess Gaṅgā. He was the rightful heir to the Kuru throne, trained in warfare by Paraśurāma himself, and endowed with every quality of a great king.
But when his father wished to marry Satyavatī, her father demanded that only her sons should inherit the throne. Śāntanu could not bring himself to ask his son to give up his birthright. Devavrata discovered the situation and made a breathtaking vow: he renounced the throne and took a lifelong vow of celibacy (brahmacarya), ensuring no descendants of his could ever claim the kingdom.
This sacrifice earned him the name Bhīṣma — the one who took a terrible vow. His action sent a powerful message through the entire dynasty. It said: duty to one's father, loyalty to dharma, and selflessness can override personal ambition.
For generations, Bhīṣma's example became the standard of the Kuru house. When people needed to remember what sacrifice looked like, they pointed to Bhīṣma. His vow, his discipline, his lifelong celibacy, his unwavering presence in the court — all set a standard.
But the story also reveals the danger of the principle in 3.21. When Bhīṣma remained silent during Draupadī's humiliation, that silence also became a kind of standard. If the greatest elder in the court says nothing when injustice occurs, what message does that send to younger members? The Mahābhārata shows both sides: the power of a noble standard and the cost of a compromised one.
This is exactly what 3.21 teaches. The great person's conduct becomes the measure. Society does not analyze arguments — it follows examples.
key_verses.real_life_example:
A company's CEO announces a wellness initiative: all employees should maintain work-life balance, leave on time, and take regular breaks. Posters go up. Emails go out. The policy is clear.

But the CEO herself stays in the office until midnight every day. She sends emails at 2 AM. She skips vacations and expects instant responses on weekends. What happens? The policy is ignored. Everyone follows the CEO's behavior, not her words. Within months, the culture of overwork is stronger than ever.

Now consider a different CEO who genuinely leaves on time, takes her vacations, and respects boundaries. She does not lecture about balance. She simply lives it. Gradually, the organization shifts. Managers feel safe taking breaks. Junior employees stop pretending to be busy after hours. The standard is set not by policy but by example.

This is the principle of 3.21 operating in modern life. Whatever standard a leader sets through behavior, the group follows. Parents who shout teach children to shout. Teachers who read teach students to read. Leaders who cheat normalize cheating. Leaders who serve normalize service.

The verse invites us to examine: in my circle — my home, my workplace, my community — what standard am I setting through my daily conduct? Not through my words, but through my actions?
key_verses.reflection:
In my home, workplace, or community, what example am I setting through my daily conduct?
Have I seen how one person's behavior — good or bad — influenced an entire group?
Am I acting according to what I believe is right, knowing that others may silently follow my example?

kids_content.explanation_script:
Kṛṣṇa teaches a very important idea here. He says that when a great person does something, other people copy it. If a leader does good things, people around that leader start doing good things too. If a leader does bad things, people start doing bad things.
Think about your family. If your older brother or sister reads books at home, you might feel like reading too. If your elder sibling watches TV all day, you might also want to watch TV all day. That is how examples work — they spread.
Kṛṣṇa wants Arjuna to understand that as a prince and a hero, many people are watching him. If he runs away from his duty, others will think it is okay to run away too. If he stands and does the right thing, others will be inspired to do the right thing.
kids_content.story:
The Captain Who Changed the Team
Vikram was the captain of his school cricket team. He was a good player, but what made him special was his attitude.
Before practice, Vikram always reached early. He stretched, warmed up, and helped set up the stumps. He never complained about the heat or the hard ground. When he got out cheaply, he did not throw his bat. He walked back, thought about what went wrong, and practiced that shot later.
The other boys noticed. At first, some used to arrive late and grumble about practice. But seeing their captain already sweating on the field, they felt embarrassed. One by one, they started arriving on time. When Vikram handled failure calmly, the others stopped making excuses when they got out.
By the end of the season, the whole team had changed. They were more disciplined, more patient, and more hardworking. And they had not changed because Vikram gave them a speech. They changed because they watched him.
The new coach was impressed. He told the school principal, 'I did not coach this discipline. The captain did, just by being himself.'
Kṛṣṇa teaches this same truth: whatever a respected person does, others follow. You do not need to give big lectures. Just do the right thing, and the people around you will slowly change.
kids_content.reflection:
Can I think of someone in my life whose good habits I have started copying?
What is one good thing I can do regularly that might inspire my friends or siblings?

key_verses.detailed_meaning:
Step 1 — Whatever a great person does (yad yad ācarati śreṣṭhaḥ)
Yad yad is a correlative pronoun meaning 'whatever.' Ācarati means 'does, practices, conducts.' Śreṣṭhaḥ means 'the best, the most distinguished, the leader.' The combination says: whatever specific action a great person performs — whether it is a daily habit, a moral choice, or a public decision — becomes a reference point for others.

Step 2 — Common people follow that (tat tad eva itaraḥ janaḥ)
Tat tad means 'that very thing.' Eva emphasizes exactness — not approximately, but precisely that. Itaraḥ janaḥ means 'the other person, the common people.' The mechanism is direct imitation. People do not analyze or philosophize about the leader's action; they simply replicate it.

Step 3 — Whatever standard he sets (sa yat pramāṇaṁ kurute)
Sa means 'he,' referring to the śreṣṭha. Yat means 'whatever.' Pramāṇam means 'standard, measure, authority, norm.' Kurute means 'makes, establishes.' The leader does not merely perform isolated actions; he implicitly establishes a norm, a measure of what is acceptable and what is not.

Step 4 — The world follows (lokas tad anuvartate)
Lokaḥ means 'the world, the people.' Tad means 'that.' Anuvartate means 'follows, conforms to, goes along with.' The world follows the standard set by the leader. Anu- means 'after, along with' — indicating that people fall in line with the established norm.

Step 5 — Why this matters for Arjuna
Arjuna is a śreṣṭha — a prince of the Kuru dynasty, a renowned warrior, and a person whose actions will be watched by millions. If he abandons his duty, the implicit message is: when life becomes difficult, you may run away. Kṛṣṇa is telling Arjuna that his personal choice has far-reaching social consequences.

Step 6 — Universal application
Śaṅkarācārya and Swami Paramarthananda both emphasize that this principle applies universally. Every parent is a śreṣṭha for their children. Every teacher is a śreṣṭha for their students. Every elder is a śreṣṭha in their circle. The responsibility of right conduct extends to all who are in positions of influence, however small that circle may be.
key_verses.more_stories:
1. Droṇa's Lesson on Truth-Telling (Mahābhārata)
Droṇa, the great teacher of the Kauravas and Pāṇḍavas, once asked all his students to memorize a verse about truthfulness. The next day, he called each student to recite. One by one, they stood and recited perfectly — all except Yudhiṣṭhira.
When Droṇa asked him, Yudhiṣṭhira said, 'I have memorized the verse, but I have not yet lived it. I cannot say I have learned it until I have practiced it fully.' Droṇa was deeply impressed.
Yudhiṣṭhira did not merely recite a standard; he became the standard. His commitment to truth was so absolute that he was called Dharmarāja. Throughout his life, people looked to his conduct as the measure of righteousness. When Yudhiṣṭhira spoke, even enemies believed him, because his life was his teaching.
This illustrates 3.21: the standard a great person sets through conduct carries more weight than a thousand lectures.

2. Sage Vasiṣṭha Forgives Viśvāmitra (Purāṇic Tradition)
Vasiṣṭha and Viśvāmitra had a long and bitter conflict. Viśvāmitra, originally a kṣatriya king, wanted Vasiṣṭha's divine cow Nandinī. When Vasiṣṭha refused, Viśvāmitra waged war against him. The conflict escalated over many lifetimes, with Viśvāmitra destroying Vasiṣṭha's sons and Vasiṣṭha suffering tremendous personal loss.
Yet when Viśvāmitra finally attained the status of brahmarṣi through severe tapas and genuine transformation, Vasiṣṭha was the first to acknowledge him. He did not hold a grudge. He did not remind Viśvāmitra of past offenses. He accepted the transformation with grace.
Vasiṣṭha's forgiveness set a standard for the entire ṛṣi community. If the sage who suffered the most could forgive, others had no excuse to hold petty grudges. The pramāṇam he established through his conduct shaped the culture of the āśramas for generations.

3. Lord Rāma Walks Into Exile Willingly (Rāmāyaṇa)
When Kaikeyī demanded that Rāma be exiled for fourteen years, Rāma did not protest. He did not argue, bargain, or show resentment. He accepted the exile with calm dignity, honoring his father's word.
This single act set a standard for the entire kingdom. Lakṣmaṇa followed Rāma into the forest. Sītā followed without hesitation. Citizens of Ayodhyā were inspired to such devotion that many wanted to accompany him. Even Bharata, who had been given the throne, refused to rule and instead placed Rāma's sandals on the throne as a symbol of the true king.
Rāma's conduct did not come with a lecture. He simply acted according to dharma. But that single action became the pramāṇam — the standard — that everyone around him followed. This is the power described in 3.21.

4. Śrī Kṛṣṇa Washes the Feet of Guests at the Rājasūya (Mahābhārata)
At Yudhiṣṭhira's Rājasūya yajña, the question arose: who should be honored first? Bhīṣma declared Kṛṣṇa the most worthy. But Kṛṣṇa, the Lord of the universe, did not sit on a high throne and accept worship.
Instead, he took upon himself the humble task of washing the feet of the brāhmaṇa guests and distributing food. The Supreme Lord served as an attendant. He set a standard of humility that silenced every ego in that assembly.
When Kṛṣṇa — the greatest — served with humility, how could any lesser person justify arrogance? His conduct became the pramāṇam for every king, warrior, and priest present. This is 3.21 demonstrated by Kṛṣṇa himself.

5. Mahatma Gandhi's Salt March (Modern History)
In 1930, Gandhi decided to protest the British salt tax by walking to the sea and making salt from seawater. It was a simple, humble act. He did not call for violence. He did not organize an armed uprising. He walked.
But Gandhi was a śreṣṭha — a leader whom millions watched. His walking became their walking. Within weeks, thousands joined the march. Within months, millions across India were making their own salt in defiance of the British law. The act was small; the example was enormous.
This modern event perfectly illustrates the Gītā's teaching. Whatever standard a great person sets, the world follows. Gandhi's conduct — nonviolent, principled, disciplined — became the standard for India's freedom movement.

rich_grammar.padacchedah: यत् यत् आचरति श्रेष्ठः तत् तत् एव इतरः जनः । सः यत् प्रमाणम् कुरुते लोकः तत् अनुवर्तते ॥
rich_grammar.pratipadarthah: यत् | (yat) = whatever यत् | (yat) = whatever आचरति | (ācarati) = does, practices, conducts श्रेष्ठः | (śreṣṭhaḥ) = the best, the great person, the leader तत् | (tat) = that तत् | (tat) = that एव | (eva) = indeed, exactly इतरः | (itaraḥ) = the other, the common जनः | (janaḥ) = person, people सः | (saḥ) = he यत् | (yat) = whatever प्रमाणम् | (pramāṇam) = standard, measure, norm कुरुते | (kurute) = makes, establishes लोकः | (lokaḥ) = the world, people तत् | (tat) = that अनुवर्तते | (anuvartate) = follows, conforms to
rich_grammar.anvayah: श्रेष्ठः यत् यत् आचरति, इतरः जनः तत् तत् एव (आचरति) । सः यत् प्रमाणं कुरुते, लोकः तत् अनुवर्तते ।
rich_grammar.sandhi: यत् + यत् = यद्यत् | यत् + आचरति = यदाचरति | श्रेष्ठः + तत् = श्रेष्ठस्तत् | तत् + तत् = तत्तत् | तत् + एव = तदेव | एव + इतरः = एवेतरः | सः + यत् = स यत् | यत् + प्रमाणम् = यत्प्रमाणम् | लोकः + तत् = लोकस्तत् | तत् + अनुवर्तते = तदनुवर्तते
rich_grammar.samasa: श्रेष्ठः = अतिशयेन श्रीमान् / प्रशस्ततमः — superlative form of श्रेय; the best, the most distinguished. | प्रमाणम् = प्र + मा + ल्युट् — that by which something is measured; standard, authority. | अनुवर्तते = अनु + वृत् — follows after, conforms to.
rich_grammar.other: आचरति is लट्-लकारः, प्रथमपुरुष एकवचनम्, परस्मैपदम्, from आ + चर्, meaning 'does, practices, conducts.' | कुरुते is लट्-लकारः, प्रथमपुरुष एकवचनम्, आत्मनेपदम्, from कृ, meaning 'makes, does.' | अनुवर्तते is लट्-लकारः, प्रथमपुरुष एकवचनम्, आत्मनेपदम्, from अनु + वृत्, meaning 'follows after, conforms to.' | यत् यत् ... तत् तत् is a correlative construction meaning 'whatever ... that very thing.' The repetition (yad yad, tat tat) emphasizes universality — without exception. | The verse has two parallel clauses: the first states the principle of imitation, the second states the principle of standard-setting.

key_verses.final_takeaway: Great people shape society not through speeches but through conduct. Whatever standard a leader sets — through integrity, discipline, compassion, or negligence — the world follows. This places a sacred responsibility on everyone who holds influence, however small their circle may be.

key_verses.title: The Lord Who Needs Nothing
key_verses.sanskrit:
न मे पार्थास्ति कर्तव्यं(न्) त्रिषु लोकेषु किञ्चन ।
नानवाप्तमवाप्तव्यं(वँ) वर्त एव च कर्मणि ॥
key_verses.transliteration:
na me pārthāsti kartavyaṁ(n) triṣu lokeṣu kiñcana ।
nānavāptamavāptavyaṁ(v̐) varte eva ca karmaṇi ॥
key_verses.one_line_meaning: O Pārtha, there is nothing in the three worlds that I need to do, nor is there anything unattained that I need to attain — yet I continue to engage in action.
key_verses.concise_journey: Having explained the principle that great people set the standard for society (3.21), Bhagavān Kṛṣṇa now offers the supreme example — himself. He is Īśvara, the Lord of all three worlds. He has nothing to achieve, nothing to gain, nothing left unattained. Yet he continues to act. This verse demolishes every excuse for laziness disguised as spiritual detachment. If the Lord himself acts without personal need, how can any seeker justify withdrawal?
key_verses.meaning_detail:
This verse is one of the most striking in the Gītā. Bhagavān Kṛṣṇa, speaking as Īśvara, reveals his own relationship with action. He says: na me pārtha asti kartavyam triṣu lokeṣu kiñcana — O Arjuna, there is nothing in the three worlds that I must do. The word kartavyam means 'duty, obligatory action.' Triṣu lokeṣu refers to the three realms — earth, heaven, and the space between. Kiñcana means 'anything whatsoever.' The Lord has no unfulfilled obligation anywhere in creation.

The second part strengthens this: na anavāptam avāptavyam — there is nothing unattained that needs to be attained. Anavāptam means 'not yet obtained.' Avāptavyam means 'worthy of being obtained, needing to be obtained.' For ordinary beings, action is driven by desire for what is not yet possessed. But Kṛṣṇa, being pūrṇa (complete), has nothing lacking. There is no object, status, power, or state that he does not already possess.

Yet — and this is the extraordinary turn — varta eva ca karmaṇi: 'I continue to engage in action.' Varte means 'I am engaged in, I continue in.' Eva emphasizes that this is genuinely so. Ca ('and') connects this with the preceding negations. The contrast is total: complete fullness, zero need, yet continuous action.

Śaṅkarācārya explains that Kṛṣṇa's action is not karma in the ordinary sense. Ordinary karma is driven by rāga-dveṣa (attraction and aversion) and kartṛtva-abhimāna (the notion of doership). Kṛṣṇa's action is līlā — free play, arising not from need but from compassion and the natural maintenance of cosmic order. He sustains the sun, the seasons, the moral law, and the welfare of all beings — not because he would suffer without doing so, but because that is the nature of Īśvara.

Swami Paramarthananda draws the practical lesson: if the Lord acts despite having nothing to gain, then the jñānī also has reason to continue acting. And if the jñānī acts, then the seeker has even more reason to act. The verse removes every possible excuse. The argument unfolds: 'If even I, the Lord, continue to act without need, how can you, Arjuna, justify inaction?'

This verse also prepares the ground for 3.23, where Kṛṣṇa will explain the consequence of his hypothetical inaction — the collapse of world order.
key_verses.story:
Kṛṣṇa Serves as Arjuna's Charioteer (Mahābhārata)
Before the war of Kurukṣetra began, both Arjuna and Duryodhana approached Kṛṣṇa to seek his support. Kṛṣṇa offered them a choice: one could have his entire army, and the other could have Kṛṣṇa himself — but as a non-combatant. Duryodhana chose the army. Arjuna chose Kṛṣṇa.
Kṛṣṇa agreed to serve as Arjuna's charioteer. The Lord of the universe, the one from whom all creation emerges, who sustains all beings and into whom all beings dissolve — this Lord held the reins of horses and steered a chariot through dust and arrows.
He did not need to be there. He had nothing to gain from the war. No kingdom awaited him. No reward would enhance his fullness. He was already the possessor of all power, all knowledge, and all existence.
Yet he drove the chariot with complete attention. He guided Arjuna through strategic positions. He protected the horses. He navigated through showers of arrows. He performed the humble, physical work of a sārathi (charioteer) with the same presence he brought to sustaining the cosmos.
When Arjuna collapsed in grief and confusion before the first battle, Kṛṣṇa did not say, 'This is not my problem.' He taught the Gītā. He spent eighteen chapters guiding his friend from confusion to clarity. He did this not for personal benefit but because Arjuna needed it, because dharma needed it, because the world needed it.
This entire episode is the living commentary on 3.22. The Lord who has nothing to do, nothing to gain, and nothing unattained — yet he engages in action, serving as a charioteer, teaching as a guru, and protecting as a friend. His action is pure selflessness, free from the slightest trace of personal need.
key_verses.real_life_example:
Imagine a retired professor who has published extensively, won every significant award in her field, and secured her financial future. She has nothing left to achieve professionally. No committee appointment would add to her reputation. No new publication would change her standing.

Yet every morning, she walks to the community library and offers free tutoring to underprivileged students. She prepares carefully, explains patiently, and celebrates each student's small progress. When asked why she does this, she does not say, 'Because I need to.' She says, 'Because they need it, and I can help.'

This is a human approximation of Kṛṣṇa's teaching in 3.22. When action arises from fullness rather than need, it becomes pure service. There is no anxiety about results because there is nothing at stake for the doer. There is no resentment because no reward is expected. There is no pressure because the action is not driven by incompleteness.

For most of us, this state is aspirational. We act because we need something — money, recognition, love, security. The verse does not condemn these needs. It shows us the direction of growth. As we mature through karma-yoga, our actions become less about 'What will I get?' and more about 'What does the situation need?' That gradual shift from need-driven to service-driven action is the practical meaning of this verse.
key_verses.reflection:
Do I act mainly because I need something, or can I sometimes act purely because the situation requires it?
What would my work look like if I had nothing to prove and nothing to gain — would I still do it?
Can I see Kṛṣṇa's example as an invitation to slowly transform my own motivation from need to service?

kids_content.explanation_script:
Now Kṛṣṇa tells Arjuna something amazing. He says: 'Arjuna, I am the Lord of everything. There is nothing in the whole universe that I need. There is nothing I have not already got. But look — I still work! I still take care of the world. I still help, protect, and guide.'
Why would someone who has everything still work? Because Kṛṣṇa does not work for himself. He works for others. He works because people need his help, not because he needs anything from them.
Imagine your mother has already eaten her dinner. She is full. She does not need any more food. But if you are still hungry, she gets up and serves you. She does not do it because she is hungry. She does it because you need it. That is how Kṛṣṇa acts — from love, not from need.
kids_content.story:
The Gardener Who Had Everything
There was once an old gardener named Thatha who had a beautiful garden. It had every kind of flower, fruit tree, and herb. Birds sang in the branches. Butterflies visited every morning. Thatha's garden was famous in the whole village.
One day, a young boy asked him, 'Thatha, why do you still water the plants and pull weeds every day? Your garden is already perfect!'
Thatha smiled. 'My garden does not need me to become beautiful for my sake. But the plants need water to live. The flowers need space to bloom. The birds need seeds to eat. I do not garden for myself. I garden for them.'
The boy watched Thatha work that day. The old man's hands moved gently. He trimmed each plant with care. He scattered seeds for the birds. He left water in shallow dishes for the squirrels. He was not tired or grumpy. He looked happy.
'But you do not get anything from this,' the boy said.
'I get to see them grow,' Thatha replied. 'That is more than enough.'
Kṛṣṇa is like Thatha — except Kṛṣṇa's garden is the whole universe. He does not need anything from it. But he takes care of it because every living being needs his care.
kids_content.reflection:
Have I ever helped someone even though I did not need anything in return?
Can I think of someone who works hard for others without expecting a reward?

key_verses.detailed_meaning:
Step 1 — Nothing to be done (na me asti kartavyam)
Na means 'not.' Me means 'for me.' Asti means 'there is.' Kartavyam is a कृत्य-प्रत्ययान्तम् from कृ, meaning 'what must be done, duty, obligation.' Kṛṣṇa states that there is no obligation, no duty, no compulsory action anywhere for him. He is not bound by any law of karma.

Step 2 — O Pārtha (pārtha)
Pārtha is a vocative, addressing Arjuna as the son of Pṛthā (Kuntī). The direct address makes the teaching personal and intimate. Kṛṣṇa is not making a philosophical abstraction; he is telling his dear friend why even the Lord continues to act.

Step 3 — In the three worlds (triṣu lokeṣu kiñcana)
Triṣu lokeṣu means 'in the three worlds' — bhūlokaḥ (earth), antarikṣalokaḥ (atmosphere/intermediate realm), and svargalokaḥ (heaven). Kiñcana means 'anything whatsoever.' The scope is total: not in any world, not in any dimension, does any duty exist for the Lord.

Step 4 — Nothing unattained that needs attaining (na anavāptam avāptavyam)
Anavāptam means 'not yet obtained.' Avāptavyam means 'needing to be obtained, worthy of attainment.' For ordinary beings, desire drives action: we act to gain what we lack. Kṛṣṇa lacks nothing. He is pūrṇa — complete. This eliminates desire as a motivator for his action.

Step 5 — Yet I engage in action (varta eva ca karmaṇi)
Varte is लट्-लकारः, उत्तमपुरुष एकवचनम्, आत्मनेपदम्, from वृत्, meaning 'I am engaged in, I continue.' Eva emphasizes the reality: 'I truly am engaged.' Ca means 'and, yet.' Karmaṇi is सप्तमी of कर्मन्, meaning 'in action.' Despite having no need and no obligation, the Lord acts.

Step 6 — The nature of Īśvara's action
Śaṅkarācārya explains that Kṛṣṇa's action is not driven by karma-phala (fruit of action) or kartṛtva-abhimāna (sense of doership). It is driven by karuṇā (compassion) and dharma-rakṣaṇa (protection of righteousness). This is līlā — divine play without bondage. The Lord acts to maintain cosmic order, to set an example, and to guide beings toward liberation.

Step 7 — The argument for Arjuna
The implicit argument is devastating: if even the Lord — who is beyond all obligation and beyond all need — continues to act, then Arjuna, who still has duties and who still seeks liberation, has no excuse to withdraw. The verse eliminates the last refuge of escapism disguised as spirituality.
key_verses.more_stories:
1. Viṣṇu Sustains the Universe Through Avatāra (Purāṇic Tradition)
The Purāṇas describe how Bhagavān Viṣṇu descends into creation through various avatāras whenever dharma declines and adharma rises. As Matsya (the fish), he saved the Vedas from a deluge. As Kūrma (the tortoise), he supported the churning of the ocean. As Varāha (the boar), he rescued the earth from the depths. As Narasiṁha, he protected Prahlāda and destroyed tyranny.
In each avatāra, the Lord had nothing personal to gain. He was not seeking power, wealth, or fame. He descended because beings were suffering and dharma was in danger. His action was born entirely from compassion and the maintenance of cosmic order.
This pattern across multiple avatāras illustrates the teaching of 3.22 on a cosmic scale. The Lord who has nothing to attain continues to act — again and again, age after age — for the welfare of creation.

2. Kṛṣṇa Carries Sudāmā's Rice Pouch (Bhāgavata Purāṇa)
When Sudāmā visited Dvārakā, Kṛṣṇa — the king of a golden city, the lord of sixteen thousand queens, the wielder of the Sudarśana cakra — personally received his poor friend. He washed Sudāmā's dusty feet with his own hands. He seated him on his own bed. He fanned him.
Sudāmā had brought a small pouch of beaten rice. Kṛṣṇa snatched it with delight and ate it, savoring the humble offering as if it were a royal feast. He did not need the rice. He did not need Sudāmā's friendship for any worldly purpose. He acted purely from love.
The contrast is breathtaking: the master of all wealth eating beaten rice; the lord of all beings washing a poor man's feet. This is the living meaning of 'varta eva ca karmaṇi' — I continue to act, not from need, but from love.

3. Śiva Drinks the Poison at the Ocean's Churning (Purāṇic Tradition)
During the churning of the cosmic ocean, a terrible poison called hālāhala emerged, threatening to destroy all creation. Neither the devas nor the asuras could handle it. In desperation, they turned to Lord Śiva.
Śiva had nothing to gain by drinking the poison. He was already the lord of ascetics, beyond worldly attachment, established in his own infinite nature. Yet when the situation demanded it, he drank the poison without hesitation. Pārvatī pressed his throat to prevent the poison from descending further, and his throat turned blue — earning him the name Nīlakaṇṭha.
Śiva did not act for reward. He acted because creation needed protection. This act of selfless engagement — from one who needed nothing — mirrors Kṛṣṇa's statement in 3.22.

4. Bhagavān Rāma Builds a Bridge for the Vānaras (Rāmāyaṇa)
When the army of vānaras needed to cross the ocean to reach Laṅkā, Rāma did not simply use his divine power to teleport everyone across. He participated in the effort. Tradition describes Rāma lifting stones alongside Hanumān, Nala, and the other vānaras as they built the bridge (setu) across the sea.
Rāma was an avatāra of Viṣṇu. He could have accomplished anything by mere will. But he chose to act alongside his companions, sharing their labor, encouraging their effort, and honoring their contribution. His participation was not out of need. It was out of love and the desire to set the right example.
This echoes 3.22: the Lord who needs nothing engages in action alongside those who do, not because he must, but because his example sustains the world.

5. A Mother's Night Vigil (Everyday Life)
In countless homes across the world, a mother sits awake at night beside a sick child. She has already cared for the family all day. She is tired. She has nothing to gain by losing sleep. No one will give her an award for being awake at 3 AM.
But she sits, checking the child's temperature, offering water, whispering reassurance. Her action comes not from obligation written in a contract but from love that asks for nothing in return.
This humble scene is the closest everyday reflection of Kṛṣṇa's teaching. The Lord who needs nothing acts for the sake of beings. A mother who needs nothing more for herself acts for the sake of her child. Pure action, born from fullness, is the highest form of karma-yoga.

rich_grammar.padacchedah: न मे पार्थ अस्ति कर्तव्यम् त्रिषु लोकेषु किञ्चन । न अनवाप्तम् अवाप्तव्यम् वर्ते एव च कर्मणि ॥
rich_grammar.pratipadarthah: न | (na) = not मे | (me) = for me पार्थ | (pārtha) = O son of Pṛthā (Arjuna) अस्ति | (asti) = there is कर्तव्यम् | (kartavyam) = duty, what must be done त्रिषु | (triṣu) = in the three लोकेषु | (lokeṣu) = worlds किञ्चन | (kiñcana) = anything whatsoever न | (na) = not अनवाप्तम् | (anavāptam) = unattained अवाप्तव्यम् | (avāptavyam) = needing to be attained वर्ते | (varte) = I engage in, I continue in एव | (eva) = indeed, truly च | (ca) = and, yet कर्मणि | (karmaṇi) = in action
rich_grammar.anvayah: पार्थ, मे त्रिषु लोकेषु किञ्चन कर्तव्यम् न अस्ति । अनवाप्तम् अवाप्तव्यम् न (अस्ति) । (तथापि) कर्मणि एव च वर्ते ।
rich_grammar.sandhi: मे + पार्थ = मे पार्थ | पार्थ + अस्ति = पार्थास्ति | न + अनवाप्तम् = नानवाप्तम् | अनवाप्तम् + अवाप्तव्यम् = अनवाप्तमवाप्तव्यम् | वर्ते + एव = वर्त एव
rich_grammar.samasa: कर्तव्यम् = कर्तुं योग्यम् — कृत्य-प्रत्ययान्तम् from कृ; what ought to be done, duty. | अनवाप्तम् = न अवाप्तम् — नञ्-तत्पुरुषः; not attained, unattained. | अवाप्तव्यम् = अवाप्तुं योग्यम् — कृत्य-प्रत्ययान्तम् from अव + आप्; needing to be attained. | त्रिषु लोकेषु = three worlds — numeral adjective + noun in सप्तमी बहुवचनम्.
rich_grammar.other: अस्ति is लट्-लकारः, प्रथमपुरुष एकवचनम्, परस्मैपदम्, from अस्, meaning 'is, exists.' | वर्ते is लट्-लकारः, उत्तमपुरुष एकवचनम्, आत्मनेपदम्, from वृत्, meaning 'I am engaged in, I continue in.' | किञ्चन is an indefinite pronoun used with न to mean 'nothing whatsoever.' | The verse has two negation clauses (no duty exists, nothing unattained needs attaining) followed by one affirmation (yet I act). This structure emphasizes the paradox of the Lord's selfless engagement. | मे is चतुर्थी (dative) or षष्ठी (genitive) of अस्मद्: 'for me.'

key_verses.final_takeaway: Even Bhagavān Kṛṣṇa, who has nothing to accomplish and nothing left to attain, continues to act for the welfare of the world. If the Lord himself does not withdraw from action, no seeker can justify inaction. The highest form of action flows not from need but from fullness and compassion.

key_verses.title: If the Lord Were to Stop
key_verses.sanskrit:
यदि ह्यहं(न्) न वर्तेयं(ञ्) जातु कर्मण्यतन्द्रितः ।
मम वर्त्मानुवर्तन्ते मनुष्याः(फ्) पार्थ सर्वशः ॥
key_verses.transliteration:
yadi hyahaṁ(n) na varteyaṁ(ñ) jātu karmaṇyatandritaḥ ।
mama vartmānuvartante manuṣyāḥ(f) pārtha sarvaśaḥ ॥
key_verses.one_line_meaning: For if I were ever to cease engaging in action tirelessly, O Pārtha, people would follow My path in every way — the world would lapse into inaction and chaos.
key_verses.concise_journey: Having declared that he has nothing to achieve yet continues to act (3.22), Bhagavān Kṛṣṇa now explains the consequence of his hypothetical withdrawal. If the Lord stopped acting, all human beings would follow his example and become inactive. Since the Lord's conduct is the ultimate pramāṇam (standard), his inaction would lead to universal inaction, collapse of dharma, and the destruction of cosmic order. This verse powerfully connects the personal responsibility of the leader to the stability of the entire world.
key_verses.meaning_detail:
This verse completes the argument begun in 3.20. Kṛṣṇa has said: (1) Janaka and others attained perfection through action (3.20), (2) great people set the standard that others follow (3.21), and (3) the Lord himself continues to act despite having nothing to gain (3.22). Now he gives the final reason — the catastrophic consequence of his withdrawal.

Yadi hi ahaṁ na varteyam jātu karmaṇi atandritaḥ — if I were to not engage in action, even once, without laziness. The word jātu means 'ever, at any time' — even a single lapse would be dangerous. Atandritaḥ means 'without laziness, without weariness, tirelessly.' This word reveals that Kṛṣṇa's engagement in action is not reluctant or intermittent. It is tireless and continuous. The hypothetical is introduced with yadi ('if') and the potential mood varteyam ('I were to engage') — this is a contrary-to-fact condition, meaning Kṛṣṇa will never actually stop, but he is showing what would happen if he did.

Mama vartma anuvartante manuṣyāḥ pārtha sarvaśaḥ — people follow my path in every way, O Pārtha. Vartma means 'path, way, track.' Anuvartante means 'follow along, walk in the footsteps of.' Sarvaśaḥ means 'in every way, completely, without exception.' The statement is sweeping: all human beings, without exception, follow the Lord's example.

Śaṅkarācārya explains the implication: if the Lord were to become inactive, human beings, who naturally imitate the highest authority, would also become inactive. Action would cease. Yajñas would stop. The cycle described in 3.14–16 would collapse. Rains would fail, food would diminish, and the world order would disintegrate. The result would not merely be individual laziness but civilizational collapse.

Swami Paramarthananda draws a distinction between two kinds of inaction: (1) the wise inaction of the jñānī, who has truly transcended the need for action through Self-knowledge (described in 3.17–18), and (2) the dangerous inaction of a leader who withdraws when duty calls. The Lord's own continued engagement proves that even the wisest and most complete being acts for lokasaṅgraha. Therefore, no one — seeker, jñānī, or leader — should use spiritual reasoning as an excuse to avoid responsibility.

The verse also has a subtle emotional dimension. Kṛṣṇa is telling Arjuna: 'I am not asking you to do something I am unwilling to do myself. I act tirelessly, even though I need nothing. Now you — who still have much to learn and much to gain — should certainly act.' The Lord leads by example, which is the most powerful form of teaching.
key_verses.story:
The Sun That Never Rests (Vedic / Upaniṣadic Tradition)
In the Vedic worldview, the sun is one of the most powerful symbols of tireless, selfless action. Every morning, without fail, the sun rises. It does not take a holiday. It does not ask for gratitude. It does not negotiate terms. It simply performs its function — giving light, enabling life, driving the seasons, sustaining the food chain.
The Chāndogya Upaniṣad and the Bṛhadāraṇyaka Upaniṣad both reference the sun as a symbol of dharma in action. The sun's movement drives the rain cycle (described in Gītā 3.14–15). Without the sun, there is no evaporation. Without evaporation, no rain. Without rain, no food. Without food, no beings. The entire chain of life depends on the sun's tireless engagement.
Now imagine if the sun decided to rest. Not permanently — just for a few days. Within hours, the temperature would plummet. Within days, plants would begin to die. Within weeks, the food chain would collapse. Within months, life on earth would be threatened. The sun's 'inaction' would cascade into universal destruction.
This is precisely Kṛṣṇa's argument in 3.23. If the Lord — the cosmic sun of dharma — were to stop acting, the world would follow his example and collapse into inaction. The sun does not act for personal gain. It shines because that is its nature. Similarly, Kṛṣṇa acts not out of need but because creation depends on his continuous engagement.
The next time you see the sunrise, remember this teaching. That tireless, silent, undemanding action — sustaining all life without asking for anything — is the model of action Kṛṣṇa describes in this verse.
key_verses.real_life_example:
Consider a family where the father has always been the anchor of discipline and structure. He wakes early, exercises, goes to work, comes home, helps with homework, attends school meetings, and manages the household responsibly. The children, consciously or unconsciously, absorb this rhythm. They learn that mornings are for activity, commitments are to be honored, and effort is a normal part of life.

Now imagine this father suddenly stops. He sleeps until noon, ignores his work, watches television all day, and abandons his responsibilities. He does not say anything negative. He does not tell the children to be lazy. He simply stops acting.

What happens? Within weeks, the children's routines start deteriorating. The older child stays up late and misses school. The younger one stops doing homework. The household rhythm collapses — not because anyone announced a change, but because the person whose example held everything together withdrew from action.

This is the principle of 3.23 in daily life. The 'world' in this context is the family. The 'Lord' is the parent or elder whose conduct sets the standard. When that anchor stops acting, the entire system follows.

The teaching applies equally to teachers, community leaders, organizational heads, and anyone whose behavior serves as a reference point for others. Their engagement holds the group together. Their withdrawal causes the group to scatter. This is why Kṛṣṇa says he never stops — because the world depends on his tireless example.
key_verses.reflection:
If I stopped doing my duties today, what would happen to the people who depend on me?
Do I sometimes use spiritual ideas as an excuse to avoid action that is genuinely needed?
Can I commit to tireless, selfless engagement in my sphere of influence, following the Lord's own example?

kids_content.explanation_script:
Kṛṣṇa says something very powerful here. He tells Arjuna: 'If I stopped working, everyone would stop too. People follow what I do. If I became lazy, the whole world would become lazy.'
Think about it this way: the sun rises every single day. It never says, 'I am tired. I will not shine today.' If the sun stopped shining, what would happen? Everything would become dark and cold. Plants would die. Animals would have no food. People would be in trouble.
Kṛṣṇa works like the sun. He never stops taking care of the world. And he wants Arjuna to understand: important people cannot just stop. When you have a responsibility, people are counting on you.
kids_content.story:
The Day the Clock Stopped
In a small town, there was a big clock tower in the center of the market square. Every morning, the clockkeeper, old Mr. Sharma, climbed the stairs and wound the clock. The clock chimed at every hour. People used it to know when to open shops, when to take lunch, and when to go home.
Mr. Sharma had been doing this for forty years. Nobody thanked him. Nobody noticed him. They only noticed the clock.
One day, Mr. Sharma felt tired. 'Nobody cares about me,' he thought. 'Let the clock stop. Let them see what happens.'
So he stayed home. By 9 AM, the clock had stopped. At first, nobody noticed. Then the baker missed his bread timing. The school bell did not match. The bus driver left early. The market became confused. By afternoon, three arguments had started because people disagreed about the time. By evening, the whole town was in a muddle.
The mayor came to Mr. Sharma's house. 'What happened to the clock?'
Mr. Sharma said, 'I thought nobody cared.'
The mayor looked serious. 'The whole town depends on that clock. And the clock depends on you. When you stopped, everything stopped.'
Mr. Sharma understood. The next morning, he climbed the stairs and wound the clock. The chimes rang out, and the town settled back into order.
Kṛṣṇa is like Mr. Sharma — except his clock is the whole universe. If he stopped, everything would stop. That is why Kṛṣṇa never rests. And that is why he tells Arjuna: you too have people counting on you. Do not stop.
kids_content.reflection:
If I stopped doing my chores at home, what would happen to my family?
Can I think of someone who works every day without complaining, like the sun or the clockkeeper?

key_verses.detailed_meaning:
Step 1 — If (yadi hi)
Yadi means 'if' — introducing a hypothetical condition. Hi means 'indeed, for' — adding emphasis. The hypothetical nature is important: Kṛṣṇa is not saying he will stop. He is showing Arjuna the consequence of inaction by projecting a contrary-to-fact scenario.

Step 2 — I were to not engage (ahaṁ na varteyam)
Aham means 'I.' Na means 'not.' Varteyam is विधिलिङ्, उत्तमपुरुष एकवचनम् of वृत्, meaning 'I were to engage, I should act.' The optative/potential mood indicates a hypothetical: 'if I were to not engage.' Kṛṣṇa speaks in first person as Īśvara.

Step 3 — Ever, tirelessly in action (jātu karmaṇi atandritaḥ)
Jātu means 'ever, at any time.' Karmaṇi is सप्तमी of कर्मन्, meaning 'in action.' Atandritaḥ means 'without laziness, without fatigue, tirelessly.' The combination means: if I were to stop — even once — engaging tirelessly in action. The word atandritaḥ shows that the Lord's engagement is not occasional or reluctant but vigorous and continuous.

Step 4 — People follow My path (mama vartma anuvartante manuṣyāḥ)
Mama means 'my.' Vartma means 'path, way, track, course.' Anuvartante is लट्-लकारः, प्रथमपुरुष बहुवचनम्, आत्मनेपदम् of अनु + वृत्, meaning 'they follow, they walk along.' Manuṣyāḥ means 'human beings.' People naturally follow the Lord's path — his conduct sets the cosmic standard.

Step 5 — O Pārtha, in every way (pārtha sarvaśaḥ)
Pārtha is the vocative, addressing Arjuna directly. Sarvaśaḥ means 'in every way, completely, from all sides.' The following is not partial or selective — it is total. Whatever the Lord does, the world replicates in every dimension.

Step 6 — The cascading consequence
Śaṅkarācārya's commentary spells out what would follow: if the Lord became inactive, human beings would become inactive. The yajña-cycle (3.14–16) would collapse. Varṇa-saṅkara (confusion of social order) would arise. Dharma would perish. The very fabric of civilization would unravel. This shows that the Lord's action is not incidental — it is the foundation of cosmic stability.

Step 7 — The teaching for Arjuna
The personal message is clear: Arjuna, you are a leader. People watch you. If you drop your weapons and walk away, warriors across the land will think it is acceptable to abandon duty when it becomes painful. Your inaction will not be private; it will cascade through society. Therefore, act — not merely for personal liberation but for the welfare of the world.
key_verses.more_stories:
1. Dhṛtarāṣṭra's Blindness of Attachment Leads to Ruin (Mahābhārata)
Dhṛtarāṣṭra was the blind king of Hastināpura — blind not only in body but also in judgment. He knew that Duryodhana's actions were unjust: the attempt to poison Bhīma, the conspiracy of the house of lac, the rigged dice game, the humiliation of Draupadī. At each step, Dhṛtarāṣṭra knew what was right. Vidura told him. Bhīṣma told him. His own conscience told him.
But he did not act. He did not stop Duryodhana. He did not enforce dharma. He chose inaction — not out of wisdom but out of attachment to his son.
What followed? The entire Kuru dynasty followed the king's passive example. If the king would not uphold dharma, why should ministers? If the king would not punish injustice, why should the court? The culture of impunity spread from the throne outward, eventually leading to the catastrophic war at Kurukṣetra.
Dhṛtarāṣṭra's story is the dark illustration of 3.23. When a leader stops acting rightly, those who depend on his example also stop. The result is not just individual failure but collective ruin.

2. Prahlāda's Continued Devotion Inspires an Entire Kingdom (Bhāgavata Purāṇa)
After the destruction of Hiraṇyakaśipu by Lord Narasiṁha, young Prahlāda was placed on the throne of the asura kingdom. He could have rested. The enemy was gone, and the kingdom was peaceful. But Prahlāda did not become complacent.
He continued his devotion, his dharmic governance, and his compassion toward all beings — including former enemies. His tireless engagement in dharma transformed the asura kingdom. Former supporters of Hiraṇyakaśipu's tyranny gradually came under the influence of Prahlāda's example. Dharma spread not through force but through the king's living conduct.
If Prahlāda had stopped after the crisis was over — if he had said, 'My work is done, I will now withdraw' — the kingdom would have reverted to its old ways. But his tireless action (atandritaḥ) held the realm together. This is the positive illustration of 3.23: when the leader continues to act, the people continue to follow.

3. Vyāsa Compiles the Vedas for Future Generations (Purāṇic Tradition)
Vyāsa — Kṛṣṇa Dvaipāyana — is credited with organizing the entire body of Vedic knowledge into four Vedas, composing the Mahābhārata, writing the Brahma-Sūtras, and narrating the eighteen major Purāṇas. His output was staggering. His contribution to human civilization is incalculable.
Vyāsa did not compile these works for personal fame. He was already a ṛṣi of the highest order. He acted because he foresaw that in the coming age (Kali-yuga), people's memories would weaken, understanding would diminish, and the knowledge needed for liberation would be lost.
His tireless work ensured that generations of seekers would have access to the highest teachings. If Vyāsa had said, 'I know the truth; why should I bother writing it down for others?' the entire Vedic tradition would have been lost. His refusal to stop working, despite personal fullness, sustained dharma for millennia.
This is the spirit of 3.23: tireless engagement in action, not for personal gain but for the continuity of dharma and the welfare of future generations.

4. A Teacher Who Never Gave Up on a Difficult Student (Modern Life)
Mrs. Iyer taught mathematics in a government school. One of her students, Rajan, was consistently failing. Other teachers had written him off. His parents had stopped hoping. Rajan himself believed he could not learn.
Mrs. Iyer refused to stop trying. She stayed after school, gave extra worksheets, used different methods, and patiently repeated concepts. When Rajan failed the midterm, she did not say, 'I have done my part.' She adjusted her approach and tried again.
By the end of the year, Rajan passed. Not brilliantly, but he passed. More importantly, he believed he could learn. Years later, he became an engineer and credited Mrs. Iyer's tireless effort.
But the impact went beyond Rajan. Other teachers watched Mrs. Iyer. Her persistence set a standard. Slowly, a few of them started staying after school too. The culture of the school shifted — from 'some students cannot learn' to 'we have not found the right method yet.'
This is 3.23 in everyday life. When a leader acts tirelessly, the people around follow. When a leader stops, the people stop too. Mrs. Iyer's example held her small world together.

5. The River That Never Stops Flowing (Nature as Teacher)
Indian spiritual literature often uses rivers as examples of selfless, tireless action. The Gaṅgā flows from the Himalayas to the sea, day after day, century after century. She does not flow for herself. She flows for the millions who drink from her, bathe in her, irrigate their fields with her water, and seek purification in her presence.
The Gaṅgā does not say, 'I have given enough water today; I will rest tomorrow.' She does not negotiate. She does not calculate. She flows.
If the Gaṅgā stopped, the plains of northern India would dry up. Agriculture would fail. Cities would lack water. Civilizations built along her banks would collapse.
Kṛṣṇa's action is like the Gaṅgā's flow — tireless, selfless, and sustaining. The verse says: if I stopped, the world would follow my path of inaction. The river teaches us that true greatness lies not in occasional generosity but in sustained, tireless giving without expectation.

rich_grammar.padacchedah: यदि हि अहम् न वर्तेयम् जातु कर्मणि अतन्द्रितः । मम वर्त्म अनुवर्तन्ते मनुष्याः पार्थ सर्वशः ॥
rich_grammar.pratipadarthah: यदि | (yadi) = if हि | (hi) = indeed, for अहम् | (aham) = I न | (na) = not वर्तेयम् | (varteyam) = were to engage in, should act जातु | (jātu) = ever, at any time कर्मणि | (karmaṇi) = in action अतन्द्रितः | (atandritaḥ) = without laziness, tirelessly मम | (mama) = my वर्त्म | (vartma) = path, way, course अनुवर्तन्ते | (anuvartante) = follow, walk along मनुष्याः | (manuṣyāḥ) = human beings, people पार्थ | (pārtha) = O son of Pṛthā (Arjuna) सर्वशः | (sarvaśaḥ) = in every way, completely
rich_grammar.anvayah: पार्थ, यदि हि अहम् अतन्द्रितः जातु कर्मणि न वर्तेयम्, मनुष्याः सर्वशः मम वर्त्म अनुवर्तन्ते ।
rich_grammar.sandhi: हि + अहम् = ह्यहम् | अहम् + न = अहं न | न + वर्तेयम् = न वर्तेयम् | वर्तेयम् + जातु = वर्तेयं जातु | कर्मणि + अतन्द्रितः = कर्मण्यतन्द्रितः | वर्त्म + अनुवर्तन्ते = वर्त्मानुवर्तन्ते
rich_grammar.samasa: अतन्द्रितः = न तन्द्रितः — नञ्-तत्पुरुषः; not lazy, tireless, vigorous. | वर्त्म = path, way — from वृत् (to move, to exist); the track or course one follows. | अनुवर्तन्ते = अनु + वृत् — following after, walking in the footsteps of.
rich_grammar.other: वर्तेयम् is विधिलिङ्, उत्तमपुरुष एकवचनम्, आत्मनेपदम्, from वृत्, meaning 'I were to engage in.' The optative mood indicates a hypothetical condition. | अनुवर्तन्ते is लट्-लकारः, प्रथमपुरुष बहुवचनम्, आत्मनेपदम्, from अनु + वृत्, meaning 'they follow.' | जातु is an अव्ययम् meaning 'ever, at any time.' Used with न to emphasize 'never' or in a hypothetical to mean 'even once.' | सर्वशः is an अव्ययम् meaning 'in every way, from all sides, completely.' It indicates the totality of imitation — people do not follow partially; they follow in every respect. | अतन्द्रितः is a नञ्-युक्त क्तप्रत्ययान्तम् from तन्द्री (laziness, drowsiness), meaning 'free from laziness, tireless.' The Lord's engagement is not grudging or intermittent; it is vigorous and unceasing.

key_verses.final_takeaway: The Lord never ceases to act, because the world follows his example in every way. If he stopped, dharma would collapse and chaos would follow. This teaches every person of influence — parent, teacher, leader — that tireless, selfless engagement is not optional but essential for the welfare of all who depend on their example.
