key_verses.title: The Cosmic Consequences of Inaction
key_verses.sanskrit:
उत्सीदेयुरिमे लोका(:) न कुर्यां(ङ्) कर्म चेदहम् ।
सङ्करस्य च कर्ता स्याम् उपहन्यामिमाः(फ्) प्रजाः ॥
key_verses.transliteration:
utsīdeyurime lokā(ḥ) na kuryāṁ(ṅ) karma cedaham ।
saṅkarasya ca kartā syām upahanyāmimāḥ(f) prajāḥ ॥
key_verses.one_line_meaning: These worlds would perish if I did not perform action; I would be the cause of confusion and would destroy these beings.
key_verses.concise_journey: Bhagavān Kṛṣṇa continues His personal example of why even the Lord engages in action. If He, the Supreme, were to stop acting, the entire cosmic order would collapse. People follow the leader's example, and His inaction would produce saṅkara — the confusion and mixing of dharmic roles — ultimately destroying society.
key_verses.meaning_detail:
In the preceding verses (3.22–23), Bhagavān Kṛṣṇa explained that He has nothing to gain in all three worlds, yet He continues to act so that people do not follow a wrong example. Now He spells out the catastrophic consequences if He were to cease action.

Utsīdeyuḥ ime lokāḥ — these worlds would perish. The word utsīdeyuḥ (from ut + sad) means to sink, collapse, or be destroyed. If the Lord stopped performing His cosmic functions — sustaining the sun, the rain cycle, the seasons, dharmic governance — the entire framework on which life depends would fall apart. This is not merely political or social collapse; it is the dissolution of the cosmic yajña-cycle described in 3.14–16.

Saṅkarasya ca kartā syām — I would become the author of saṅkara. Śaṅkarācārya glosses saṅkara as varṇa-saṅkara, the confusion and mixing of dharmic roles and responsibilities. When proper order breaks down, duties are neglected, responsibilities are abandoned, and society degenerates. The word saṅkara echoes Arjuna's own fear in 1.41–43, where he worried about varṇa-saṅkara arising from the war. Here Kṛṣṇa shows that inaction — not action — is the real cause of such confusion.

Upahanyām imāḥ prajāḥ — I would destroy these creatures. The destruction is not merely physical. When the cosmic order collapses, beings lose their support system for dharmic living, spiritual growth, and eventual liberation. The Lord's action is therefore an act of compassion (karuṇā) toward all beings.

From the Advaita standpoint, this verse must be understood carefully. The Self (ātman) is actionless (akartā). Yet Bhagavān, speaking from the vyāvahārika (transactional) standpoint, shows that the one who occupies a position of influence must act responsibly. The teaching is addressed to Arjuna, a kṣatriya leader, to show that his withdrawal from duty would have consequences far beyond his personal concerns.
key_verses.story:
Kṛṣṇa Lifts Govardhana to Protect the World Order (Bhāgavata Purāṇa)
In Vṛndāvana, the cowherd community had been worshipping Indra, the lord of rain, through an annual yajña. Young Kṛṣṇa questioned this practice, not to destroy the worship tradition, but to redirect the community's gratitude toward the actual sustainer — Govardhana hill, the cows, and the dharmic order of nature.
Indra, enraged by the disruption of his worship, unleashed devastating rains upon Vṛndāvana. The storm was not merely weather — it was cosmic fury aimed at punishing a village for daring to change their ritual. The people, animals, and land were all in danger of destruction.
Kṛṣṇa did not sit idle and philosophize. He lifted Govardhana hill on His little finger and sheltered the entire community beneath it. For seven days and nights, He stood there, holding the mountain aloft while people, cows, and calves found refuge under its shelter.
This act was not for His own benefit — He had nothing to gain. It was lokasaṅgraha in its most vivid form. Had Kṛṣṇa remained passive, the people of Vṛndāvana would have perished. His action preserved the social order, protected innocent beings, and demonstrated that the Lord acts not from need but from compassion.
The Govardhana episode illustrates 3.24 directly. If the Lord ceased to act, the worlds would collapse. His action sustains, protects, and maintains the dharmic order on which all life depends.
key_verses.real_life_example:
Consider a senior doctor in a hospital who is also the head of a department. She has achieved recognition, financial security, and personal fulfillment. She could retire early and live comfortably. But her department depends on her leadership — junior doctors learn from her rounds, patients trust her judgment, and hospital protocols run smoothly under her guidance.

If she suddenly stopped coming to work, saying, 'I have nothing more to gain,' the department would suffer. Junior doctors would lose mentorship. Critical decisions would be delayed. Patient care quality would decline. Her inaction would create confusion (saṅkara) in the system she leads.

This does not mean she must work forever out of obligation. But until the system can sustain itself, her responsible action maintains order. The same applies to parents who guide families, teachers who shape classrooms, and community leaders whose presence holds things together.

Kṛṣṇa's teaching here is about responsible leadership. Those in positions of influence cannot simply withdraw without considering the consequences of their absence. Action performed out of duty and compassion — not for personal gain — is the hallmark of a dharmic leader.
key_verses.reflection:
What responsibilities do I hold where my withdrawal would create confusion or harm for others?
Am I tempted to abandon duties prematurely under the guise of detachment or spiritual growth?
Do I see responsible action as a burden, or can I learn to see it as compassionate participation in the cosmic order?

kids_content.explanation_script:
Kṛṣṇa says something very important here. He says, 'Even I keep doing My work. If I stopped, everything would go wrong!'
Imagine if the sun decided to take a holiday. What would happen? Plants would not grow, animals would not find food, and people would be cold and confused. The whole world depends on the sun doing its job every single day.
Kṛṣṇa is like that. He keeps the whole universe running. And He does it not because He needs something, but because He cares about everyone.
This teaches us that when we have responsibilities — like homework, helping at home, or being kind to friends — we should not just stop doing them. Other people are counting on us!
kids_content.story:
The Lighthouse Keeper
On a rocky island far from the mainland, there lived an old lighthouse keeper named Ramu. Every evening, he climbed the tall stone steps and lit the great lamp at the top. Ships far out at sea would see the light and know which way to sail safely.
One stormy night, Ramu felt tired. His bones ached, and the wind was howling. 'Nobody will come tonight,' he thought. 'I can skip lighting the lamp just this once.'
But then he imagined a fishing boat caught in the storm, looking for the light. A family waiting for their father to come home safely. A cargo ship bringing food to the village.
Ramu picked himself up, climbed the steps, and lit the lamp. Sure enough, the next morning, a fisherman came to thank him. 'Your light saved us last night. We were lost in the storm.'
Ramu smiled. He realized that his job was not just about him. Many people depended on that little light.
Kṛṣṇa teaches us the same thing. When we do our work — even when it is hard — we help keep the world running smoothly. If we stop, others may suffer.
kids_content.reflection:
What is one job I do that helps others, even if they do not always say thank you?
What might happen if I stopped doing my part at home or school?

key_verses.detailed_meaning:
Step 1 — The consequence of inaction (utsīdeyuḥ ime lokāḥ):
Utsīdeyuḥ is from ut + sad (to sink, collapse, perish) in the vidhiliṅ (optative), expressing a hypothetical: 'these worlds would perish.' Lokāḥ refers not just to the physical world but to the entire social and cosmic framework sustained by dharmic action. If the Lord — or any leader people emulate — ceases to act, the support structure for life collapses.

Step 2 — The conditional clause (na kuryāṁ karma cet aham):
Cet is a conditional particle: 'if.' Na kuryām means 'I would not perform.' Kṛṣṇa speaks in the first person to make the argument personal and powerful. He is saying: even I, who have nothing to gain, would cause destruction by not acting. How much more would Arjuna's inaction harm the world?

Step 3 — Becoming the author of confusion (saṅkarasya ca kartā syām):
Saṅkara means mixing, confusion, or disorder. Śaṅkarācārya interprets this as varṇa-saṅkara — the breakdown of dharmic roles and social order. Kartā syām means 'I would become the doer/author.' The Lord would be responsible for the very chaos Arjuna feared in Chapter 1.

Step 4 — Destruction of beings (upahanyām imāḥ prajāḥ):
Upahanyām (from upa + han) means 'I would destroy' or 'I would cause harm to.' Prajāḥ means beings or creatures. This is not mere physical death but the destruction of the conditions that enable dharmic living and spiritual growth.

Step 5 — The Advaitic perspective:
From the pāramārthika (absolute) standpoint, the ātman is akartā (non-doer). But from the vyāvahārika standpoint, one who holds a position of responsibility must act. This verse addresses Arjuna at the vyāvahārika level: as a kṣatriya and a warrior, his inaction would have devastating consequences. The verse does not contradict the teaching of ātman as actionless; it shows why karma-yoga is necessary at the relative level.

Step 6 — Connection to the cosmic yajña-cycle:
This verse completes the argument begun in 3.14–16. Rain, food, yajña, and action form an interdependent cycle. If anyone — especially a leader — breaks the cycle by ceasing to act, the entire system suffers. Kṛṣṇa's personal example makes the strongest possible case for Arjuna to act.
key_verses.more_stories:
1. Rāma Returns to Rule Ayodhyā After Exile (Rāmāyaṇa)
After fourteen years of exile, the defeat of Rāvaṇa, and the rescue of Sītā, Rāma could have chosen to continue living in the forest. He had tasted the simplicity of a sage's life, the beauty of nature, and the freedom from palace politics. He had nothing personal to gain from kingship.
But Ayodhyā needed its king. The people had waited for fourteen years. The throne had remained symbolically empty, with Bharata placing Rāma's sandals upon it as a regent. Administration, justice, and social order all awaited the rightful ruler.
Rāma returned. He did not return for wealth, power, or enjoyment. He returned because his absence would have left the kingdom in disorder. Without a dharmic king, confusion would arise — exactly the saṅkara Kṛṣṇa warns about in this verse.
Rāma's return to Ayodhyā is a perfect illustration of 3.24. The leader's action sustains the world. His inaction would have left society without guidance and protection.

2. Bhīṣma's Lifelong Vow of Service (Mahābhārata)
Bhīṣma made an extraordinary vow: he renounced his right to the throne and to marriage so that his father Śāntanu could marry Satyavatī. This was not a one-time sacrifice. Bhīṣma then spent his entire life serving the Kuru dynasty — through multiple generations of kings, through political turmoil, through the rise of Duryodhana's adharma.
Did Bhīṣma need to stay? He had no personal stake — no wife, no children, no throne. He could have walked away to the forest at any point. But he understood that his departure would create a power vacuum. The elders who respected him, the warriors who obeyed him, the citizens who trusted him — all depended on his steady presence.
When the Mahābhārata war came, Bhīṣma fought for the Kauravas not out of agreement with Duryodhana, but because his vow placed him there. Even in a wrong situation, he continued to act, knowing that his complete withdrawal would cause greater confusion.
Bhīṣma's life shows the weight of 3.24. Those in positions of responsibility cannot simply abandon their roles. Their inaction would produce the very saṅkara Kṛṣṇa describes.

3. Vidura Advises Dhṛtarāṣṭra Despite Rejection (Mahābhārata)
Vidura was the wisest counselor in the Kuru court. He repeatedly warned Dhṛtarāṣṭra about the consequences of favoring Duryodhana over dharma. Again and again, his advice was ignored, dismissed, or overruled by the blind king's attachment to his son.
Vidura could have left the court in frustration. He had no throne to protect, no army to command, and no family ambition to pursue. His advice was unwanted. Why stay?
But Vidura continued. He spoke truth when no one wanted to hear it. He remained available when the court needed wisdom. He represented the voice of dharma in a system sliding toward destruction.
When Vidura finally left the court after being insulted by Duryodhana, the absence was felt deeply. Without that dharmic voice, the court moved faster toward the catastrophic war.
Vidura's persistence illustrates 3.24. Even when the fruits of action seem absent, the responsible person continues to act because withdrawal would accelerate the disorder.

4. The Devas Churn the Ocean Despite Hardship (Bhāgavata Purāṇa)
When the devas lost their strength due to Durvāsā's curse, the cosmic order was disrupted. The asuras gained power, and dharma declined. The remedy required churning the ocean of milk — a massive cooperative effort that demanded patience, endurance, and action.
The devas could have given up. The task was enormous, the obstacles were many, and they had to cooperate with the very asuras who opposed them. Mount Mandara served as the churning rod, Vāsuki the serpent as the rope, and Viṣṇu as the tortoise supporting the mountain.
Poison emerged first. Then dangers. Then temptations. Only through persistent action did the nectar finally appear. Had the devas stopped midway, the cosmic imbalance would have continued, and all beings would have suffered.
This cosmic narrative embodies the teaching of 3.24. The work of maintaining dharmic order requires sustained action. Abandoning the effort midway would lead to the destruction Kṛṣṇa warns about.

5. Sage Vasiṣṭha Continues Teaching After Personal Tragedy (Rāmāyaṇa / Purāṇic Tradition)
Vasiṣṭha, the family priest of the Ikṣvāku dynasty, suffered tremendous personal loss. His conflict with Viśvāmitra led to the death of his sons. The grief was unbearable. A lesser person would have withdrawn from all worldly engagement.
But Vasiṣṭha continued his role as guru and counselor. Generations of kings depended on his guidance — from Daśaratha to Rāma. The Raghu dynasty's dharmic conduct was sustained in large part by Vasiṣṭha's steady presence and teaching.
Had Vasiṣṭha abandoned his post out of personal grief, the royal family would have lost its spiritual anchor. Decisions of state, matters of dharma, and the education of princes all required his wisdom.
Vasiṣṭha's choice to continue serving despite personal tragedy shows the spirit of 3.24. The wise do not withdraw when others depend on them. Their action prevents the collapse that would follow their absence.

rich_grammar.padacchedah: उत्सीदेयुः इमे लोकाः न कुर्याम् कर्म चेत् अहम् । सङ्करस्य च कर्ता स्याम् उपहन्याम् इमाः प्रजाः ॥
rich_grammar.pratipadarthah: उत्सीदेयुः | (utsīdeyuḥ) = would perish, would be destroyed इमे | (ime) = these लोकाः | (lokāḥ) = worlds, people न | (na) = not कुर्याम् | (kuryām) = I would perform कर्म | (karma) = action चेत् | (cet) = if अहम् | (aham) = I सङ्करस्य | (saṅkarasya) = of confusion, of mixing च | (ca) = and कर्ता | (kartā) = author, doer स्याम् | (syām) = I would become उपहन्याम् | (upahanyām) = I would destroy इमाः | (imāḥ) = these प्रजाः | (prajāḥ) = beings, creatures
rich_grammar.anvayah: अहम् कर्म न कुर्याम् चेत् इमे लोकाः उत्सीदेयुः, सङ्करस्य च कर्ता स्याम्, इमाः प्रजाः उपहन्याम् ।
rich_grammar.sandhi: उत्सीदेयुः + इमे = उत्सीदेयुरिमे | चेत् + अहम् = चेदहम् | स्याम् + उपहन्याम् = स्यामुपहन्याम् | उपहन्याम् + इमाः = उपहन्यामिमाः
rich_grammar.samasa: सङ्करस्य = सम् + करणम् — the act of mixing together; here varṇa-saṅkara, confusion of dharmic roles. Not a compound in the samāsa sense but a prefixed noun (सम् + कर).
rich_grammar.other: उत्सीदेयुः is विधिलिङ्, परस्मैपदम्, प्रथमपुरुषः, बहुवचनम् from उत् + सद् (to sink, perish). | कुर्याम् is विधिलिङ्, परस्मैपदम्, उत्तमपुरुषः, एकवचनम् from कृ (to do). | स्याम् is विधिलिङ्, परस्मैपदम्, उत्तमपुरुषः, एकवचनम् from अस् (to be). | उपहन्याम् is विधिलिङ्, परस्मैपदम्, उत्तमपुरुषः, एकवचनम् from उप + हन् (to destroy). | चेत् is an indeclinable conditional particle meaning 'if.' | The entire verse is in the optative mood (विधिलिङ्), expressing a hypothetical consequence.

key_verses.final_takeaway: Kṛṣṇa warns that even He, the Lord of the universe, continues to act because His inaction would cause cosmic disorder and the destruction of all beings. Those in positions of responsibility must act — not for personal gain, but to sustain the dharmic order on which all life depends.

key_verses.title: The Wise Act Without Attachment
key_verses.sanskrit:
सक्ताः(ख्) कर्मण्यविद्वांसो(सः) यथा कुर्वन्ति भारत ।
कुर्याद्विद्वांस्तथासक्तः(श्) चिकीर्षुर्लोकसंग्रहम् ॥
key_verses.transliteration:
saktāḥ(kh) karmaṇyavidvāṁso(aḥ) yathā kurvanti bhārata ।
kuryādvidvāṁstathāsaktaḥ(ś) cikīrṣurlokasaṅgraham ॥
key_verses.one_line_meaning: As the ignorant act with attachment to results, O Bhārata, so should the wise act without attachment, desiring the welfare and cohesion of the world.
key_verses.concise_journey: Bhagavān Kṛṣṇa now reveals the key distinction between the ignorant person and the wise person. Both act — but the internal attitude is completely different. The ignorant act out of attachment (sakta) to results, hoping action will complete them. The wise act without attachment (asakta) for lokasaṅgraha — the welfare, cohesion, and upliftment of society. This is one of the most practical verses in the Gītā for understanding karma-yoga.
key_verses.meaning_detail:
This verse is the heart of Kṛṣṇa's teaching on how a wise person should conduct himself in the world. It addresses a common doubt: if the wise person has gained Self-knowledge, why should he act at all? And if he acts, how is his action different from that of the ignorant?

Saktāḥ karmaṇi avidvāṁsaḥ — the ignorant act with attachment. The word sakta means 'attached, clinging.' The avidvān (one without Self-knowledge) performs action with the belief that the results of action will bring security, happiness, and completeness. Every action is driven by rāga-dveṣa (likes and dislikes), and the person is bound to the result. Success brings elation; failure brings despair. This attachment is the root of bondage.

Kuryāt vidvān tathā asaktaḥ — the wise should act similarly, but without attachment. The word tathā means 'in the same way' — externally, the actions may look identical. A wise teacher teaches, a wise doctor heals, a wise administrator governs — just as an ignorant person in those roles would. The difference is entirely internal. The vidvān (wise one) does not depend on the result for inner fulfillment. He acts from wholeness, not for wholeness.

Cikīrṣuḥ lokasaṁgraham — desiring the welfare of the world. Cikīrṣuḥ is the desiderative form of kṛ (to do), meaning 'one who desires to do.' Lokasaṁgraha means 'holding the world together' — maintaining social cohesion, dharmic order, and the welfare of all beings. The wise person's motivation is not selfish accumulation but universal welfare.

Śaṅkarācārya emphasizes that the wise person acts for two reasons: to set an example for others (who would otherwise follow a wrong model), and out of compassion for beings still bound by ignorance. He does not act to purify his own mind — that has already been accomplished. His action is purely for the benefit of the world.

This verse gives the practical formula for karma-yoga: act as fully as the world demands, but without the inner fever of attachment. The external performance should be excellent; the internal attitude should be free.
key_verses.story:
King Janaka Rules Mithilā as a Jñānī (Bṛhadāraṇyaka Upaniṣad / Mahābhārata)
King Janaka of Mithilā is the most celebrated example of a wise person acting in the world without attachment. He ruled a vast and prosperous kingdom, administered justice, conducted diplomacy, maintained armies, and engaged in all the activities of a king.
Yet Janaka was recognized by the greatest sages as a brahma-jñānī — one established in Self-knowledge. When Yājñavalkya taught in his court, Janaka listened with the humility of a student. When debates were held, Janaka participated not to display superiority but to clarify truth.
The tradition tells us that Janaka's wisdom did not make him withdraw from kingly duties. He could have retired to a forest āśrama and lived the life of a contemplative renunciate. But he understood that his kingdom needed a dharmic ruler. Thousands of people depended on his governance for their safety, prosperity, and moral guidance.
So Janaka continued to rule — but without attachment. When his palace was threatened, he remained inwardly calm. When prosperity came, he did not become arrogant. His actions were performed with full engagement but without the inner anxiety that comes from dependence on results.
Sages marveled at this rare combination: the intensity of a king's action with the serenity of a sage's detachment. Janaka became the living proof that knowledge and action are not enemies. The wise person can act fully while remaining inwardly free.
This is exactly what 3.25 teaches. Janaka acted tathā — in the same way as other kings — but asaktaḥ, without attachment. His motivation was lokasaṅgraha — the welfare of his people, not the accumulation of personal glory.
key_verses.real_life_example:
Consider two teachers in the same school. Both teach the same subject to the same grade level. Their external actions are identical — preparing lessons, explaining concepts, grading papers, meeting parents.

The first teacher is driven by attachment. He teaches for recognition: 'If my students top the exams, I will get the best-teacher award. If my results are poor, I will be humiliated.' Every class is colored by anxiety about outcomes. When students perform well, he is elated; when they struggle, he is frustrated and blames them.

The second teacher teaches with the same dedication — perhaps even more. But her motivation is different. She teaches because she genuinely cares about the students' understanding. She prepares thoroughly, explains patiently, and corrects compassionately. When results come, she reviews them with equanimity — learning what worked and what to improve. Her identity is not staked on the results.

From the outside, both teachers look the same. But one is sakta (attached) and the other is asakta (unattached). The asakta teacher is the karma-yogī of this verse. She acts fully but without the inner fever. Her teaching is lokasaṅgraha — it holds the world of her students together and lifts them toward growth.
key_verses.reflection:
Can I identify areas in my life where my actions are driven more by attachment to results than by genuine commitment to the work itself?
What would it look like to perform my daily duties with the same intensity but without anxiety about outcomes?
Do I understand the difference between caring about the quality of my work (which is healthy) and being attached to the results of my work (which creates suffering)?

kids_content.explanation_script:
Kṛṣṇa teaches something beautiful here. He says that a wise person does the same work as everyone else — but without worry!
Imagine two students drawing a picture in art class. One student draws beautifully, but keeps worrying: 'Will the teacher like it? Will I get first prize? What if someone else wins?' That student is talented but unhappy because of worry.
The other student also draws beautifully. She enjoys every stroke of color, does her best, and smiles. If she wins a prize, great! If not, she still had fun and is proud of her work.
Both students drew the same picture. But one was worried and one was free. Kṛṣṇa says the wise person is like the second student — doing great work without being trapped by worry about results.
kids_content.story:
The Two Gardeners
In a village, two neighbours each had a garden. Both planted the same seeds, watered them carefully, and pulled out the weeds.
The first gardener, Mohan, kept worrying. Every morning he would check, 'Have the flowers grown yet? Why is my neighbour's garden growing faster? What if mine fails?' He barely slept at night, thinking about whether his garden would be the best in the village.
The second gardener, Lata, also worked hard. She watered her plants with care, spoke to them gently, and enjoyed the sunshine while she worked. When a plant did not grow well, she tried a different spot. When flowers bloomed, she smiled and shared them with friends.
At the end of the season, both gardens were beautiful. But Mohan was exhausted and unhappy. He had spent the whole season worrying instead of enjoying. Lata was happy and refreshed. She had done the same work but without the heavy feeling of worry.
Kṛṣṇa's lesson is just like this. Do your work well. Put in your best effort. But do not carry the heavy bag of worry about results. That heavy bag does not make the flowers grow faster — it only makes your heart tired.
kids_content.reflection:
When I work hard on something, do I enjoy the doing, or am I only thinking about the result?
Can I be happy about my effort even when the result is not what I expected?

key_verses.detailed_meaning:
Step 1 — The ignorant act with attachment (saktāḥ karmaṇi avidvāṁsaḥ):
Saktāḥ means 'attached, clinging.' Karmaṇi is the locative of karma: 'in action.' Avidvāṁsaḥ is the nominative plural of avidvas: 'the unwise, the ignorant.' The ignorant person acts with emotional dependence on the outcome. He believes that action-results will produce lasting fulfillment.

Step 2 — The manner of their action (yathā kurvanti bhārata):
Yathā means 'in whatever way, as.' Kurvanti is the present tense third-person plural of kṛ: 'they act.' Bhārata is a vocative addressing Arjuna as a descendant of Bharata. Kṛṣṇa observes how the ignorant actually behave — with full engagement but driven by attachment.

Step 3 — The wise should act similarly (kuryāt vidvān tathā):
Kuryāt is the optative of kṛ: 'should do.' Vidvān is the nominative singular: 'the wise one.' Tathā means 'in the same way.' The wise person should act with the same external engagement as the ignorant — the same thoroughness, the same dedication. The difference is not in the action but in the attitude.

Step 4 — Without attachment (asaktaḥ):
Asaktaḥ is the key word: 'without attachment.' This is a nañ-tatpuruṣa compound (a + sakta). The vidvān acts fully but does not cling to the outcome. Success does not inflate him; failure does not crush him. His inner state is steady because it is rooted in Self-knowledge, not in action-results.

Step 5 — Desiring the welfare of the world (cikīrṣuḥ lokasaṁgraham):
Cikīrṣuḥ is the desiderative form of kṛ: 'one who desires to do, one who intends.' Lokasaṁgraham means 'the holding together of the world, the welfare of society.' The wise person's motivation is not selfish gain but universal welfare. This is compassionate action born of wisdom.

Step 6 — The practical formula of karma-yoga:
The verse gives the clearest instruction: perform action with the same intensity as everyone else, but with a fundamentally different inner attitude. Intensity of engagement plus freedom from attachment — this is the karma-yoga formula. The wise person demonstrates by example that excellence and detachment can coexist.
key_verses.more_stories:
1. Vidura Serves the Kuru Court with Wisdom and Detachment (Mahābhārata)
Vidura served the Kuru dynasty as minister and counselor for decades. He was deeply wise — perhaps the wisest person in the court. Yet he served alongside Duryodhana, Śakuni, and others who constantly worked against dharma.
Vidura performed his duties with full dedication. He advised Dhṛtarāṣṭra honestly, warned the court about the consequences of adharma, and tried to protect the Pāṇḍavas when they were in danger. His counsel was consistently ignored, yet he continued to serve.
Was Vidura attached to results? If he were, he would have given up long ago. His advice was rejected time and again. But Vidura understood that his role was to speak truth and serve dharma — not to control outcomes. His action was asakta: without dependence on whether the king listened.
When the situation became truly impossible and Duryodhana insulted him directly, Vidura quietly left. His departure was also without drama — no bitterness, no resentment. He had served fully while it was possible, and departed when the situation demanded it.
Vidura exemplifies the teaching of 3.25. He acted as any minister would — with dedication and thoroughness — but without attachment to whether his advice was accepted. His motivation was lokasaṅgraha: the welfare of the Kuru kingdom.

2. Hanumān Serves Rāma Without Seeking Reward (Rāmāyaṇa)
Hanumān is perhaps the greatest example of selfless service in Indian tradition. When he discovered Sītā in Laṅkā, he did not rest. He delivered Rāma's message, burned Laṅkā's tail-end, returned to report, built the bridge with the vānara army, fought in the war, carried the Sañjīvanī mountain to save Lakṣmaṇa, and served Rāma at every turn.
Did Hanumān seek reward? He had no desire for a kingdom, wealth, or status. When Rāma offered him anything he wished after the war, Hanumān asked only for continued devotion and service.
Hanumān's service was intense — no one worked harder. But it was asakta — free from any personal agenda. He did not calculate, 'What will I get from this?' He acted from love, from a sense of dharma, and from wholeness.
This is the spirit of 3.25. The wise person acts with the same external intensity as anyone — but without the inner fever of 'What's in it for me?' Hanumān's example shows that selfless action is not passive or lazy. It is the most powerful kind of action.

3. Swami Dayānanda Sarasvatī Teaches Vedānta for Decades (Modern Vedāntic Tradition)
Swami Dayānanda Sarasvatī spent his entire adult life teaching Vedānta — the knowledge of the Self. He established Arsha Vidya Gurukulam, trained hundreds of teachers, conducted long-term residential courses, wrote extensively, and traveled the world teaching.
His schedule was intense. He taught for hours each day, met students individually, handled organizational challenges, and continued studying śāstra himself. Externally, his life looked as busy as any corporate leader's.
But the internal attitude was different. He often taught about the difference between binding action and liberating action. Binding action, he said, is driven by the sense of 'I am incomplete, and this action will complete me.' Liberating action is performed with the understanding, 'I am already full; this action is my contribution to the world.'
Swami Dayānanda lived what he taught. His action was lokasaṅgraha — preserving and transmitting the teaching tradition for future generations. He worked tirelessly but without the anxious attachment that burns people out.
His life is a modern illustration of 3.25: acting with full engagement, without attachment, for the welfare of the world.

4. Yudhiṣṭhira Rules After the War Despite Inner Pain (Mahābhārata)
After the devastating war at Kurukṣetra, Yudhiṣṭhira was crowned king of Hastināpura. But he was not happy. The war had cost him his elders, teachers, brothers-in-law, and millions of soldiers. He carried deep guilt and grief.
Yudhiṣṭhira wanted to renounce the kingdom and retire to the forest. But Kṛṣṇa, Vyāsa, and Bhīṣma counseled him: the kingdom needs you. If you abandon the throne now, the people will suffer. There will be no dharmic ruler to guide them.
So Yudhiṣṭhira ruled. He performed the Aśvamedha yajña, administered justice, cared for the citizens, and maintained dharmic governance. He acted fully — but without attachment to royal pleasure. His heart longed for peace, yet his duty demanded action.
Yudhiṣṭhira's post-war kingship illustrates 3.25. He acted as any king would — with thoroughness and dedication — but without personal attachment to the kingdom. His motivation was the welfare of his people, not the enjoyment of power.

rich_grammar.padacchedah: सक्ताः कर्मणि अविद्वांसः यथा कुर्वन्ति भारत । कुर्यात् विद्वान् तथा असक्तः चिकीर्षुः लोकसंग्रहम् ॥
rich_grammar.pratipadarthah: सक्ताः | (saktāḥ) = attached, clinging कर्मणि | (karmaṇi) = in action अविद्वांसः | (avidvāṁsaḥ) = the ignorant, the unwise यथा | (yathā) = as, in whatever way कुर्वन्ति | (kurvanti) = they do, they act भारत | (bhārata) = O descendant of Bharata (Arjuna) कुर्यात् | (kuryāt) = should do, should act विद्वान् | (vidvān) = the wise one तथा | (tathā) = similarly, in the same way असक्तः | (asaktaḥ) = without attachment, unattached चिकीर्षुः | (cikīrṣuḥ) = desiring to do, intending लोकसंग्रहम् | (lokasaṁgraham) = welfare of the world, holding people together
rich_grammar.anvayah: भारत, अविद्वांसः कर्मणि सक्ताः यथा कुर्वन्ति, तथा विद्वान् असक्तः लोकसंग्रहं चिकीर्षुः कुर्यात् ।
rich_grammar.sandhi: कर्मणि + अविद्वांसः = कर्मण्यविद्वांसः | कुर्यात् + विद्वान् = कुर्याद्विद्वान् | तथा + असक्तः = तथासक्तः | चिकीर्षुः + लोकसंग्रहम् = चिकीर्षुर्लोकसंग्रहम्
rich_grammar.samasa: अविद्वांसः = न विद्वांसः Samāsa sense: नञ्-तत्पुरुष; the unwise, those without knowledge. | असक्तः = न सक्तः Samāsa sense: नञ्-तत्पुरुष; one who is not attached. | लोकसंग्रहम् = लोकस्य संग्रहम् Samāsa sense: षष्ठी-तत्पुरुष; the holding together / welfare of the world.
rich_grammar.other: सक्ताः is क्तप्रत्ययान्त from सञ्ज् (to be attached), प्रथमा बहुवचनम्. | कुर्वन्ति is लट्-लकारः, परस्मैपदम्, प्रथमपुरुष बहुवचनम् from कृ. | कुर्यात् is विधिलिङ्, परस्मैपदम्, प्रथमपुरुष एकवचनम् from कृ. | विद्वान् is a शतृप्रत्ययान्त (present participle) form of विद्, used as a noun meaning 'the knower, the wise one.' | चिकीर्षुः is a सन्नन्तम् (desiderative derivative) from कृ: 'one who wishes to do.' | The verse contrasts sakta/asakta and avidvān/vidvān — two pairs showing the internal difference while external action remains the same.

key_verses.final_takeaway: The wise person and the ignorant person may perform the same actions externally. The difference is entirely internal: the ignorant act with attachment, hoping results will complete them; the wise act without attachment, motivated by the welfare of the world. This is the essence of karma-yoga.

meaning.caption: Bhagavān Kṛṣṇa teaching Arjuna about the wise act without attachment, illustrating: As the ignorant act with attachment to results, O Bhārata, so should the wise act without attachment, desiring the welfare and cohesion of the world.

key_verses.title: Leading by Joyful Example
key_verses.sanskrit:
न बुद्धिभेदं(ञ्) जनयेत् अज्ञानां(ङ्) कर्मसङ्गिनाम् ।
जोषयेत्सर्वकर्माणि विद्वान्युक्तः(सः) समाचरन् ॥
key_verses.transliteration:
na buddhibhedaṁ(ñ) janayet ajñānāṁ(ṅ) karmasaṅginām ।
joṣayetsarvakarmāṇi vidvānyuktaḥ(s) samācaran ॥
key_verses.one_line_meaning: The wise one should not create confusion in the minds of the ignorant who are attached to action; rather, performing all actions with discipline and focus, he should inspire them to act joyfully.
key_verses.concise_journey: Having taught that the wise should act without attachment for lokasaṅgraha (3.25), Kṛṣṇa now addresses a practical danger: the temptation to preach renunciation to people who are not ready for it. The wise person should not create buddhibheda (confusion of understanding) in those still attached to action. Instead, he should himself perform all actions with discipline, inspiring others by example rather than by unsolicited preaching.
key_verses.meaning_detail:
This verse addresses a critical aspect of spiritual wisdom: the responsibility of the wise toward those who are still on the path. It is tempting for a person who has gained knowledge to say to others, 'Give up all this activity; it is meaningless; the Self is actionless.' But such premature teaching creates more harm than good.

Na buddhibhedaṁ janayet ajñānāṁ karmasaṅginām — the wise one should not create confusion in the minds of the ignorant who are attached to action. Buddhibheda means a splitting or confusion of understanding. When someone who naturally acts with attachment is suddenly told that action is useless, the result is not wisdom but paralysis. The person loses faith in action without gaining the maturity for jñāna. He becomes like someone who lets go of one branch before grasping another — he falls.

Śaṅkarācārya explains this clearly: the ajñānī who is still attached to karma (karmasaṅgī) needs to continue acting. His mind is not yet purified enough for renunciation. Telling him to give up action prematurely would remove his only means of growth. It is like telling a sick person to stop taking medicine because 'health is your nature.' True — but the medicine is still needed right now.

Joṣayet sarvakarmāṇi vidvān yuktaḥ samācaran — the wise one, being disciplined, should himself perform all actions and thereby inspire others to act with joy. Joṣayet is a causative from juṣ (to enjoy, to be pleased): 'should cause them to enjoy.' The wise person should make action attractive, not repulsive. By performing duties cheerfully and with full engagement, he shows that action can be a joyful offering rather than a dreary obligation.

Yuktaḥ means 'disciplined, integrated, focused.' Samācaran means 'practicing properly, performing with attention.' The wise person does not act carelessly or half-heartedly. He brings his full presence to every action. This itself becomes the teaching — no sermon is needed.

The verse has profound implications for parents, teachers, and leaders. The best way to inspire good behavior is not to lecture but to demonstrate. Children learn more from watching their parents than from hearing their instructions. Students learn more from a teacher's conduct than from their words. The wise person leads by living the teaching, not by imposing it on others.
key_verses.story:
Kṛṣṇa Teaches Through His Own Actions at Kurukṣetra (Mahābhārata)
Throughout the Mahābhārata, Kṛṣṇa teaches primarily by example rather than by preaching. At Kurukṣetra, He chose the role of Arjuna's charioteer — a position of service, not command. He did not demand the role of general or king. He took on a practical duty and performed it with complete attention.
As a charioteer, Kṛṣṇa managed the horses, positioned the chariot strategically, and served as Arjuna's closest companion in the most intense moments of the war. He did not sit on the sideline giving instructions from afar. He entered the field of action Himself.
When Arjuna collapsed in confusion, Kṛṣṇa did not immediately say, 'The Self is actionless, so give up fighting.' That would have been buddhibheda — confusion of the mind that was not ready for such teaching. Instead, He began with what Arjuna could understand: duty (svadharma), honor (kṣātra-dharma), and the practical consequences of inaction.
Only gradually, as Arjuna's mind became receptive, did Kṛṣṇa introduce deeper teachings: karma-yoga, jñāna-yoga, and finally the vision of the Self as actionless and eternal. The entire Gītā unfolds as a graded teaching, meeting the student where he is.
Kṛṣṇa's approach at Kurukṣetra is the living commentary on 3.26. He did not create confusion by dumping the highest teaching on an unprepared mind. He inspired action by performing action Himself, and He gradually elevated Arjuna's understanding through disciplined, joyful engagement.
key_verses.real_life_example:
A father wants his children to read books instead of spending hours on screens. He has two choices. He can lecture them daily: 'Reading is important! Screens are bad! You must read for one hour every day!' This approach often backfires — children resist being told what to do and may develop a negative attitude toward reading.

Or the father can simply sit down every evening with a book. He reads with visible enjoyment. He occasionally shares an interesting fact or story from what he is reading. He keeps books around the house. He takes the children to bookshops and libraries as fun outings, not as punishments.

Over time, the children notice. They see that reading is something their father enjoys — not a chore he imposes. Curiosity grows naturally. One child picks up a book. Another asks, 'What are you reading, Papa?' The reading habit develops without force or confusion.

This is the teaching of 3.26. The father did not create buddhibheda by preaching. He inspired by example. He performed the action himself (samācaran) with discipline and joy (yuktaḥ), and the children naturally wanted to follow.

The same principle applies in workplaces. A manager who preaches teamwork but works in isolation creates confusion. A manager who genuinely collaborates, listens, and shares credit inspires the team naturally. The best leadership is lived, not lectured.
key_verses.reflection:
Am I tempted to preach or advise people who are not ready for the teaching I want to share?
Do I practice what I believe before expecting others to follow?
Can I inspire people through my own joyful engagement rather than through criticism of their current level?

kids_content.explanation_script:
Kṛṣṇa gives a very wise tip here: Do not confuse people by telling them things they are not ready to hear. Instead, show them by your own example.
Imagine your little brother is just learning to write. You know how to write beautifully because you have been practicing for years. Should you say, 'Writing is easy! Why are you so slow?' That would make him feel bad and confused.
Instead, you can sit next to him and write carefully yourself. He watches you. He sees how you hold the pencil, how you form the letters. Slowly, he learns by watching.
Kṛṣṇa says the wise person should do good actions happily. When others see that, they feel inspired. They think, 'If that person can do it with a smile, I can try too!'
kids_content.story:
Amma's Kitchen
Riya's grandmother, Amma, made the best rotis in the family. They were round, soft, and perfectly puffed. Everyone loved them.
One day, little Riya wanted to learn. Amma did not start by giving a lecture: 'You must knead the dough for exactly five minutes, then roll with even pressure, then cook on medium heat...' That would have confused Riya.
Instead, Amma said, 'Come, sit with me.' She started making rotis, humming a song. Riya watched. She saw how Amma pressed the dough gently, how she rolled in smooth circles, how she flipped the roti at just the right time.
'Can I try?' Riya asked. Amma gave her a small ball of dough. Riya's first roti was a funny shape — more like a map of India than a circle. Amma laughed gently. 'That is a beautiful first roti. My first one looked like a cloud!'
Riya tried again the next day. And the next. Each time, watching Amma and practicing. After a week, her rotis were almost round.
Amma taught without confusing, without criticizing, and without lecturing. She simply did the action with love, and Riya learned naturally.
Kṛṣṇa says this is how the wise should teach: not by confusing others, but by joyfully doing the action and letting others be inspired.
kids_content.reflection:
Have I ever felt confused when someone told me something I was not ready for?
Can I help someone learn by showing them how to do it, instead of just telling them?

key_verses.detailed_meaning:
Step 1 — Do not create confusion (na buddhibhedaṁ janayet):
Buddhibhedam is a compound: buddhi (understanding) + bheda (splitting, confusion). Janayet is the causative optative from jan (to produce): 'should not cause to arise.' The wise person must not create intellectual confusion in others by teaching that is premature or inappropriate for their level.

Step 2 — Of the ignorant who are attached to action (ajñānāṁ karmasaṅginām):
Ajñānām is the genitive plural: 'of the ignorant.' Karmasaṅginām means 'of those attached to action.' These are people whose minds are still driven by desire and attachment. They are not ready for renunciation. Their growth happens through action, not through its abandonment.

Step 3 — The wise one should inspire joyful action (joṣayet sarvakarmāṇi):
Joṣayet is a causative form from juṣ (to enjoy, to delight in): 'should cause them to enjoy.' Sarvakarmāṇi means 'all actions.' The wise person should make all actions — duties, rituals, social responsibilities — attractive and inspiring. Action should feel like a joyful offering, not a burden.

Step 4 — Being disciplined and performing properly (vidvān yuktaḥ samācaran):
Vidvān means 'the wise one, the knower.' Yuktaḥ means 'disciplined, integrated, focused.' Samācaran is the present participle from sam + ā + car: 'practicing properly, performing with full attention.' The wise person does not act carelessly. He brings excellence and presence to every action.

Step 5 — The method of teaching is example, not preaching:
The verse gives no instruction to preach, lecture, or impose. The method is samācaran — personal practice. The wise person's own disciplined, joyful engagement with action is the teaching. Others are inspired not by words but by the visible example of a life lived with integrity and freedom.

Step 6 — The danger of premature renunciation teaching:
Śaṅkarācārya warns that telling a karmasaṅgī to renounce action is like removing medicine from a sick person. The ajñānī needs karma-yoga as preparation for jñāna-yoga. Disrupting this process with premature advice about actionlessness creates confusion, doubt, and spiritual paralysis.
key_verses.more_stories:
1. Śrī Rāmakṛṣṇa Teaches Different Students Differently (Modern Vedāntic Tradition)
Śrī Rāmakṛṣṇa Paramahaṁsa was known for teaching each student according to their readiness. When a devotee attached to ritual worship came to him, he did not say, 'All forms are illusion; give up worship.' Instead, he encouraged the devotee to worship with greater love and attention.
When a more mature seeker came, ready for deeper inquiry, Rāmakṛṣṇa spoke of the formless Brahman, the unreality of names and forms, and the identity of ātman and Brahman.
He once compared himself to a physician who prescribes different medicines to different patients. The same medicine does not suit everyone. A teaching that liberates one person may confuse another.
This sensitivity is exactly what 3.26 teaches. The wise person does not broadcast a single message to all. He recognizes where each person stands and supports their growth from that point — never creating buddhibheda by imposing teachings beyond their current capacity.

2. Droṇa Teaches Archery by Demonstration (Mahābhārata)
Droṇācārya was the supreme teacher of martial arts to the Kuru princes. When he taught, he did not merely lecture about technique. He demonstrated. He drew the bow himself. He hit the target himself. His students watched, learned, and were inspired to practice.
The famous test with the wooden bird illustrates his method. Droṇa asked each prince what they saw. Most described the tree, the branch, the leaves, the sky. Only Arjuna said, 'I see only the eye of the bird.' Droṇa said, 'Release your arrow.' Arjuna hit the mark.
Droṇa did not confuse the students by giving them a teaching beyond their level. He did not lecture about concentration in abstract philosophical terms. He created a situation where focus was tested through action, and the teaching emerged naturally.
This is the approach of 3.26. The teacher does not dump wisdom on unready minds. He performs action with discipline, creates the right environment, and lets the student grow at their own pace.

3. A Village Teacher Changes Her School Through Example (Contemporary)
In a small village school, a new teacher arrived. The school was in poor shape — students were irregular, parents were indifferent, and the building was crumbling. The previous teachers had tried lecturing the community about the importance of education. Nothing changed.
The new teacher took a different approach. She arrived early every day, cleaned the classroom herself, decorated it with students' artwork, and made lessons interesting with stories and activities. She did not lecture parents about their neglect.
Slowly, attendance improved. Students who had been truant started showing up because they enjoyed being in her class. Parents noticed their children's enthusiasm and began visiting the school. The community started taking pride in the school.
The teacher changed the school not by creating buddhibheda — confusing people with speeches they were not ready for — but by joṣayet — making the work itself attractive through her own joyful, disciplined example.

4. Nachiketa's Father Teaches by Performance of Yajña (Kaṭha Upaniṣad)
In the Kaṭha Upaniṣad, young Nachiketa's father Vājaśravasa performed a yajña in which he gave away all his possessions. But Nachiketa noticed that the cows being given away were old, barren, and useless — the gift was not genuine.
Nachiketa had learned the spirit of yajña by watching his father perform rituals throughout his life. But when the performance fell short of its own standard, the boy noticed the gap between teaching and practice.
This story shows the reverse of 3.26: when the elder's actions do not match the teaching, the young mind is confused — buddhibheda arises naturally. The lesson is clear: the wise person must practice what he teaches. Any gap between words and actions creates the very confusion this verse warns against.

5. Ādī Śaṅkarācārya Establishes Maṭhas for Practical Teaching (Vedāntic Tradition)
After attaining Self-knowledge at a young age, Śaṅkarācārya could have remained in silent contemplation. Instead, he traveled across India, debated with scholars of various schools, wrote commentaries on the Upaniṣads, the Gītā, and the Brahma Sūtras, and established four maṭhas (monasteries) at the four corners of India.
His action was not for personal gain — he was a saṁnyāsī with no possessions. It was for lokasaṅgraha — preserving the teaching tradition, training future teachers, and ensuring that Vedāntic knowledge would be available to seekers in every region.
Śaṅkara did not merely preach renunciation. He organized institutional structures, trained disciples, and performed action with extraordinary discipline. His life was a living demonstration of 3.26: the wise one performs all actions with focus and engagement, inspiring others by the sheer integrity of his example.

rich_grammar.padacchedah: न बुद्धिभेदम् जनयेत् अज्ञानाम् कर्मसङ्गिनाम् । जोषयेत् सर्वकर्माणि विद्वान् युक्तः समाचरन् ॥
rich_grammar.pratipadarthah: न | (na) = not बुद्धिभेदम् | (buddhibhedam) = confusion of understanding जनयेत् | (janayet) = should produce, should cause अज्ञानाम् | (ajñānām) = of the ignorant कर्मसङ्गिनाम् | (karmasaṅginām) = of those attached to action जोषयेत् | (joṣayet) = should cause to enjoy, should inspire सर्वकर्माणि | (sarvakarmāṇi) = all actions विद्वान् | (vidvān) = the wise one युक्तः | (yuktaḥ) = disciplined, focused समाचरन् | (samācaran) = performing properly, practicing with attention
rich_grammar.anvayah: विद्वान् अज्ञानाम् कर्मसङ्गिनाम् बुद्धिभेदं न जनयेत् । युक्तः समाचरन् सर्वकर्माणि जोषयेत् ।
rich_grammar.sandhi: जनयेत् + अज्ञानाम् = जनयेदज्ञानाम् | जोषयेत् + सर्वकर्माणि = जोषयेत्सर्वकर्माणि | विद्वान् + युक्तः = विद्वान्युक्तः
rich_grammar.samasa: बुद्धिभेदम् = बुद्धेः भेदम् Samāsa sense: षष्ठी-तत्पुरुष; the splitting/confusion of understanding. | कर्मसङ्गिनाम् = कर्मणि सक्ताः ये ते तेषाम् Samāsa sense: सप्तमी-तत्पुरुष/बहुव्रीहि; of those who are attached to action. | सर्वकर्माणि = सर्वाणि कर्माणि Samāsa sense: कर्मधारय; all actions.
rich_grammar.other: जनयेत् is णिजन्त विधिलिङ्, परस्मैपदम्, प्रथमपुरुष एकवचनम् from जन् (causative: to produce). | जोषयेत् is णिजन्त विधिलिङ्, परस्मैपदम्, प्रथमपुरुष एकवचनम् from जुष् (causative: to cause to enjoy/delight in). | समाचरन् is शतृप्रत्ययान्त (present active participle) from सम् + आ + चर्: 'while performing properly.' | विद्वान् is the subject of both clauses: the wise one should not create confusion AND should inspire joyful action. | The verse has two instructions: a negative one (do not confuse) and a positive one (inspire by example).

key_verses.final_takeaway: The wise person must not confuse the minds of those who are still attached to action by preaching premature renunciation. Instead, by performing all duties himself with discipline and joy, he inspires others naturally. The best teaching is a lived example, not an imposed sermon.

key_verses.title: The Delusion of Doership
key_verses.sanskrit:
प्रकृतेः(ख्) क्रियमाणानि गुणैः(ख्) कर्माणि सर्वशः ।
अहङ्कारविमूढात्मा कर्ताहमिति मन्यते ॥
key_verses.transliteration:
prakṛteḥ(kh) kriyamāṇāni guṇaiḥ(kh) karmāṇi sarvaśaḥ ।
ahaṅkāravimūḍhātmā kartāhamiti manyate ॥
key_verses.one_line_meaning: All actions are performed in every way by the guṇas of prakṛti; but the one whose mind is deluded by ahaṅkāra (ego) thinks 'I am the doer.'
key_verses.concise_journey: Bhagavān Kṛṣṇa now introduces one of the most important Vedāntic teachings in the Gītā: the Self (ātman) is not the doer. All actions — physical, mental, and emotional — are performed by the three guṇas (sattva, rajas, tamas) of prakṛti (material nature). But the person deluded by ahaṅkāra (ego-sense) falsely identifies with the body-mind and thinks, 'I am doing this.' This verse begins the transition from karma-yoga instruction to the deeper Vedāntic vision of ātman as akartā (non-doer).
key_verses.meaning_detail:
This verse marks a pivotal moment in Chapter 3. Until now, Kṛṣṇa has been teaching karma-yoga — act, but without attachment, for lokasaṅgraha. Now He reveals the deeper reason why attachment to action creates bondage: it is based on a fundamental error about who the doer is.

Prakṛteḥ kriyamāṇāni guṇaiḥ karmāṇi sarvaśaḥ — all actions are being performed in every way by the guṇas of prakṛti. Prakṛti is the material principle — the body, senses, mind, intellect, and prāṇa. It functions through three fundamental modes (guṇas): sattva (clarity, knowledge), rajas (activity, passion), and tamas (inertia, dullness). Every action — seeing, hearing, thinking, walking, speaking, digesting — is a function of prakṛti. The Self (ātman) does nothing.

Śaṅkarācārya explains: just as the sun illuminates the world but does not participate in worldly activities, the ātman illuminates (makes conscious) the activities of the body-mind but does not perform them. The body moves, the mind thinks, the senses perceive — all of this is prakṛti's domain. The ātman is the witness (sākṣī), the consciousness in whose presence all this happens.

Ahaṅkāravimūḍhātmā kartā aham iti manyate — the one whose mind is deluded by ego thinks 'I am the doer.' Ahaṅkāra is the ego-function — the sense of 'I' that identifies with the body-mind. Vimūḍha means 'thoroughly deluded.' When the ātman (pure consciousness) is confused with the body-mind (prakṛti), the result is the false notion: 'I am doing this. I am the doer. I am responsible for results.'

This is the fundamental error (adhyāsa) that Śaṅkarācārya describes in his introduction to the Brahma Sūtras: the superimposition of the properties of the not-Self (body, mind, action) upon the Self (consciousness, witness, non-doer). This superimposition is beginningless (anādi) and is the root of all saṁsāra.

The teaching is not that action should be avoided. The teaching is that the sense of personal doership — 'I did this, I achieved this, I failed at this' — is a misunderstanding. When this misunderstanding is corrected through Self-knowledge, the person continues to act but without the burden of ego-driven ownership.

This verse prepares the ground for 3.28, which describes how the tattvavit (knower of truth) sees action differently and remains unattached.
key_verses.story:
Arjuna's Confusion About Doership Before the War (Mahābhārata)
Before the Gītā was spoken, Arjuna stood on his chariot and looked at the armies assembled for war. He saw his teachers, grandfathers, uncles, brothers, sons, and friends on both sides. His body trembled, his bow slipped, and his mouth went dry.
Arjuna said, 'I will not fight. I would rather beg for food than kill my elders. The sin of killing them will cling to me. I will be the destroyer of my own family.' His entire argument was based on one assumption: 'I am the doer. If I fight, I am the killer. The sin belongs to me.'
This is exactly the ahaṅkāra-vimūḍha-ātmā that 3.27 describes. Arjuna identified completely with the role of warrior, the body that would wield the bow, and the social identity that would bear the consequences. He could not see beyond the ego.
Kṛṣṇa's response in Chapter 2 already hinted at the solution: 'The Self does not kill, nor is it killed' (2.19). Now in 3.27, He states the principle more broadly: all actions belong to prakṛti's guṇas. The Self is not the actor.
Arjuna's crisis was not about the ethics of war alone. It was about the fundamental question: who acts? If Arjuna understood that the body-mind acts through the guṇas, and the Self remains the untouched witness, his paralysis would dissolve. He would act because action is the nature of prakṛti, while knowing that the Self is free from action.
This understanding does not make a person irresponsible. It frees the person from the emotional burden of ego-driven ownership, allowing action to flow naturally, appropriately, and without the distortion of 'I am the doer.'
key_verses.real_life_example:
Think about what happens when you eat food. The teeth chew, the tongue tastes, the stomach digests, the intestines absorb nutrients, and the blood carries nourishment to every cell. You did not consciously direct any of these processes. They happen automatically through the body's systems — which is prakṛti.

Yet we say, 'I ate lunch. I digested the food.' This is ahaṅkāra — the ego claiming ownership of what the body does automatically. The same applies to seeing, hearing, breathing, and thinking. The eyes see because of their biological structure. The ears hear because of their design. Thoughts arise based on stored impressions (saṁskāras) and the current state of the guṇas.

Now extend this to larger actions. When someone works on a project, many factors contribute: education (received from teachers and parents), health (maintained by the body's systems), intelligence (a function of sattva-guṇa), motivation (rajas), rest (tamas), tools (created by others), and support (from colleagues and family). The 'I' that claims, 'I accomplished this alone' is overlooking the vast network of causes.

This does not mean effort is meaningless. It means the ego's claim to sole ownership of results is inflated. A wiser approach is to act with full engagement while recognizing that action flows through a complex system of causes. This recognition reduces arrogance in success and despair in failure — because neither belongs to the Self.

In daily life, this teaching translates to humility and gratitude. When things go well: 'Many factors aligned — I am grateful.' When things go poorly: 'Many factors were at play — I will learn and adjust.' This is the freedom that comes from understanding 3.27.
key_verses.reflection:
When I accomplish something, do I tend to think 'I did this entirely on my own,' or can I see the many factors that contributed?
When something goes wrong, do I feel crushed because 'I failed,' or can I see that the result depended on factors beyond my personal control?
Can I begin to observe the body and mind acting, while recognizing that the witnessing consciousness is not the actor?

kids_content.explanation_script:
Kṛṣṇa explains something very deep here. He says that the body does all the actions — walking, talking, eating, thinking — but the real 'You' inside is not doing any of it!
Think about it: do you tell your heart to beat? No, it beats on its own. Do you tell your stomach to digest food? No, it happens automatically. Do you tell your eyes to see? No, they see by themselves.
All these actions happen because of the body, which Kṛṣṇa calls 'prakṛti.' But we often say, 'I did this! I won! I made this!' The 'I' feeling is called ahaṅkāra — ego. It makes us think we are the doer of everything.
Kṛṣṇa says a wise person understands: the body acts, but the real Self inside is like a peaceful light that watches everything.
kids_content.story:
The Puppet and the Light
In a village, there was a puppeteer who put on a wonderful show every evening. He had colourful puppets — a king, a queen, a soldier, and a clown. He moved the strings, and the puppets danced, fought, laughed, and cried.
The children loved the show. One small boy, Kiran, was so fascinated that he shouted, 'The king is so brave! He is fighting the dragon!'
Kiran's grandfather smiled. After the show, he took Kiran behind the stage. Kiran saw the puppeteer sitting quietly, moving the strings. The puppets were hanging limply.
'Thatha, the king is not moving by himself?' Kiran asked.
'No,' said grandfather. 'The strings make him move. Without the puppeteer, the puppets are just cloth and wood.'
Kiran looked at the still puppets. 'But during the show, it felt so real! Like the king was actually doing everything.'
Grandfather nodded. 'That is what Kṛṣṇa teaches. Our body is like the puppet. The three forces of nature — energy, rest, and clarity — are like the strings. The real 'You' is the one who watches the show, like the light that makes the whole stage visible. The light does not move the puppets, but without the light, nobody can see anything.'
Kṛṣṇa says the body does all actions, but we often forget and think, 'I am the puppet! I am moving!' When we remember that we are the watching light, we feel free.
kids_content.reflection:
Can I think of something my body does all by itself, without me telling it to?
When I say 'I did it!', who exactly is the 'I' — is it my body, my mind, or something deeper?

key_verses.detailed_meaning:
Step 1 — All actions belong to prakṛti (prakṛteḥ kriyamāṇāni):
Prakṛteḥ is the genitive of prakṛti: 'of/by prakṛti.' Kriyamāṇāni is the present passive participle of kṛ: 'being done, being performed.' All actions — physical, verbal, and mental — belong to prakṛti, the material principle that includes body, senses, mind, and intellect. The Self (ātman) is not the agent of any action.

Step 2 — By the guṇas (guṇaiḥ):
Guṇaiḥ is the instrumental plural: 'by the guṇas.' The three guṇas — sattva, rajas, and tamas — are the fundamental modes of prakṛti. Sattva produces clarity and knowledge. Rajas produces activity and desire. Tamas produces inertia and delusion. All actions arise from the interplay of these three forces. There is no action that exists outside the guṇa framework.

Step 3 — In every way (sarvaśaḥ):
Sarvaśaḥ means 'in every way, entirely, without exception.' No action of any kind — perception, locomotion, speech, thought, emotion, digestion — is performed by the Self. The word emphasizes the completeness of this teaching: not even a single action belongs to the ātman.

Step 4 — The ego-deluded person (ahaṅkāravimūḍhātmā):
Ahaṅkāravimūḍhātmā is a compound: ahaṅkāra (ego) + vimūḍha (thoroughly deluded) + ātmā (self, mind). The one whose mind is thoroughly deluded by the ego-sense. Ahaṅkāra is the function that creates the sense of 'I' and 'mine' by identifying consciousness with the body-mind.

Step 5 — The false claim of doership (kartā aham iti manyate):
Kartā means 'doer.' Aham means 'I.' Iti means 'thus.' Manyate means 'thinks, believes.' The deluded person thinks: 'I am the doer.' This is the fundamental error — attributing the actions of prakṛti to the Self. This error is the root of bondage, because the ego then takes ownership of results, leading to pride, anxiety, guilt, and suffering.

Step 6 — The Advaitic teaching of akartṛtva:
Śaṅkarācārya identifies this verse as teaching akartṛtva — the non-doership of the Self. The ātman is pure consciousness (cit), changeless (nirvikāra), and actionless (niṣkriya). Action belongs to the body-mind (prakṛti). The confusion between the two is adhyāsa (superimposition). Self-knowledge removes this confusion, revealing the Self as ever-free.

Step 7 — Practical implication for the seeker:
The teaching does not eliminate action. It eliminates the ego's claim to action. The seeker continues to act through karma-yoga but gradually recognizes: 'The body acts, the mind thinks, the senses perceive — but I, the witness, am free from all of this.' This recognition is the beginning of liberation.
key_verses.more_stories:
1. The Story of the Tenth Man (Vedāntic Teaching Story)
Ten students once crossed a river. After reaching the other side, they decided to count themselves to make sure everyone had crossed safely. The leader counted: one, two, three... nine. He counted again: still nine. Panic arose. 'One of us has drowned!' they cried.
A passing traveller asked what happened. The leader explained. The traveller smiled and said, 'Please count again.' The leader counted the others and got nine. 'You forgot to count yourself,' the traveller said. 'You are the tenth man.'
The 'missing' person was never lost. He was always there — but was overlooked because the counter forgot himself. Similarly, the ātman is always present as the witness of all action. But the ego 'forgets' the Self and identifies only with the body-mind, claiming doership.
The teaching of 3.27 is like the traveller's correction: 'You are not the doer. The doer is prakṛti. You are the witness who was always present but overlooked.'

2. Śvetaketu Learns 'Tat Tvam Asi' (Chāndogya Upaniṣad)
Śvetaketu returned from twelve years of Vedic study, proud of his learning. His father Uddālaka Āruṇi asked him, 'Do you know that by knowing which everything else is known?'
Śvetaketu did not know. His father then taught him through a series of examples — salt dissolving in water, the essence in a seed, rivers merging into the ocean — culminating in the mahāvākya: 'Tat tvam asi' — 'You are That.'
Śvetaketu's initial pride was ahaṅkāra — the ego claiming ownership of learning. 'I have studied. I know so much.' But true knowledge revealed that the Self is not the accumulation of learning; it is the consciousness in whose light all learning happens.
This story illuminates 3.27. The ego thinks, 'I learned this, I know this, I did this.' But the Self is not the learner or the doer — it is the awareness that makes all learning and doing possible.

3. King Bharata and the Deer (Bhāgavata Purāṇa)
King Bharata renounced his kingdom and retired to the forest for spiritual practice. He was progressing well until he became attached to a young deer that he rescued. His mind became so focused on the deer that he neglected his sādhana and eventually died thinking of the deer, leading to rebirth as a deer.
In a subsequent birth as Jaḍa Bharata, he finally attained Self-knowledge and recognized the error: it was not the Self that had become attached — it was the mind (prakṛti) that had created attachment. The Self had always been the witness, untouched.
The story shows how ahaṅkāra works. When Bharata identified with the thought 'I love this deer, I must protect it,' the ego claimed ownership of a mental tendency (vṛtti). The guṇas of prakṛti produced the attachment; the ego said, 'I am attached.' This is the delusion described in 3.27.

4. Draupadī Surrenders Doership to Kṛṣṇa (Mahābhārata)
When Duḥśāsana tried to disrobe Draupadī in the Kuru court, she initially tried to hold her sari with her own hands. She struggled, fought, and tried to protect herself through her own effort. But the more she struggled, the more helpless she felt.
Finally, in utter surrender, she raised both hands and called upon Kṛṣṇa. She let go of the claim, 'I am the protector of my honour. I am the doer of my protection.' In that moment of releasing ahaṅkāra, divine protection manifested. The sari became endless, and Duḥśāsana could not complete his act.
This episode, though it operates at the level of bhakti, illustrates the principle of 3.27. As long as Draupadī clung to the ego-driven thought, 'I must do this myself,' she was limited by prakṛti's capacity. When she released the sense of doership, a greater power operated.

5. Ramaṇa Maharṣi Demonstrates Actionless Awareness (Modern Vedāntic Tradition)
Ramaṇa Maharṣi lived at Arunācala for decades. Devotees came with problems, questions, and spiritual crises. Sometimes he spoke. Sometimes he remained silent. Sometimes he simply looked at the questioner, and the question dissolved.
A devotee once asked, 'Bhagavan, how do you help people?' Ramaṇa replied, 'I do nothing. The Self does nothing. Whatever happens, happens through prakṛti.'
This was not false modesty. Ramaṇa genuinely did not experience doership. The body taught, the mind responded to questions, the voice spoke — but Ramaṇa as the Self was the witness of all this, not the actor.
Visitors sometimes marveled at how 'effortless' his teaching was. It was effortless because there was no ego claiming, 'I am teaching.' The guṇas of sattva operated through a purified body-mind, and the Self remained as the silent awareness.
This is the living demonstration of 3.27: all action belongs to prakṛti; the Self is the non-doer. The one who knows this is free from the burden of 'I am the doer.'

rich_grammar.padacchedah: प्रकृतेः क्रियमाणानि गुणैः कर्माणि सर्वशः । अहङ्कारविमूढात्मा कर्ता अहम् इति मन्यते ॥
rich_grammar.pratipadarthah: प्रकृतेः | (prakṛteḥ) = of/by prakṛti (material nature) क्रियमाणानि | (kriyamāṇāni) = being performed, being done गुणैः | (guṇaiḥ) = by the guṇas (sattva, rajas, tamas) कर्माणि | (karmāṇi) = actions सर्वशः | (sarvaśaḥ) = in every way, entirely अहङ्कारविमूढात्मा | (ahaṅkāravimūḍhātmā) = one whose mind is deluded by ego कर्ता | (kartā) = doer अहम् | (aham) = I इति | (iti) = thus मन्यते | (manyate) = thinks, believes
rich_grammar.anvayah: कर्माणि सर्वशः प्रकृतेः गुणैः क्रियमाणानि । अहङ्कारविमूढात्मा 'अहं कर्ता' इति मन्यते ।
rich_grammar.sandhi: अहङ्कारविमूढात्मा = अहङ्कार + विमूढ + आत्मा (compound, no external sandhi) | कर्ता + अहम् = कर्ताहम् | अहम् + इति = अहमिति
rich_grammar.samasa: अहङ्कारविमूढात्मा = अहङ्कारेण विमूढः आत्मा यस्य सः Samāsa sense: तृतीया-तत्पुरुष + बहुव्रीहि; one whose mind/self is deluded by ego. | क्रियमाणानि = कर्मणि-प्रत्ययान्त present passive participle; 'being performed' — not a compound but a verbal form.
rich_grammar.other: क्रियमाणानि is कर्मणि-प्रत्ययान्त (present passive participle, शानच्) from कृ, नपुंसकलिङ्ग, प्रथमा बहुवचनम्: 'being done.' | गुणैः is तृतीया बहुवचनम्, indicating the instrumental agent: 'by the guṇas.' | प्रकृतेः is षष्ठी एकवचनम्, indicating the source: 'of prakṛti.' | सर्वशः is an indeclinable meaning 'in every way.' | मन्यते is लट्-लकारः, आत्मनेपदम्, प्रथमपुरुष एकवचनम् from मन् (to think). | The verse has two clauses: (1) a factual statement — actions are performed by guṇas of prakṛti; (2) the error — the ego-deluded person claims doership. The contrast between fact and error is the core teaching.

key_verses.final_takeaway: All actions — without exception — are performed by the guṇas of prakṛti (material nature). The Self is the non-doer, the witness. The ego-deluded person falsely claims, 'I am the doer,' and this false claim is the root of all bondage. Recognizing the Self as actionless is the beginning of liberation.
