key_verses.title: The Vision of the Knower of Truth
key_verses.sanskrit:
तत्त्ववित्तु महाबाहो गुणकर्मविभागयोः ।
गुणा गुणेषु वर्तन्त इति मत्वा न सज्जते ॥
key_verses.transliteration:
tattvavittu mahābāho guṇakarmavibhāgayoḥ ।
guṇā guṇeṣu vartanta iti matvā na sajjate ॥
key_verses.one_line_meaning: But the knower of truth, O mighty-armed, understanding the distinction between the guṇas and their actions, knows that 'guṇas operate among guṇas' and does not become attached.
key_verses.concise_journey: Having described the delusion of the ignorant person in 3.27 — who thinks 'I am the doer' — Kṛṣṇa now describes the vision of the tattvavit, the knower of truth. Such a person understands the division (vibhāga) between the guṇas and their actions. He sees that all activity is simply guṇas interacting with guṇas — senses (which are guṇa-products) operating among sense-objects (which are also guṇa-products). The Self remains untouched. With this understanding, the tattvavit does not become attached.
key_verses.meaning_detail:
This verse is the companion and contrast to 3.27. There, the ignorant person's delusion was described. Here, the wise person's clarity is described. Together, they form a complete teaching about doership and freedom.

Tattvavit tu — 'but the knower of truth.' The word tu marks a strong contrast with the previous verse. The ahaṅkāra-vimūḍha-ātmā of 3.27 thinks 'I am the doer.' The tattvavit of 3.28 knows the truth: the Self is not the doer. Tattva means 'truth, reality, the real nature of things.' Vit means 'one who knows.' The tattvavit has understood the fundamental distinction between the Self (ātman) and the not-Self (anātman / prakṛti).

Guṇakarmavibhāgayoḥ — 'of the division of guṇas and actions.' This compound refers to the two categories: guṇas (the three modes of prakṛti — sattva, rajas, tamas) and karmas (actions that arise from these guṇas). The tattvavit understands that both the guṇas and their actions belong to prakṛti. The Self is separate from both.

Guṇā guṇeṣu vartante — 'guṇas operate among guṇas.' This is the key insight. Śaṅkarācārya explains: the senses (which are products of the guṇas) operate among the sense-objects (which are also products of the guṇas). The eye (a guṇa-product) sees colour (a guṇa-product). The ear (a guṇa-product) hears sound (a guṇa-product). The mind (a guṇa-product) processes thoughts (which are guṇa-products). At no point does the Self enter the picture as an actor. The Self is the witness-consciousness in whose light all this activity takes place.

Iti matvā na sajjate — 'knowing this, he does not become attached.' Matvā is the absolutive of man (to think, to understand): 'having understood.' Sajjate is from sañj (to cling, to be attached): 'does not become attached.' The tattvavit continues to live in the world. His senses still perceive, his mind still thinks, his body still acts. But he does not identify with these activities. He does not take personal credit for successes or personal blame for failures. He sees all activity as the play of prakṛti.

This is not a cold, detached indifference. It is clarity. The tattvavit can act with full engagement, compassion, and dedication — precisely because he is not burdened by the ego's constant calculation of 'What does this mean for me?' His action is lighter, freer, and more effective because it is not weighed down by ahaṅkāra.

From the Advaita standpoint, this verse describes the jīvanmukta — the one who is liberated while living. The body continues, the mind continues, but the identification with them has been dissolved by knowledge. The Self shines as the witness, untouched by the dance of the guṇas.
key_verses.story:
Yājñavalkya Teaches Maitreyī Before Renunciation (Bṛhadāraṇyaka Upaniṣad)
Yājñavalkya was one of the greatest Vedāntic sages. He was also a householder with two wives, wealth, cattle, and social standing. When the time came for him to renounce worldly life and enter the final stage of saṁnyāsa, he called his wife Maitreyī to divide his property between his two wives.
Maitreyī asked a penetrating question: 'If this entire earth, filled with wealth, were mine, would I become immortal through it?'
Yājñavalkya answered honestly: 'No. Your life would be like that of the wealthy, but there is no hope of immortality through wealth.'
Maitreyī then said, 'What shall I do with that which will not make me immortal? Tell me what you know about the Self.'
Yājñavalkya taught her the supreme truth: the ātman is the real Self, and everything that we consider 'mine' — body, mind, wealth, relationships — belongs to the not-Self. When the ātman is known, everything is known. When the ātman is not known, attachment persists because we search for ourselves in things that are not ourselves.
Yājñavalkya himself was a tattvavit. He had lived in the world fully — as a scholar, debater, husband, and householder. But his identification was with the Self, not with the roles. When the time came to leave, he left without inner conflict, because he was not leaving 'himself' behind. His possessions, status, and relationships were guṇas operating among guṇas. The Self was untouched.
This story illustrates 3.28 perfectly. The tattvavit sees that guṇas interact with guṇas — senses with objects, mind with thoughts, roles with responsibilities — while the Self remains the unchanging witness. With this understanding, attachment dissolves naturally.
key_verses.real_life_example:
Imagine watching a movie. While you are fully absorbed in the story, you feel what the characters feel. When the hero is in danger, your heart races. When there is a sad scene, your eyes water. When the villain is defeated, you feel relief.

But then the movie ends, and the lights come on. You stretch, smile, and walk out. Why are you not devastated by the hero's suffering? Because you know you were watching, not participating. The movie was playing on the screen; your awareness was the light that illuminated it. The drama belonged to the characters; your consciousness was untouched.

The tattvavit lives in the world like a person who has 'remembered' that life is being projected on the screen of consciousness. The senses perceive, the mind reacts, the body acts — but these are the guṇas operating among guṇas: sense-organs meeting sense-objects, thoughts meeting impressions. The Self (the awareness watching it all) is not entangled.

This does not mean the tattvavit becomes cold or unfeeling. Just as a moviegoer can deeply appreciate art without mistaking it for reality, the wise person can participate in life fully — with compassion, creativity, and care — without the ego's anxious grip of 'This defines me. This will complete me. If this fails, I am finished.'

In daily life, we can practice this by occasionally stepping back and observing: 'The eyes are seeing. The ears are hearing. The mind is thinking. But I — the one who is aware of all this — am not doing any of it.' This is the seed of the tattvavit's vision.
key_verses.reflection:
Can I occasionally observe my senses and mind in action, noticing that 'seeing is happening, thinking is happening,' without identifying as the doer of these activities?
What would change in my daily experience if I truly understood that all activity is the guṇas interacting with guṇas, and my real Self is the uninvolved witness?
Do I confuse non-attachment with indifference, or can I see that true non-attachment allows more genuine engagement, not less?

kids_content.explanation_script:
Kṛṣṇa now tells us about a very wise person — someone who really understands the truth.
Remember the puppet show from the last verse? The puppets dance because of the strings and the puppeteer. Now imagine there is a bright lamp shining on the stage. Without that lamp, nobody can see the show. But the lamp itself is not part of the show. It does not dance. It does not fight. It just shines.
Kṛṣṇa says the wise person understands: 'My body and mind are like the puppets. They move because of three forces: energy, rest, and clarity. But the real Me is like the lamp — I just shine and watch.'
When you know this, you do not worry too much. If you win a game, great — the body and mind played well. If you lose, okay — the body and mind did their best. The real You is peaceful inside, like the lamp that keeps shining no matter what happens on stage.
kids_content.story:
The River and the Bridge
In a busy town, there was an old stone bridge over a wide river. Every day, boats passed under the bridge — big boats, small boats, colourful boats, and fishing boats. Sometimes the water was calm. Sometimes it was wild with waves.
A traveller once sat on the bridge and watched. He saw boats moving, fish jumping, leaves floating, and water swirling. 'How busy the river is!' he thought.
An old wise woman was sitting nearby. She said, 'The bridge has been here for a hundred years. Boats come and go. Floods come and go. Fish come and go. But the bridge remains still.'
The traveller looked at the bridge beneath him. It was true. The bridge was strong, steady, and unmoved by whatever happened in the river.
The wise woman smiled. 'You are like the bridge. Your body and mind are like the river — always moving, always busy. But the real You, the one who watches everything, is steady like the bridge. Boats of happy thoughts come, boats of sad thoughts come, waves of excitement come, waves of tiredness come — but You, the watcher, remain peaceful.'
Kṛṣṇa says the same thing. The wise person watches the river of life flowing through the body and mind, but knows, 'I am the bridge — steady, peaceful, and untouched.'
kids_content.reflection:
Can I sit quietly for a moment and watch my own thoughts, like watching boats pass under a bridge?
When something exciting or upsetting happens, can I remember that the real Me is like the steady bridge, not like the moving water?

key_verses.detailed_meaning:
Step 1 — The knower of truth (tattvavit tu):
Tattvavit is a compound: tattva (truth, reality) + vit (knower). Tu means 'but,' marking a contrast with the ego-deluded person of 3.27. The tattvavit has understood the fundamental distinction between the Self (ātman / consciousness) and the not-Self (anātman / prakṛti / body-mind).

Step 2 — O mighty-armed (mahābāho):
Mahābāho is a vocative addressing Arjuna: 'O mighty-armed one.' This address reminds Arjuna that he is a warrior — his strength should be used with wisdom, not with ego. The epithet also conveys affection and encouragement.

Step 3 — Of the division of guṇas and actions (guṇakarmavibhāgayoḥ):
Guṇakarmavibhāgayoḥ is a compound in the genitive dual or locative sense: 'regarding the division of guṇas and karmas.' The tattvavit understands that both the guṇas (modes of prakṛti) and the karmas (actions arising from them) belong to prakṛti. The Self is distinct from both. This understanding is the knowledge (vibhāga-jñāna) that liberates.

Step 4 — Guṇas operate among guṇas (guṇā guṇeṣu vartante):
Guṇāḥ here refers to the senses and internal organs (which are products of the guṇas). Guṇeṣu refers to the sense-objects (which are also guṇa-products). Vartante means 'they operate, they move, they function.' The entire field of experience — perception, action, thought — is guṇas interacting with guṇas. The Self is not involved.

Step 5 — Having understood this (iti matvā):
Iti means 'thus.' Matvā is the absolutive of man: 'having thought, having understood.' The tattvavit has not merely intellectually grasped this idea. He has assimilated it through śravaṇa (hearing the teaching), manana (reflecting on it), and nididhyāsana (meditating on it) until it becomes firm, unshakeable knowledge.

Step 6 — Does not become attached (na sajjate):
Sajjate is from sañj (to cling, to be attached): 'does not cling.' The tattvavit does not take ownership of actions or results. He lives lightly. He may act with full dedication, but the ego's heavy hand of 'I did this, I deserve this, I fear losing this' is absent.

Step 7 — The state of jīvanmukti:
This verse describes the jīvanmukta — one who is liberated while living. The body-mind continues to function through the momentum of prārabdha-karma (past karma that has begun to fructify). But the jīvanmukta's identification is with the Self, not with the body-mind. He sees the world as a play of the guṇas and remains the uninvolved witness — free, full, and at peace.
key_verses.more_stories:
1. King Janaka Untouched While Mithilā Burns (Vedāntic Tradition)
The most famous illustration of this verse comes from King Janaka. When news reached him that Mithilā was on fire, the sages around him panicked. But Janaka said, 'Mithilānāṁ dahyamānāyāṁ na me dahyati kiñcana' — 'Even if Mithilā burns, nothing of mine burns.'
This was not callousness. Janaka would have acted to protect his people — that was his dharmic duty. But he recognized that the fire belonged to the realm of prakṛti. His body, city, and possessions were guṇa-products. The Self — his real identity — was not touched by fire.
This is the vision of 3.28. The tattvavit knows: guṇas operate among guṇas. The fire (a physical phenomenon) acts upon buildings (physical structures). The Self, being consciousness, is not a physical entity and cannot be burned. With this understanding, Janaka remained unattached — not uncaring, but unbound.

2. Sage Vasiṣṭha Teaches Rāma About the Nature of Action (Yoga Vāsiṣṭha)
In the Yoga Vāsiṣṭha, sage Vasiṣṭha teaches young prince Rāma, who has become disenchanted with the world. Rāma sees impermanence everywhere and questions the point of action.
Vasiṣṭha does not tell Rāma to abandon action. Instead, he teaches: 'Action happens. The body moves, the senses perceive, the mind thinks. But the Self — pure consciousness — is the silent witness. Identify with the witness, and action becomes free.'
Vasiṣṭha uses vivid imagery: consciousness is like the sky, and actions are like clouds passing through it. The sky is never stained by clouds, never altered by storms, never diminished by darkness. Similarly, the Self is never stained by actions performed by the body-mind.
Rāma absorbs this teaching and returns to his princely duties — not as a reluctant actor, but as a wise one who acts without identification. This is the tattvavit of 3.28.

3. Kabīr Weaves Cloth While Singing of Brahman (Sant Tradition)
Kabīr was a simple weaver in Banaras. Every day, he sat at his loom and wove cloth. His hands moved the shuttle, his feet worked the pedals, and the cloth grew inch by inch. Externally, he was a common artisan.
But Kabīr's songs reveal a different inner reality. He sang, 'The weaver weaves — but who is the weaver? The thread comes from Brahman, the loom is Brahman, the weaver is Brahman, and the cloth is Brahman.' He saw all activity as the play of one Reality expressing itself through forms.
Kabīr did not stop weaving. He did not claim the work was beneath him. He wove with full skill and attention. But he understood that the guṇas were operating among guṇas — hands among threads, mind among patterns — while the Self remained the silent awareness.
Kabīr's life is a folk illustration of 3.28. The tattvavit acts in the world fully but knows that all activity belongs to prakṛti. The Self remains untouched, like the sky behind the clouds.

4. Bhagavān Kṛṣṇa Acts Without Attachment Throughout the Mahābhārata (Mahābhārata)
Kṛṣṇa Himself is the supreme example of one who acts without attachment. Throughout the Mahābhārata, He performs an astonishing range of actions: He serves as a diplomat, negotiates peace, drives Arjuna's chariot, counsels kings, fights when necessary, and governs Dvārakā.
Yet at no point does Kṛṣṇa show the anxiety of a doer. He negotiates for peace with full effort — but when Duryodhana refuses, He does not break down. He drives the chariot with perfect skill — but does not seek praise for it. He governs Dvārakā wisely — but does not cling to kingship.
After the war, when the Pāṇḍavas are established and the purpose is served, Kṛṣṇa returns to Dvārakā without ceremony. Eventually, the Yādava dynasty itself ends, and Kṛṣṇa departs from the world without drama.
His entire life demonstrates the principle of 3.28: guṇas operate among guṇas. The body-mind engages with the world — but the Self, ever-free, remains untouched. Kṛṣṇa's life is the living commentary on this verse.

5. Nisargadatta Maharaj Runs a Shop While Abiding as Awareness (Modern Vedāntic Tradition)
Nisargadatta Maharaj lived in a small flat in Mumbai, running a bīḍī (hand-rolled cigarette) shop. Seekers from around the world came to sit with him in his tiny room above the shop. The contrast was striking: a man in a cramped urban space, selling cigarettes, who spoke about the Absolute with the authority of the Upaniṣads.
Visitors sometimes asked, 'How can you sell bīḍīs and also be Self-realized?' Nisargadatta would smile. 'The shop runs. The body sits here. Words come out. But I am not the shopkeeper, not the speaker, not the body. I am the awareness in which all this appears.'
He often said, 'I am not the doer. Things happen through this body-mind, but I know I am not this body-mind.' This is exactly 'guṇā guṇeṣu vartante' — guṇas operate among guṇas.
Nisargadatta did not need to close his shop to be free. He did not need to move to a cave or wear special clothes. His freedom was internal — the clear knowledge that the Self is not the doer. This modern example shows that the teaching of 3.28 is not abstract philosophy but a living reality accessible in any context.

rich_grammar.padacchedah: तत्त्ववित् तु महाबाहो गुणकर्मविभागयोः । गुणाः गुणेषु वर्तन्ते इति मत्वा न सज्जते ॥
rich_grammar.pratipadarthah: तत्त्ववित् | (tattvavit) = knower of truth तु | (tu) = but, however महाबाहो | (mahābāho) = O mighty-armed one (Arjuna) गुणकर्मविभागयोः | (guṇakarmavibhāgayoḥ) = of the division of guṇas and actions गुणाः | (guṇāḥ) = guṇas (senses, which are guṇa-products) गुणेषु | (guṇeṣu) = among guṇas (sense-objects, which are guṇa-products) वर्तन्ते | (vartante) = operate, function, move इति | (iti) = thus मत्वा | (matvā) = having understood, having realized न | (na) = not सज्जते | (sajjate) = becomes attached, clings
rich_grammar.anvayah: महाबाहो, गुणकर्मविभागयोः तत्त्ववित् तु 'गुणाः गुणेषु वर्तन्ते' इति मत्वा न सज्जते ।
rich_grammar.sandhi: तत्त्ववित् + तु = तत्त्ववित्तु | गुणाः + गुणेषु = guṇā guṇeṣu (visarga-sandhi: guṇāḥ → guṇā before voiced consonant)
rich_grammar.samasa: तत्त्ववित् = तत्त्वस्य वेत्ता / तत्त्वं वेत्ति इति Samāsa sense: षष्ठी-तत्पुरुष / उपपद-तत्पुरुष; one who knows the truth. | महाबाहो = महान्तौ बाहू यस्य सः Samāsa sense: बहुव्रीहि; one who has mighty arms. | गुणकर्मविभागयोः = गुणानां च कर्मणां च विभागयोः Samāsa sense: द्वन्द्व + षष्ठी-तत्पुरुष; of the division/distinction of guṇas and actions.
rich_grammar.other: तत्त्ववित् is an उपपद-तत्पुरुष compound: tattva + vit (from vid, to know). It is a consonant-ending masculine noun in प्रथमा एकवचनम्. | वर्तन्ते is लट्-लकारः, आत्मनेपदम्, प्रथमपुरुष बहुवचनम् from वृत् (to exist, to move, to operate). | मत्वा is ल्यबन्तम् (absolutive / क्त्वा-प्रत्ययान्तम्) from मन् (to think, to understand). | सज्जते is लट्-लकारः, आत्मनेपदम्, प्रथमपुरुष एकवचनम् from सञ्ज् (to cling, to be attached). | The verse uses iti to quote the tattvavit's realization as direct speech: 'guṇāḥ guṇeṣu vartante' — this is what he understands. | गुणाः in the quoted speech refers to the senses and organs (guṇa-products) while गुणेषु refers to sense-objects (also guṇa-products). Śaṅkara: indriyāṇi indriyārtheṣu vartante.

key_verses.final_takeaway: The tattvavit — the knower of truth — sees all activity as the guṇas of prakṛti interacting with one another. Senses meet objects, mind meets thoughts, body meets the world — but the Self remains the uninvolved witness. With this understanding, attachment falls away naturally, and the wise person lives in freedom while fully engaged in the world.

meaning.caption: Bhagavān Kṛṣṇa teaching Arjuna about the vision of the knower of truth, illustrating: But the knower of truth, O mighty-armed, understanding the distinction between the guṇas and their actions, knows that 'guṇas operate among guṇas' and does not become attached.

key_verses.title: Compassionate Wisdom: Do Not Unsettle the Ignorant
key_verses.sanskrit:
प्रकृतेर्गुणसंमूढाः(स्) सज्जन्ते गुणकर्मसु ।
तानकृत्स्नविदो मन्दान् कृत्स्नविन्न विचालयेत् ॥
key_verses.transliteration:
prakṛterguṇasammūḍhāḥ(s) sajjante guṇakarmasu ।
tānakṛtsnavido mandān kṛtsnavinna vicālayet ॥
key_verses.one_line_meaning: Those deluded by the guṇas of prakṛti become attached to the actions of the guṇas. The wise one who knows the whole truth should not unsettle those slow-witted ones who know only partially.
key_verses.concise_journey: Bhagavān Kṛṣṇa now addresses a subtle danger. In verse 3.26, he warned the wise not to create confusion in the minds of the ignorant who are attached to action. Now he explains the psychology behind that attachment and gives a clear instruction: the person of complete knowledge should not disturb those who only know partially, because such disturbance does not help — it destabilizes without providing the foundation needed for higher understanding.
key_verses.meaning_detail:
Bhagavān Kṛṣṇa now addresses an important aspect of compassionate wisdom. In the preceding verses, he established that the wise person should act in the world to set an example and not create confusion in the minds of those who are attached to action. Now he explains why certain people are so attached and what the correct response of the wise should be.

The first half of the verse diagnoses the condition: prakṛteḥ guṇa-sammūḍhāḥ sajjante guṇa-karmasu — those who are deluded by the guṇas of prakṛti become attached to the actions born of the guṇas. This is the natural state of the ajñānī. The three guṇas — sattva, rajas, and tamas — together compose prakṛti. Under their influence, a person identifies with the body-mind and takes himself to be the doer (kartā). Because of this false identification, he becomes attached to results and to the sense of agency. He thinks, "I am doing this work; this result is mine; this success belongs to me." The attachment is not merely to objects but to the very sense of being the doer of guṇa-born actions.

The second half gives Kṛṣṇa's instruction: tān akṛtsna-vidaḥ mandān kṛtsna-vit na vicālayet — the one who knows the whole truth (kṛtsna-vit) should not unsettle those of partial knowledge (akṛtsna-vidaḥ) who are slow-witted (mandān). The word mandān is significant. It does not mean stupid in a derogatory sense. It means those whose minds are not yet prepared for the subtlety of ātma-jñāna. They are sincere but limited in understanding. They believe that they are doers and that their identity depends on their karma.

If a wise person suddenly tells such a person, "You are not the doer; the guṇas alone act; the Self is actionless" — without the hearer having the mental preparation (adhikāra) to receive it — the result is not liberation but confusion. The person may abandon action prematurely, losing the discipline of karma-yoga without gaining the clarity of jñāna-yoga. This is the worst outcome: he falls from both paths.

Śaṅkarācārya explains that the wise person should instead engage such people in karma-yoga by his own example, gradually leading them toward purity of mind. The teaching of ātma-jñāna comes later, when the mind is ready. Compassion in Vedānta is not indiscriminate broadcasting of the highest truth; it is the skillful, patient guidance of each person according to his or her readiness.
key_verses.story:
Vyāsa Teaches Gradually According to Readiness (Mahābhārata Tradition)
Vyāsa, the great compiler of the Vedas and author of the Mahābhārata, was the teacher of an entire age. He had direct knowledge of Brahman, the ultimate Reality. Yet when he composed the Mahābhārata, he did not begin with Brahma-vidyā. He told stories.
He narrated the tale of the Kuru dynasty — the rivalries, the injustice, the friendships, the betrayals, and the war. Ordinary listeners were drawn in by the drama. Warriors heard lessons of courage. Kings heard lessons of governance. Wives and mothers heard lessons of sacrifice and love.
Within those stories, he wove deeper teachings. The Bhagavad Gītā itself sits within the Mahābhārata, embedded in the context of battle. Vyāsa did not broadcast the Gītā as an abstract philosophical text to people who were unprepared. He placed it exactly where it was needed — in the moment of Arjuna's crisis — so that the listener would naturally be led from confusion to clarity.
If Vyāsa had simply declared to the common people, "You are Brahman; there is no world; there is no doer," most would have been bewildered. Some might have abandoned their duties. Others might have become lazy, thinking, "If I am not the doer, why should I act?" The teaching would have been misunderstood and misapplied.
Instead, Vyāsa met each listener at their level. Those ready for the highest truth found it. Those not yet ready found dharma, courage, devotion, and ethical guidance. This is the principle of 3.29 in action: the kṛtsna-vit does not unsettle the mandān. He teaches gradually, through example and story, until the mind is ready for direct knowledge.
key_verses.real_life_example:
This verse speaks directly to how wisdom should be shared. Imagine a senior colleague who has realized that corporate success does not give lasting happiness. Through years of inner work, she has discovered a quieter source of peace. Now a young, ambitious colleague comes to her, excited about a promotion, working twelve-hour days, and fully identified with career success.

Should the senior colleague say, "None of this matters; your career will not give you real happiness; you are chasing an illusion"? According to this verse, no. That would be vicālayet — unsettling the person. The young colleague is not yet ready to hear that truth. His mind is fully invested in action and results. Disturbing that investment without providing a viable alternative creates confusion and possible despair.

Instead, the wise approach is to support the young colleague in doing his work well, perhaps gently introducing the idea of working without excessive attachment to outcomes, showing by personal example what a balanced life looks like. Over time, as the colleague matures and encounters his own moments of questioning, the deeper teaching can be offered naturally.

This applies in families too. A parent who has discovered meditation and inner peace cannot force a teenager to abandon worldly ambitions. The teenager's mind is naturally drawn to achievement, recognition, and relationships. The parent's role is to model a balanced life and let the child grow at his own pace. Forcing spiritual ideas prematurely often creates rebellion rather than understanding.
key_verses.reflection:
When I discover a truth that has helped me, do I try to force it on others who are not ready, or do I share it with patience and sensitivity?
Can I respect that different people are at different stages of readiness, and that what helped me at a certain point may confuse someone who has not yet reached that point?
Do I act compassionately toward those who are still attached to results and doership, guiding by example rather than by unsettling criticism?

kids_content.explanation_script:
Kṛṣṇa says something very kind here. He says that a person who knows a lot should not confuse someone who does not know as much.
Imagine you know how to swim very well. You have been swimming for years. Now you see a small child who is just learning. The child is holding onto the side of the pool and kicking slowly.
Would you say to the child, "Just let go and swim to the deep end! It is easy!"? No! That would scare the child. The child is not ready.
Kṛṣṇa says the same thing about wisdom. Some people understand life deeply. Others are still learning. The wise person should not confuse or upset the one who is still learning. Instead, the wise person should help gently, like a good teacher.
kids_content.story:
The Gardener and the Seedling
In a village, there was a gardener who had grown a beautiful, tall mango tree. It had taken many years. The tree now gave sweet fruit every summer.
One day, a neighbor planted a small mango seedling in his garden. The neighbor watered it every day and protected it with a small fence.
The gardener looked at the tiny seedling and said, "Why do you keep that fence? My tree does not need a fence. Just remove the fence and let it grow freely!"
The neighbor tried removing the fence. That night, a cow wandered in and ate the seedling. The next morning, the neighbor was heartbroken.
An old woman in the village shook her head. "The gardener's tree is strong because it grew for many years. The seedling was small and needed protection. What works for a big tree does not work for a baby plant."
The gardener felt sorry. He realized his mistake. He helped the neighbor plant a new seedling and this time kept the fence until the plant was strong enough.
Kṛṣṇa's teaching is like this. A wise person should not push others too fast. Just because the wise person no longer needs certain supports does not mean others are ready to give them up. Help gently. Be patient.
kids_content.reflection:
Have I ever given advice to a friend that confused them because they were not ready for it?
Do I try to understand where someone is before telling them what to do?

key_verses.detailed_meaning:
Step 1 — The cause of attachment (prakṛteḥ guṇa-sammūḍhāḥ):
Phrase: prakṛteḥ guṇa-sammūḍhāḥ = those deluded by the guṇas of prakṛti
Prakṛti is the material principle composed of three guṇas: sattva (clarity), rajas (activity), and tamas (inertia). When a person does not know the Self as distinct from prakṛti, the guṇas delude the mind. The person takes himself to be the body-mind complex and sees himself as the doer and enjoyer.

Step 2 — The nature of attachment (sajjante guṇakarmasu):
Phrase: sajjante guṇa-karmasu = they become attached to the actions of the guṇas
The deluded person does not realize that it is the guṇas that act (guṇā guṇeṣu vartante, as taught in 3.28). He attributes agency to himself and becomes attached to specific actions and their results. This attachment is the root of bondage.

Step 3 — Description of the deluded (tān akṛtsna-vidaḥ mandān):
Phrase: tān akṛtsna-vidaḥ mandān = those of incomplete knowledge, who are slow-witted
Akṛtsna-vidaḥ means those who do not know the whole truth — specifically, they do not know that the Self is actionless and that the guṇas alone perform all action. Mandān means those whose intellect is not yet sharp enough to grasp the subtle teaching. These are not bad people; they are simply not yet ready.

Step 4 — The instruction to the wise (kṛtsna-vit na vicālayet):
Phrase: kṛtsna-vit na vicālayet = the one of complete knowledge should not unsettle them
Kṛtsna-vit is the person who knows the whole truth: the distinction between Self and not-Self, the actionlessness of the ātman, and the role of the guṇas. Such a person should not create doubt, confusion, or instability in the minds of those who are not ready for this knowledge.

Step 5 — Why unsettling is harmful:
If the wise person tells an unprepared person, "You are not the doer; action is meaningless," the hearer may abandon karma-yoga without gaining jñāna. He falls from both disciplines. This is worse than continuing in karma-yoga with attachment, because at least karma-yoga purifies the mind over time.

Step 6 — The correct approach of the wise:
Śaṅkarācārya teaches that the wise person should engage the ignorant in karma-yoga through personal example (as taught in 3.25-26). He should act with detachment, inspiring others to act similarly. The direct teaching of ātma-jñāna should be reserved for those whose minds have been sufficiently purified through karma-yoga and who have developed viveka (discrimination) and vairāgya (dispassion).

Step 7 — Connection to the teaching flow:
This verse continues the theme from 3.25-28. Having established that the wise person should act for loka-saṅgraha (3.25), should not create confusion (3.26), should understand that the guṇas act (3.27-28), Kṛṣṇa now adds compassion: do not unsettle those not ready. The wise person's role is gentle guidance, not forceful imposition of truth.
key_verses.more_stories:
1. Śrī Rāmakṛṣṇa and the Worldly Devotee (Modern Vedāntic Tradition)
Śrī Rāmakṛṣṇa Paramahaṃsa was established in the direct vision of Brahman. He could enter samādhi at will and lived in an extraordinary state of God-consciousness. Many seekers of different levels came to him.
One day, a householder came to him and said, "I want to realize God, but I cannot give up my love for my family and my attachment to my work." Some strict renunciates might have scolded him. But Rāmakṛṣṇa smiled warmly. He did not say, "Give up everything immediately." Instead, he told the man to continue his duties with love and to remember God gradually. "Do your work with one hand and hold God with the other," he taught.
Rāmakṛṣṇa understood that this man was not ready for total renunciation. Forcing the highest teaching on him would only create guilt and confusion. Instead, he met the devotee where he was and gently pointed the way forward. This is exactly the spirit of 3.29: the kṛtsna-vit does not unsettle the mandān.

2. Bhīṣma's Silence Before the Court (Mahābhārata)
Bhīṣma, the grandsire of the Kuru dynasty, was the wisest elder in the assembly of Hastināpura. He knew dharma, he knew the truth of the Self, and he understood the political corruption unfolding before him. When Draupadī was humiliated in the court, Bhīṣma's heart was torn.
Yet he remained largely silent. Many have criticized this silence, but from a subtler perspective, Bhīṣma faced a terrible dilemma. The assembly was filled with people of mixed understanding — warriors bound by loyalty, princes blinded by ego, elders compromised by obligation. A sudden, radical pronouncement of ultimate dharma would not have been heard by most. Duryodhana would not have listened. Dhṛtarāṣṭra could not act.
Bhīṣma later taught Yudhiṣṭhira extensively on the bed of arrows, when the student was ready. His silence in the court was not cowardice but a tragic recognition that truth spoken to the unready often falls on deaf ears or causes greater chaos. The kṛtsna-vit must choose the right moment.

3. Yājñavalkya Teaches Maitreyī, Not the Assembly (Bṛhadāraṇyaka Upaniṣad)
Yājñavalkya was a supreme knower of Brahman. In the court of King Janaka, he defeated all scholars in debate and won the prize of a thousand cows. But he did not deliver Brahma-vidyā to the entire assembly.
Later, when he was about to renounce household life, he called his wife Maitreyī. She asked him the profound question: "If the whole earth filled with wealth cannot give me immortality, what shall I do with it?" This question revealed her readiness. Only then did Yājñavalkya unfold the teaching of the Self: "It is the Self that should be seen, heard, reflected upon, and meditated upon."
He did not teach this in the public assembly because most people there were not ready. Maitreyī's question proved her adhikāra (qualification). This is the principle of 3.29: the wise choose their audience carefully and do not unsettle those who lack the preparation for the highest truth.

4. Kṛṣṇa Teaches Arjuna Privately on the Battlefield (Bhagavad Gītā Context)
It is significant that Kṛṣṇa chose to teach the Gītā to Arjuna alone, in the private space between two armies. He did not stand up and address both armies together. He did not broadcast ātma-jñāna to Duryodhana's camp.
Arjuna had the necessary qualifications: he was in genuine distress, he had humility to ask for guidance, and his mind was open to receive teaching. Duryodhana, by contrast, knew that Kṛṣṇa was divine but chose power over wisdom. Teaching him would have been futile.
Even among the Pāṇḍavas, only Arjuna received the teaching directly. Sañjaya narrated it to Dhṛtarāṣṭra, and through Vyāsa's grace it reached the world. But the direct teaching was personal, contextual, and addressed to a ready student. Kṛṣṇa himself practiced what he teaches in 3.29.

5. A Guru's Patient Guidance of Two Disciples (Traditional Vedāntic Story)
A Vedānta guru had two disciples. One was sharp, contemplative, and eager for Self-knowledge. The other was sincere but deeply attached to ritual, family duties, and social recognition.
The sharp student asked questions about Brahman and the guru taught him Upaniṣadic mahāvākyas. The other student once overheard the teaching and became confused. He asked, "If I am Brahman, why should I perform my daily rituals? Why should I serve my parents?"
The guru gently said to him, "Continue your rituals with devotion. Serve your parents with love. These purify the heart. The deeper teaching will make sense when your mind is ready." He did not scold the student for overhearing, nor did he force the higher teaching upon him.
Years later, after sustained karma-yoga and devotion, the second student naturally began to ask the right questions. Then the guru taught him Brahma-vidyā, and the student understood. The guru's patience was his compassion — he did not unsettle a mind that was not ready.

rich_grammar.padacchedah: प्रकृतेः गुणसम्मूढाः सज्जन्ते गुणकर्मसु । तान् अकृत्स्नविदः मन्दान् कृत्स्नवित् न विचालयेत् ॥
rich_grammar.pratipadarthah: प्रकृतेः | (prakṛteḥ) = of prakṛti (material nature) गुणसम्मूढाः | (guṇasammūḍhāḥ) = those deluded by the guṇas सज्जन्ते | (sajjante) = become attached गुणकर्मसु | (guṇakarmasu) = to the actions born of the guṇas तान् | (tān) = those (people) अकृत्स्नविदः | (akṛtsnavidaḥ) = of incomplete knowledge मन्दान् | (mandān) = slow-witted, dull कृत्स्नवित् | (kṛtsnavit) = one who knows the whole truth न | (na) = not विचालयेत् | (vicālayet) = should unsettle, should disturb
rich_grammar.anvayah: प्रकृतेः गुणसम्मूढाः गुणकर्मसु सज्जन्ते । कृत्स्नवित् तान् अकृत्स्नविदः मन्दान् न विचालयेत् ।
rich_grammar.sandhi: प्रकृतेः + गुणसम्मूढाः = प्रकृतेर्गुणसम्मूढाः (विसर्ग → र् before ग) | तान् + अकृत्स्नविदः = तानकृत्स्नविदो (न् + अ → न, विसर्ग → ओ before म) | मन्दान् + कृत्स्नवित् = मन्दान्कृत्स्नवित् | कृत्स्नवित् + न = कृत्स्नविन्न (त् + न = न्न)
rich_grammar.samasa: गुणसम्मूढाः = गुणैः सम्मूढाः Samāsa sense: तृतीया-तत्पुरुष; those deluded by the guṇas. | गुणकर्मसु = गुणानां कर्मसु / गुणजन्येषु कर्मसु Samāsa sense: षष्ठी-तत्पुरुष; in the actions of/born from the guṇas. | अकृत्स्नविदः = न कृत्स्नं विद्यते यैः ते / अकृत्स्नं वेत्ति इति Samāsa sense: नञ्-बहुव्रीहि; those whose knowledge is not complete. | कृत्स्नवित् = कृत्स्नं वेत्ति इति Samāsa sense: उपपद-तत्पुरुष; one who knows the whole.
rich_grammar.other: सज्जन्ते is लट्-लकारः, आत्मनेपदम्, प्रथमपुरुष बहुवचनम् from सञ्ज् (to cling, to be attached). | विचालयेत् is विधिलिङ् (optative), परस्मैपदम्, प्रथमपुरुष एकवचनम् from वि + चल् (causative: to shake, to unsettle); the optative conveys a prohibition with न: "should not unsettle." | मन्दान् is द्वितीया बहुवचनम् (accusative plural), the object of विचालयेत्. | The verse has two distinct clauses: the first (lines 1-2a) describes the condition of the ignorant; the second (lines 2b-4) prescribes the conduct of the wise.

key_verses.final_takeaway: The wise person does not forcefully impose higher knowledge on those who are not ready. Those deluded by the guṇas are attached to action; the compassionate sage guides them gradually through example and karma-yoga, not by unsettling their current understanding.

meaning.caption: Bhagavān Kṛṣṇa teaching Arjuna about compassionate wisdom: do not unsettle the ignorant, illustrating: Those deluded by the guṇas of prakṛti become attached to the actions of the guṇas. The wise one who knows the whole truth should not unsettle those slow-witted ones who know only partially.

key_verses.title: The Formula for Karma Yoga: Offering All Actions to the Lord
key_verses.sanskrit:
मयि सर्वाणि कर्माणि संन्यस्याध्यात्मचेतसा ।
निराशीर्निर्ममो भूत्वा युध्यस्व विगतज्वरः ॥
key_verses.transliteration:
mayi sarvāṇi karmāṇi saṃnyasyādhyātmacetasā ।
nirāśīrnirmamo bhūtvā yudhyasva vigatajvaraḥ ॥
key_verses.one_line_meaning: Dedicating all actions to Me with a mind centered on the Self, free from desire and possessiveness, fight without mental fever.
key_verses.concise_journey: This is the climactic verse of the karma-yoga section in Chapter 3. After explaining why action is necessary (3.4-8), how action connects to the cosmic yajña-cycle (3.9-16), when action is no longer binding for the jñānī (3.17-18), and how the wise should act for loka-saṅgraha (3.20-29), Bhagavān now gives the complete formula for karma-yoga in one verse. This is the Īśvara-arpaṇa-buddhi — the attitude of offering all actions to the Lord, with a mind centered on the Self, free from desire, possessiveness, and anxiety.
key_verses.meaning_detail:
This is one of the most important verses in the entire Bhagavad Gītā. It crystallizes the essence of karma-yoga into a single, complete instruction. Bhagavān Kṛṣṇa brings together everything he has taught in Chapter 3 and gives Arjuna the practical formula.

Mayi sarvāṇi karmāṇi sannyasya — dedicating all actions to Me. The word sannyasya means "having offered" or "having placed." The word mayi means "in Me" — in Īśvara, the Lord who is the total intelligence behind creation. This is Īśvara-arpaṇa-buddhi: the attitude that every action is an offering to the Lord. Just as in a yajña the oblation is placed into the sacred fire, here every action — physical, verbal, and mental — is placed into Bhagavān. The doer no longer claims ownership of the action or its result.

Adhyātma-cetasā — with a mind centered on the Self. This is the jñāna component within karma-yoga. The karma-yogī is not merely a ritualistic performer; he acts with the understanding that the true Self is distinct from the body-mind, which is the field of action. The mind stays connected to the awareness that "I am the ātman, not the doer."

Nirāśīḥ — free from desire. Āśīḥ means desire or expectation for results. Nirāśīḥ means one who has given up the craving for specific outcomes. This does not mean one acts without purpose; it means the emotional dependence on results is abandoned. The karma-yogī does his best and offers the result to the Lord.

Nirmamaḥ — free from possessiveness. Mama means "mine." Nirmamaḥ means one who does not claim ownership over actions, their results, or even the instruments of action (body, mind, skills). Everything belongs to Īśvara and is used in his service.

Bhūtvā yudhyasva vigatajvaraḥ — having become thus, fight without mental fever. Jvara literally means fever, and vigatajvaraḥ means one from whom the fever has gone. The fever is the burning anxiety, guilt, worry, and inner conflict that arise when one acts with attachment. Kṛṣṇa says: once you adopt this attitude of offering, the fever will leave. Then fight — perform your svadharma — with a cool, clear, steady mind.

Śaṅkarācārya notes that this verse combines three elements: (1) Īśvara-arpaṇa — offering to the Lord, (2) adhyātma-cintā — awareness of the Self, and (3) kāma-tyāga — freedom from desire. Together, these constitute the complete attitude of karma-yoga. This is the teaching that transforms bondage-producing action into liberation-producing action.
key_verses.story:
Hanumān Serves Rāma Without Personal Claim (Rāmāyaṇa)
Hanumān is the supreme example of one who offered every action to the Lord without personal desire or possessiveness. When Rāma's wife Sītā was abducted by Rāvaṇa, Hanumān leapt across the ocean to Laṅkā. The leap itself was extraordinary, but what made it sacred was the inner attitude.
Hanumān did not leap to prove his strength. He did not seek fame, reward, or recognition. When he found Sītā in the Aśoka grove, he delivered Rāma's message with humility and reassurance. He did not claim credit for the heroic journey.
When Rāvaṇa's soldiers captured him and set his tail on fire, Hanumān used the fire to burn Laṅkā, but even this was not personal vengeance. It was action performed in service of Rāma's mission.
After returning to Rāma, Hanumān reported the news with simplicity. He did not boast, "I crossed the ocean; I found Sītā; I burned Laṅkā." His attitude was: "I am Rāma's servant; whatever happened was Rāma's will working through me."
This is exactly mayi sarvāṇi karmāṇi sannyasya. Hanumān placed every action in Rāma. He was nirāśīḥ — he desired nothing for himself. He was nirmamaḥ — he claimed nothing as his own. He was vigatajvaraḥ — he acted without inner anxiety or conflict. The result was not burnout or resentment; it was the deepest joy and freedom that comes from selfless service.
key_verses.real_life_example:
This verse gives us a practical formula for transforming ordinary work into spiritual practice. Consider a doctor in a busy hospital. She works long hours, makes critical decisions, and faces life-and-death situations daily. Without the right attitude, the pressure can become unbearable — anxiety about outcomes, fear of blame, desire for recognition, and emotional exhaustion.

Now imagine she adopts the attitude of this verse. She thinks: "I will do my very best for each patient, using all the skill and knowledge I have. But the final outcome is not in my hands alone — it depends on countless factors beyond my control. I offer my work to the larger order that governs life and death." This is Īśvara-arpaṇa-buddhi. She is not being careless; she is releasing the psychological burden of claiming total control.

She becomes nirāśīḥ — she does not obsess over whether each case will bring praise or criticism. She becomes nirmamaḥ — she does not think, "These are my patients, my success, my reputation." She becomes vigatajvaraḥ — the inner fever of anxiety reduces, and she can act with clarity and compassion.

The same applies to a student preparing for exams, a parent raising children, or anyone performing their duties. The formula is: do your best, offer the action and its results to the Lord, stay connected to the awareness that you are more than the doer, and release the fever of anxious expectation. This transforms work from a source of bondage into a means of inner growth.
key_verses.reflection:
Can I adopt the attitude that my actions are offerings to the Lord, rather than personal projects for my own gain?
What desire or expectation am I holding onto that creates inner fever and anxiety in my work?
What would change in my daily life if I acted with full effort but without claiming ownership of the results?

kids_content.explanation_script:
Kṛṣṇa gives Arjuna a beautiful instruction here. He says, "Do everything as a gift to Me. Do not be greedy for results. Do not say 'this is mine.' And do not worry too much."
Imagine you have to do your homework. If you do it thinking, "I must get the highest marks or I will be upset," then you feel nervous and stressed. But if you do it thinking, "I will do my best as a gift — to my teacher, to my parents, to God — and then I will accept whatever happens," then you feel peaceful.
Kṛṣṇa is not saying, "Do not try hard." He is saying, "Try your very best, but offer your work with love and do not be burned by worry."
kids_content.story:
Riya's Science Project
Riya had to make a science project for school. She chose to build a model of the solar system. She worked on it every evening. She painted the planets carefully. She made Saturn's rings with cardboard. She glued the Sun in the center.
Her friend Aarav said, "What if you do not get first place?" Riya's face fell. She started worrying. She could not sleep that night.
The next morning, her grandmother saw her looking tired. "What happened, Riya?"
"I am worried. What if my project is not the best?"
Grandmother smiled. "Did you do your best work?"
"Yes, Paati."
"Then think of it as a gift. You made something beautiful. Offer it with love. If you get a prize, wonderful. If not, your work is still beautiful."
Riya liked that idea. She took her project to school and presented it happily. When the results came, she got second place. For a moment she felt disappointed. But then she remembered: "I gave my best as a gift."
She smiled and clapped for the first-place winner too.
Kṛṣṇa's teaching is this: give your best as an offering, and let go of the worry about results.
kids_content.reflection:
When I do something, am I offering my best work with love, or am I only thinking about winning?
Can I feel peaceful even when the result is different from what I wanted?

key_verses.detailed_meaning:
Step 1 — Offering all actions to the Lord (mayi sarvāṇi karmāṇi sannyasya):
Phrase: mayi sarvāṇi karmāṇi sannyasya = having dedicated all actions to Me
Mayi means "in Me" — in Bhagavān, the Lord. Sarvāṇi karmāṇi means all actions without exception — not just rituals, but every physical, verbal, and mental action. Sannyasya is the gerund (ktvānta) from sam-ni-as, meaning "having placed" or "having offered." The karma-yogī places every action in Īśvara as an oblation. This is Īśvara-arpaṇa-buddhi, the defining attitude of karma-yoga.

Step 2 — With a mind centered on the Self (adhyātma-cetasā):
Phrase: adhyātma-cetasā = with a mind focused on the inner Self
Adhyātma means "pertaining to the Self" or "the inner spiritual dimension." Cetasā is the instrumental case of cetas (mind). The karma-yogī does not merely perform actions mechanically; he maintains the awareness that the true Self is the witnessing consciousness, distinct from the body-mind that acts. This connects karma-yoga to jñāna.

Step 3 — Free from desire (nirāśīḥ):
Phrase: nirāśīḥ = free from desire/expectation
Āśīḥ means desire, hope, or craving for specific results. Nis-āśīḥ is one who has abandoned this craving. The karma-yogī acts with full effort but without emotional dependence on outcomes. He does not say, "I will be happy only if this succeeds." He offers his best and accepts what comes.

Step 4 — Free from possessiveness (nirmamaḥ):
Phrase: nirmamaḥ = free from the sense of "mine"
Mama means "mine." Nirmamaḥ is one who has given up the claiming ownership — "my action, my result, my achievement, my body, my skill." Everything is recognized as belonging to Īśvara and used in his service.

Step 5 — Fight without mental fever (yudhyasva vigatajvaraḥ):
Phrase: yudhyasva vigatajvaraḥ = fight, free from inner fever
Yudhyasva is the imperative of yudh (to fight) — Kṛṣṇa directly commands Arjuna to act. Vigata-jvaraḥ means one from whom jvara (fever, burning anxiety, inner conflict) has departed. When actions are offered to the Lord, desire is abandoned, and possessiveness is released, the inner burning naturally subsides. Action becomes clean, clear, and free.

Step 6 — The complete formula:
This verse combines four elements into one instruction: (1) Īśvara-arpaṇa — offer actions to the Lord, (2) adhyātma-awareness — stay connected to Self-knowledge, (3) desire-freedom — release craving for results, and (4) fever-freedom — act without inner conflict. Together, these transform binding action into liberating karma-yoga.

Step 7 — Context and significance:
Śaṅkarācārya considers this verse the summary instruction (upasaṃhāra) of the karma-yoga teaching in Chapter 3. Everything taught from 3.1 to 3.29 converges here. The verse also anticipates Chapter 18, verse 66, where Kṛṣṇa will give the ultimate surrender teaching. Here in Chapter 3, the emphasis is specifically on action performed with the right attitude.
key_verses.more_stories:
1. Arjuna's Transformation on the Battlefield (Bhagavad Gītā / Mahābhārata)
At the beginning of the Gītā, Arjuna was paralyzed. His body trembled, his mouth went dry, his bow slipped from his hands. He saw teachers, grandfathers, cousins, and friends on the opposing side and said, "I will not fight." His refusal was born from emotional attachment (mama) and desire for specific outcomes (āśīḥ): "I want my loved ones alive; I want to avoid the guilt of killing."
Kṛṣṇa spent eighteen chapters transforming that attitude. But the seed of the transformation is here in 3.30. The instruction is clear: offer all actions to Me, stay centered in the Self, abandon desire and possessiveness, and act without inner fever.
By the end of Chapter 18, Arjuna will say, "My delusion is destroyed; I will do as you say." The fever will have left. He will fight — not from hatred, not from desire, but from clarity and surrender. This verse is the formula that makes that transformation possible.

2. Vidura's Selfless Counsel (Mahābhārata)
Vidura served the Kuru court with unwavering dharma. Though he was the wisest counselor in Hastināpura, he held no personal ambition. When he advised Dhṛtarāṣṭra to treat the Pāṇḍavas justly, he did not do so for personal gain. He had no desire for throne, wealth, or recognition.
When his advice was repeatedly ignored and he was insulted by Duryodhana, Vidura did not retaliate. He did not say, "I will stop advising because my counsel is not appreciated." Nor did he say, "I must force them to listen." He continued to speak truth when called upon and stepped back when dismissed.
Vidura embodied nirāśīḥ and nirmamaḥ. His counsel was an offering, not a personal project. When it was rejected, he did not burn with fever. He quietly left the court and lived in the forest, peaceful and whole. His action was mayi sannyasya — offered to dharma and to the Lord — and therefore it did not bind him.

3. Sugrīva and Hanumān: Contrasting Motivations (Rāmāyaṇa)
When Rāma allied with Sugrīva to defeat Vālī, Sugrīva had a personal motive: he wanted his kingdom and wife restored. His action was driven by āśīḥ (desire) and mama (my kingdom, my wife). After Vālī's death, Sugrīva became so absorbed in enjoyment that he forgot his promise to help Rāma find Sītā. It took Lakṣmaṇa's angry visit to remind him.
Hanumān, by contrast, served Rāma without personal desire. He had no kingdom to gain, no wife to reclaim, no personal score to settle. His action was pure offering. When he crossed the ocean, burned Laṅkā, and brought news of Sītā, he claimed nothing. His service was vigatajvaraḥ — free from the fever of personal ambition.
The contrast illustrates 3.30 perfectly. Sugrīva acted with desire and possessiveness; his action was burdened. Hanumān acted with Īśvara-arpaṇa-buddhi; his action was free. Both acted, but the quality of their inner experience was vastly different.

4. Karṇa's Charity as Offering (Mahābhārata)
Karṇa, despite being on the side of adharma in many political decisions, had one extraordinary quality: his dāna (charity) was absolute. He had vowed never to refuse anyone who asked him for a gift. When Indra came disguised as a brāhmaṇa and asked for his divine armor and earrings (kavaca-kuṇḍala), Karṇa gave them knowing it would cost him his protection in battle.
Karṇa's giving was not calculated. He did not ask, "What will I get in return?" He did not hold back thinking, "This is mine; I need it." In this one dimension, Karṇa embodied nirmamaḥ and nirāśīḥ. His charity was an offering without expectation.
Yet Karṇa's tragedy was that this attitude did not extend to all his actions. In matters of loyalty to Duryodhana, he was driven by obligation, ego, and desire. The lesson is that partial application of 3.30 liberates partially; complete application liberates fully.

5. Mother Yaśodā's Loving Service (Bhāgavata Purāṇa)
Yaśodā, the foster mother of Kṛṣṇa, served the child with complete devotion. She cooked for him, cleaned his mischief, scolded him gently, chased him through the village, and held him close when he was frightened. Every act of mothering was an offering of love.
Yaśodā did not serve Kṛṣṇa thinking, "I am doing this so that my son will take care of me in old age." She did not serve thinking, "I must make him obedient so people will praise my parenting." Her service was spontaneous, selfless, and joyful.
When Kṛṣṇa eventually left Vṛndāvana, Yaśodā's heart ached, but her love did not turn into resentment or possessiveness. She did not say, "After all I did for you, how could you leave?" Her love remained pure because it was never transactional.
This maternal devotion illustrates the spirit of 3.30 in its sweetest form. Every action offered to the Lord, without desire for return, without possessiveness, without fever. Even ordinary daily actions — cooking, cleaning, caring — become sacred when done in this spirit.

rich_grammar.padacchedah: मयि सर्वाणि कर्माणि सन्न्यस्य अध्यात्मचेतसा । निराशीः निर्ममः भूत्वा युध्यस्व विगतज्वरः ॥
rich_grammar.pratipadarthah: मयि | (mayi) = in Me (in the Lord) सर्वाणि | (sarvāṇi) = all कर्माणि | (karmāṇi) = actions सन्न्यस्य | (sannyasya) = having offered, having dedicated अध्यात्मचेतसा | (adhyātmacetasā) = with a mind centered on the Self निराशीः | (nirāśīḥ) = free from desire/expectation निर्ममः | (nirmamaḥ) = free from possessiveness/sense of 'mine' भूत्वा | (bhūtvā) = having become युध्यस्व | (yudhyasva) = fight! (imperative) विगतज्वरः | (vigatajvaraḥ) = one from whom fever/anxiety has gone
rich_grammar.anvayah: मयि सर्वाणि कर्माणि अध्यात्मचेतसा सन्न्यस्य, निराशीः निर्ममः भूत्वा, विगतज्वरः युध्यस्व ।
rich_grammar.sandhi: सन्न्यस्य + अध्यात्मचेतसा = सन्न्यस्याध्यात्मचेतसा (अ + अ = आ) | निराशीः + निर्ममः = निराशीर्निर्ममो (विसर्ग → र् before न; ओ due to following भ) | निर्ममः + भूत्वा = निर्ममो भूत्वा (विसर्ग → ओ before भ) | विगत + ज्वरः = विगतज्वरः (तत्पुरुष compound, no external sandhi)
rich_grammar.samasa: अध्यात्मचेतसा = अध्यात्मनि चेतः यस्य तेन / अध्यात्मविषये चेतसा Samāsa sense: बहुव्रीहि or सप्तमी-तत्पुरुष; with a mind directed toward the inner Self. | निराशीः = निर्गताः आशिषः यस्य सः Samāsa sense: नञ् (निर्) + बहुव्रीहि; one from whom desires have departed. | निर्ममः = निर्गतं ममत्वं यस्य सः Samāsa sense: नञ् (निर्) + बहुव्रीहि; one from whom the sense of 'mine' has gone. | विगतज्वरः = विगतः ज्वरः यस्य सः Samāsa sense: बहुव्रीहि; one from whom fever/anxiety has departed.
rich_grammar.other: सन्न्यस्य is a क्त्वान्त (gerund) from सम् + नि + अस् (to place), meaning 'having placed/offered.' | भूत्वा is a क्त्वान्त from भू, meaning 'having become.' | युध्यस्व is लोट्-लकारः, आत्मनेपदम्, मध्यमपुरुष एकवचनम् from युध्, meaning 'fight!' — a direct command from Kṛṣṇa to Arjuna. | विगतज्वरः is used as a predicate adjective modifying the implied subject (त्वम् = you, Arjuna). | The verse has two participial clauses (सन्न्यस्य and भूत्वा) leading to the main imperative (युध्यस्व), creating the structure: 'having done X, having become Y, do Z.'

key_verses.final_takeaway: This is the complete formula for karma-yoga: offer every action to the Lord with a Self-aware mind, release desire and possessiveness, and act without inner anxiety. When action becomes offering, the doer is freed from bondage even while performing his duties in the world.

meaning.caption: Bhagavān Kṛṣṇa teaching Arjuna about the formula for karma yoga: offering all actions to the lord, illustrating: Dedicating all actions to Me with a mind centered on the Self, free from desire and possessiveness, fight without mental fever.

key_verses.title: Faith and Freedom from Fault-Finding
key_verses.sanskrit:
ये मे मतमिदं(न्) नित्यम् अनुतिष्ठन्ति मानवाः ।
श्रद्धावन्तोऽनसूयन्तो(तः) मुच्यन्ते तेऽपि कर्मभिः ॥
key_verses.transliteration:
ye me matamidaṁ(n) nityam anutiṣṭhanti mānavāḥ ।
śraddhāvanto'nasūyanto(taḥ) mucyante te'pi karmabhiḥ ॥
key_verses.one_line_meaning: Those who always follow this teaching of Mine with faith and without fault-finding are freed from the bondage of actions.
key_verses.concise_journey: Having given the complete karma-yoga formula in verse 3.30, Bhagavān Kṛṣṇa now states the result for those who follow it. Two qualities are essential: śraddhā (trust in the teaching and the teacher) and anasūyā (freedom from the tendency to find fault with the teaching or the teacher). When both are present, the person is freed from the bondage of karma, even while performing actions in the world.
key_verses.meaning_detail:
After giving the climactic instruction in 3.30, Bhagavān now declares the fruit for those who follow it. The verse has a simple but profound structure: the condition (follow this teaching with faith and without caviling) and the result (freedom from the bondage of karma).

Ye me matam idaṁ nityam anutiṣṭhanti mānavāḥ — those human beings who always follow this teaching of Mine. Me matam means "My teaching" or "My doctrine" — referring to the entire karma-yoga teaching given from 3.4 through 3.30. Nityam means "always" or "consistently" — not as a one-time experiment but as a sustained way of living. Anutiṣṭhanti means "they follow" or "they practice" — it implies not just intellectual agreement but actual implementation in daily life.

Śraddhāvantaḥ — endowed with śraddhā. Śraddhā is often translated as "faith," but in Vedānta it has a specific meaning. It is the trust that the teaching of the śāstra and the teacher is valid, even before one has personally verified it through experience. It is not blind belief; it is provisional trust that allows the student to practice the teaching long enough for it to bear fruit. Without śraddhā, a person tries the teaching half-heartedly, gives up quickly, and concludes that it does not work.

Anasūyantaḥ — free from fault-finding. Asūyā is the tendency to find fault with the teaching or the teacher. It may take the form of, "This teaching is impractical," or "Kṛṣṇa is just saying this because he wants Arjuna to fight," or "This philosophy does not apply to modern life." The fault-finding mind never gives the teaching a fair trial. It constantly looks for reasons to reject, criticize, or diminish the teaching. Anasūyantaḥ describes those who are free from this tendency.

Mucyante te'pi karmabhiḥ — they are freed from the bondage of actions. The word api (also, even) is significant. Even though they continue to perform actions in the world, they are freed from the binding nature of those actions. This is because karma-yoga, properly practiced with Īśvara-arpaṇa-buddhi, purifies the mind and gradually leads to self-knowledge, which alone is the ultimate liberator.

Śaṅkarācārya explains that śraddhā and anasūyā together create the right soil for the seed of karma-yoga to grow. Without trust, the teaching is never applied. Without freedom from fault-finding, the teaching is constantly undermined. With both, the practice is sustained, the mind is purified, and freedom follows naturally.
key_verses.story:
Ekalavya's Unwavering Śraddhā (Mahābhārata)
Ekalavya was a tribal boy who longed to learn archery from Droṇa, the greatest archery teacher of the age. But Droṇa refused to teach him, citing social restrictions. A lesser person might have become bitter, might have said, "This teaching system is unfair; the knowledge itself must be worthless."
But Ekalavya did not find fault. He did not reject the value of the teaching because the institution had rejected him. Instead, he made a clay image of Droṇa and practiced before it with absolute faith and devotion, treating the image as his guru.
Through years of sustained practice guided by śraddhā, Ekalavya became an extraordinary archer — so skilled that even Arjuna was threatened by his ability. The teaching worked because the student's attitude was right: he had śraddhā (trust in the teaching) and anasūyā (no bitterness or fault-finding).
The tragic ending of the story — Droṇa demanding his thumb as guru-dakṣiṇā — adds complexity, but the principle remains. Ekalavya's mastery came from the quality of his inner attitude, not from the circumstances of his training. This is the spirit of 3.31: when faith is genuine and fault-finding is absent, the teaching bears fruit.

The verse does not promise that the path will be easy or that the world will always be fair. It promises that the right inner attitude — śraddhā and anasūyā — will lead to freedom from the bondage of karma.
key_verses.real_life_example:
Consider two students learning meditation from the same teacher. The first student approaches with genuine trust. She does not expect instant results, but she believes the practice has value. She sits daily, follows the instructions, and when difficulties arise — a restless mind, sleepiness, doubt — she returns to the practice with patience. Over months and years, her mind gradually becomes clearer, calmer, and more focused.

The second student starts with skepticism. He tries the practice for a week, finds his mind still restless, and thinks, "This does not work. The teacher is probably not very good. This method is outdated." He reads a different book, tries a different technique, finds fault with that too, and keeps jumping from method to method. After years, he has gained little because he never gave any single teaching a sustained, faithful trial.

The difference is not intelligence or talent — it is śraddhā and anasūyā. The first student trusted the teaching enough to practice it consistently. The second student's fault-finding mind prevented the teaching from taking root.

This applies beyond meditation. In any field — learning music, building a career, raising children, maintaining a marriage — sustained trust in a sound principle, combined with freedom from the habit of constant criticism, allows growth to happen. The fault-finding mind is always starting over; the faithful mind keeps deepening.
key_verses.reflection:
Do I give teachings and practices a fair trial, or do I abandon them quickly when results do not come immediately?
Is there a tendency in me to find fault with teachers, traditions, or teachings rather than honestly examining my own commitment?
Can I cultivate the patience to follow a wise teaching consistently, even when progress seems slow?

kids_content.explanation_script:
Kṛṣṇa says that if you follow his teaching with trust and without complaining about it, you will become free from the trouble that actions cause.
Think of it like learning a musical instrument. If your teacher says, "Practice these scales every day," and you trust the teacher and practice without grumbling, you will slowly become a good musician. But if you say, "This is boring, this teacher does not know anything, I want to skip the scales and play songs directly," you will not learn properly.
Kṛṣṇa says the same thing about his teaching. If you trust it and follow it sincerely, it will work. If you keep complaining and finding fault, it will not help you.
kids_content.story:
Meera Learns to Swim
Meera wanted to learn swimming. Her swimming coach, Usha Ma'am, said, "First, you must learn to float. Hold the edge of the pool and practice kicking. Then we will slowly move to floating on your back."
Meera's friend Tina said, "This is so boring. I want to dive right away. This coach is too slow." Tina skipped the basics and tried to swim in the deep end. She panicked and had to be pulled out. After that, she stopped coming to class.
Meera was also a little impatient, but she trusted Usha Ma'am. She practiced kicking, then floating, then gliding. Some days she swallowed water. Some days she felt tired. But she kept going.
After two months, Meera could swim across the pool smoothly. Tina, who had quit, still could not swim.
Meera's trust in her coach and her willingness to follow the steps patiently made the difference. She did not complain that the method was wrong; she simply practiced.
Kṛṣṇa says the same: follow the teaching with trust and without fault-finding, and you will reach the goal.
kids_content.reflection:
When I am learning something new, do I trust my teacher and follow their guidance, or do I give up quickly?
Do I sometimes find fault with something just because it is hard or takes time?

key_verses.detailed_meaning:
Step 1 — The teaching referred to (me matam idaṁ):
Phrase: ye me matam idaṁ = those who (follow) this teaching of Mine
Me matam means "My teaching" or "My doctrine." Idaṁ (this) points to the entire karma-yoga teaching that Kṛṣṇa has unfolded in Chapter 3, culminating in the Īśvara-arpaṇa formula of verse 30. The word matam also implies that this is not a casual suggestion but a considered, authoritative teaching.

Step 2 — Consistent practice (nityam anutiṣṭhanti mānavāḥ):
Phrase: nityam anutiṣṭhanti mānavāḥ = those human beings who always follow/practice
Nityam means "always" or "consistently" — not a one-time experiment. Anutiṣṭhanti (from anu + sthā) means "they follow" or "they practice." Mānavāḥ means "human beings," emphasizing that this teaching is available to all people.

Step 3 — The first condition: faith (śraddhāvantaḥ):
Phrase: śraddhāvantaḥ = endowed with śraddhā (faith/trust)
Śraddhā in Vedānta is the provisional trust that allows a student to practice the teaching before personal verification. It is not blind belief but intelligent openness. Without śraddhā, the teaching is never given a fair trial.

Step 4 — The second condition: freedom from fault-finding (anasūyantaḥ):
Phrase: anasūyantaḥ = free from the tendency to find fault
Asūyā is the habit of criticizing, diminishing, or rejecting the teaching or the teacher. Anasūyantaḥ describes those who are free from this tendency. The fault-finding mind creates a barrier that prevents the teaching from penetrating.

Step 5 — The result: liberation from karma-bondage (mucyante te'pi karmabhiḥ):
Phrase: mucyante te'pi karmabhiḥ = they are freed, even from (the bondage of) actions
Mucyante is passive of muc (to release). Te api means "they also" — even while performing actions, they are freed. Karmabhiḥ means "from actions" in the sense of "from the binding effect of actions." Karma-yoga, practiced with the right attitude, purifies the mind and leads to knowledge, which is the ultimate liberator.

Step 6 — Why both conditions are necessary:
Śraddhā ensures that the practice is sustained; anasūyā ensures that the practice is not undermined. Together, they create the inner environment in which karma-yoga can bear fruit. Without either, the practice remains superficial or is abandoned prematurely.
key_verses.more_stories:
1. Naciketas and Unwavering Faith (Kaṭha Upaniṣad)
Young Naciketas was sent to the abode of Yama, the Lord of Death, by his own father's careless words. When Naciketas arrived, Yama was absent for three days. Upon returning, Yama offered Naciketas three boons to compensate for the inhospitable welcome.
Naciketas used the first boon for reconciliation with his father, the second for knowledge of the sacred fire ritual. But for the third, he asked the ultimate question: "What happens after death? Does the Self exist or not?"
Yama tried to dissuade him. He offered wealth, long life, beautiful women, kingdoms, and heavenly pleasures. But Naciketas refused everything. His śraddhā in the pursuit of truth was unshakable. He did not find fault with the difficulty of the question or the seeming evasiveness of the teacher.
Finally, Yama was pleased. He said, "The Self is not gained by the weak." And he taught Naciketas the truth of the ātman.
Naciketas embodies both qualities of 3.31: śraddhā (trust in the pursuit of knowledge) and anasūyā (no fault-finding with the teacher or the teaching process).

2. Satyakāma Jābāla's Honest Trust (Chāndogya Upaniṣad)
Satyakāma wanted to study under a guru. When the teacher Gautama asked him his lineage, Satyakāma honestly said, "I do not know my father's name. My mother Jābālā told me she served many people and does not know whose son I am."
Gautama was moved. He said, "Only a brāhmaṇa (one of truthful nature) would speak so honestly. I will teach you." He gave Satyakāma four hundred lean cows and sent him to the forest to tend them.
For years, Satyakāma lived in the forest with the cows. He did not complain. He did not say, "The guru sent me away; he does not care about me." He tended the cows with faith and patience. During this time, the bull, the fire, a swan, and a diver bird taught him aspects of Brahman.
When Satyakāma returned, his face was radiant. Gautama saw that he had received knowledge and completed his instruction. Satyakāma's śraddhā — trusting the guru's method even when it seemed strange — and his anasūyā — never complaining about the years of apparent exile — brought him to knowledge.

3. Śrī Rāmānuja's Persistent Seeking (Vaiṣṇava Tradition)
Śrī Rāmānuja, the great Viśiṣṭādvaita teacher, sought the secret meaning of certain mantras from his guru Goṣṭhīpūrṇa. The guru refused him multiple times. Rāmānuja did not become bitter. He did not say, "This guru is withholding knowledge unfairly." He kept approaching with humility and trust.
After being refused many times, Goṣṭhīpūrṇa finally taught him the mantra, but with a strict condition: "Do not share this with anyone; it is meant for you alone."
Rāmānuja received the teaching with gratitude. Then, moved by compassion, he climbed a temple tower and shouted the mantra for all to hear. His guru was furious, but Rāmānuja said, "If I alone go to hell for this but many are liberated, I am willing."
This story shows both śraddhā and anasūyā in the seeking phase, and then an extraordinary compassion in the sharing phase. Rāmānuja's persistent trust, without fault-finding, allowed him to receive the teaching; his compassion then extended it to others.

4. Arjuna's Śraddhā in Kṛṣṇa's Guidance (Bhagavad Gītā)
Throughout the Gītā, Arjuna asks difficult questions. He doubts, he struggles, he expresses confusion. But at no point does he say, "Kṛṣṇa, you are wrong." At no point does he find fault with Kṛṣṇa's character or motives. His questions come from genuine seeking, not from asūyā.
In Chapter 2, Arjuna said, "I am your disciple; teach me." This surrender established the foundation. Even when the teachings seemed contradictory — "Renounce action" followed by "Perform action" — Arjuna trusted that there was coherence and asked for clarification rather than rejecting the teacher.
By Chapter 18, this sustained śraddhā bore fruit. Arjuna declared, "My delusion is destroyed. I stand firm. I will do as you say." The path from confusion to clarity was possible because Arjuna never abandoned śraddhā and never fell into asūyā. This is the living demonstration of 3.31.

5. A Village Teacher and Her Patient Student (Indian Folklore)
In a small village, an elderly teacher ran a pāṭhaśālā (school) under a banyan tree. One student struggled more than others. He could not memorize ślokas quickly. Other children laughed at him.
The teacher never gave up on him. But more importantly, the student never gave up on the teaching. He came every morning, sat in his place, and repeated the verses with sincerity. He did not say, "This method is bad because I am not learning fast." He did not blame the teacher for his difficulty.
Years passed. The student's mind slowly deepened. What others memorized in days, he memorized in months. But his understanding was rooted deeply because every verse had been held in faith and sustained effort.
Eventually, this student became one of the most respected teachers in the region. People marveled at how someone who struggled so much could teach so beautifully. The answer was śraddhā and anasūyā: trust in the teaching, freedom from fault-finding, and the patience to let the process work.

rich_grammar.padacchedah: ये मे मतम् इदम् नित्यम् अनुतिष्ठन्ति मानवाः । श्रद्धावन्तः अनसूयन्तः मुच्यन्ते ते अपि कर्मभिः ॥
rich_grammar.pratipadarthah: ये | (ye) = those who मे | (me) = My (of Me) मतम् | (matam) = teaching, doctrine इदम् | (idam) = this नित्यम् | (nityam) = always, consistently अनुतिष्ठन्ति | (anutiṣṭhanti) = follow, practice मानवाः | (mānavāḥ) = human beings श्रद्धावन्तः | (śraddhāvantaḥ) = endowed with faith अनसूयन्तः | (anasūyantaḥ) = free from fault-finding मुच्यन्ते | (mucyante) = are freed, are liberated ते | (te) = they अपि | (api) = also, even कर्मभिः | (karmabhiḥ) = from (the bondage of) actions
rich_grammar.anvayah: ये मानवाः श्रद्धावन्तः अनसूयन्तः इदं मे मतं नित्यम् अनुतिष्ठन्ति, ते अपि कर्मभिः मुच्यन्ते ।
rich_grammar.sandhi: मतम् + इदम् = मतमिदं (म् + इ = मि; म् → anusvāra) | नित्यम् + अनुतिष्ठन्ति = नित्यमनुतिष्ठन्ति (म् + अ = म) | श्रद्धावन्तः + अनसूयन्तः = श्रद्धावन्तोऽनसूयन्तो (विसर्ग → ओ before अ, avagraha) | मुच्यन्ते + ते = मुच्यन्ते ते | ते + अपि = तेऽपि (ए + अ = एऽ, avagraha)
rich_grammar.samasa: श्रद्धावन्तः = श्रद्धा अस्ति येषां ते Samāsa sense: बहुव्रीहि / मतुप्-प्रत्ययान्त; those who possess śraddhā (faith). | अनसूयन्तः = न असूयन्ते इति अनसूयन्तः Samāsa sense: नञ्-तत्पुरुष (शतृप्रत्ययान्तम्); those who do not find fault.
rich_grammar.other: अनुतिष्ठन्ति is लट्-लकारः, परस्मैपदम्, प्रथमपुरुष बहुवचनम् from अनु + स्था, meaning 'they follow/practice.' | मुच्यन्ते is लट्-लकारः, कर्मणि (passive), प्रथमपुरुष बहुवचनम् from मुच्, meaning 'they are released/freed.' | कर्मभिः is तृतीया बहुवचनम्, used in the sense 'from (the bondage of) actions' — the ablative sense is conveyed through the passive construction. | अपि (even/also) is emphatic: even though they perform actions, they are freed — because karma-yoga purifies the mind and leads to knowledge. | The verse sets up a pair with 3.32, which gives the opposite case (those who do not follow).

key_verses.final_takeaway: Two attitudes unlock the transformative power of karma-yoga: śraddhā (trust in the teaching) and anasūyā (freedom from fault-finding). With these, even a person immersed in worldly action is gradually freed from the bondage of karma.
