key_verses.title: Offering the Fruits of Action
key_verses.sanskrit:
अथैतदप्यशक्तोऽसि कर्तुं(म्) मद्योगमाश्रितः ।
सर्वकर्मफलत्यागं(न्) ततः(क्) कुरु यतात्मवान् ॥
key_verses.transliteration:
athaitadapyaśakto'si kartuṁ madyogamāśritaḥ ।
sarvakarmaphalatyāgaṁ(n) tataḥ(k) kuru yatātmavān ॥
key_verses.one_line_meaning: Bhagavān Kṛṣṇa says: if we are unable to do even this, then, taking refuge in His worship, we should renounce the results of all actions with self-mastery.
key_verses.concise_journey:
Bhagavān Kṛṣṇa now gives the lowest and most accessible step in the ladder of bhakti-yoga. If we are not ready for niṣkāma karma, if we are not ready for abhyāsa-yoga, if we are not ready for Viśvarūpa upāsanā, and if we are certainly not ready for nirguṇa jñāna-yoga, then we should begin here.
This step is meant for a person who still has many personal desires.
key_verses.meaning_detail:
Bhagavān Kṛṣṇa now gives the lowest and most accessible step in the ladder of bhakti-yoga. If we are not ready for niṣkāma karma, if we are not ready for abhyāsa-yoga, if we are not ready for Viśvarūpa upāsanā, and if we are certainly not ready for nirguṇa jñāna-yoga, then we should begin here.
This step is meant for a person who still has many personal desires. Such a person may be concerned with family, children, health, education, house, money, success, security, and many other legitimate worldly goals. The teaching does not ask such a person to suppress all desires immediately. Suppressed desires will continue to disturb the mind. Therefore, the instruction is: fulfill legitimate desires through dhārmic means, but change the attitude toward the result.
The key instruction is sarvakarmaphalatyāgam kuru — renounce the results of all actions. This does not mean physically throwing away the result. It means mentally offering the result to Bhagavān first, and then receiving it back as Bhagavān’s prasāda. If we build a house, we dedicate it to Bhagavān and live in it as Bhagavān’s gift. If we receive food, clothing, success, or family joy, we first recognize it as prasāda.
Even sakāma karma can become karma-yoga when the result is received as Bhagavān’s prasāda. Ordinary selfish action does not purify because selfishness itself is impurity. But when the result is offered to Bhagavān and received with prasāda-buddhi, even such action slowly purifies the mind. From this first step, we gradually grow into niṣkāma karma, then upāsanā, and eventually jñāna-yoga.
key_verses.story:
In the Bhāgavata Purāṇa, the Govardhana episode beautifully shows how an ordinary action can be transformed through offering.
In Vṛndāvana, the people were preparing for the annual worship of Indra. They depended on rain, cattle, grass, and land, and they believed that Indra’s grace gave them prosperity. Food was prepared, offerings were gathered, and the community was ready for ritual worship.
Then young Kṛṣṇa questioned the elders.
He asked them to think more deeply about their life. Their cows grazed on Govardhana. The mountain gave grass, streams, caves, trees, and shelter. Their daily life was sustained by the land, the cows, and the whole order around them.
Kṛṣṇa guided them to offer their gratitude to Govardhana instead. The people prepared heaps of food, decorated the cows, gathered together, and offered everything with reverence. What had begun as a customary ritual became a conscious offering to Bhagavān manifest through the very world that supported them.
After the offering, the people partook of the food as prasāda.
This is the heart of sarvakarmaphalatyāga. The food was not wasted. The people still ate. But before enjoying, they offered. By offering first and receiving later, the attitude changed.
The action remained part of life, but the result was no longer treated as “mine alone.” It became Bhagavān’s prasāda.
key_verses.real_life_example:
This verse is extremely practical for modern life. Many of us are not yet ready to give up personal goals. We want a good education, a home, career growth, financial stability, children’s success, family health, and social security. This verse does not ask us to pretend that we have no such desires.
Instead, it gives a realistic spiritual beginning. Pursue legitimate goals through dhārmic means, but do not hold the results with egoistic ownership. Before enjoying any result, inwardly offer it to Bhagavān. Then receive it as prasāda.
This attitude reduces arrogance when things go well and bitterness when things do not go as expected. It helps us see life not merely as personal achievement, but as participation in Bhagavān’s order.
Even small actions can become spiritual practice. Eating food, entering a new home, wearing new clothes, receiving salary, celebrating success, or facing failure — all can be connected to Bhagavān through prasāda-buddhi.
key_verses.reflection:
Do I treat my achievements as entirely mine, or can I offer them to Bhagavān first?
Can I receive both success and difficulty as prasāda, without arrogance or bitterness?

kids_content.explanation_script:
“Suppose you get your favorite sweet. You may want to eat it immediately.
But before eating, you can place it before Bhagavān and say, ‘Thank you.’ Then you eat it as prasāda.
The sweet is still yours to enjoy, but now your heart becomes thankful. Kṛṣṇa is teaching that whatever good result we get, we can first offer it to Bhagavān and then enjoy it with gratitude.”
kids_content.story:
A child got a new box of crayons.
The child was very excited and wanted to start coloring immediately.
The parent said, “Before using them, let us place them near Bhagavān and say thank you.”
The child asked, “Will Bhagavān take the crayons?”
The parent smiled and said, “No. We are offering our thanks. Then you can use them happily as prasāda.”
The child placed the crayons near Bhagavān, folded hands, and then began drawing.
The crayons were the same, but the child’s heart had changed. Now the drawing felt like a gift.
kids_content.reflection:
What is one thing you can say “thank you” to Bhagavān for today?
Can you offer something before enjoying it?

key_verses.detailed_meaning:
Step 1: The Next Descent
Phrase: atha etad api aśakto'si kartum = if you are unable to do even this
Bhagavān Kṛṣṇa now addresses one who is not able to practice even the previous step. This is a compassionate descent to the most accessible level of karma-yoga.
Step 2: Taking Refuge in Worship
Phrase: madyogamāśritaḥ = taking refuge in My yoga / My worship
We should still remain connected to Bhagavān. Even if the life is filled with personal desires and worldly duties, it should not be cut off from Bhagavān. This life too can become a form of bhakti-yoga when Bhagavān is included.
Step 3: Renouncing the Results
Phrase: sarvakarmaphalatyāgaṁ kuru = renounce the results of all actions
This does not mean throwing away the results. It means offering all karma-phala to Bhagavān and then receiving it back as prasāda. The result may still be used, enjoyed, or shared, but the attitude of ownership changes.
Step 4: All Kinds of Results
Phrase: sarvakarmaphala = the results of all actions
This includes the results of kāmya karmas, ordinary worldly actions, and even unavoidable mixed actions in daily life. Before enjoying anything, we dedicate it to Bhagavān.
Step 5: With Self-Mastery
Phrase: yatātmavān = one with self-restraint / self-mastery
Even at this entry level, some discipline is required. Desires may be fulfilled, but only through legitimate and dhārmic means. The mind should learn gratitude, restraint, and prasāda-buddhi.
Step 6: The Teaching
Phrase: tataḥ kuru = then do
The teaching is: begin where we are. If higher practices are not possible, do not abandon spiritual life. Perform legitimate actions, offer their results to Bhagavān, receive them as prasāda, and gradually allow the mind to become purer.
key_verses.more_stories:
1. Story of Govardhana Pūjā (Bhāgavata Purāṇa)
In Vṛndāvana, people lived close to the land, cows, forests, rain, and mountains. Their prosperity came through a web of visible and invisible blessings.
When the time came for worship, Kṛṣṇa redirected their attention to Govardhana. The mountain nourished their cows, gave grass, water, shade, fruits, and shelter. Their life was not separate from this sacred order.
The people prepared great quantities of food and offered everything with reverence.
After the offering, they did not discard the food. They received it back as prasāda.
This is a beautiful model of karma-phala-tyāga. We work, prepare, earn, cook, and receive. But before enjoying, we offer. Then the same result becomes sacred.
2. Story of Mahābali and Vāmana (Bhāgavata Purāṇa)
Mahābali was a powerful king who had gained great prosperity through effort, courage, and sacrifice. When Vāmana, the dwarf brāhmaṇa, came to him and asked for three steps of land, Bali agreed.
Śukrācārya warned him that this was no ordinary brāhmaṇa. This was Bhagavān Himself.
Bali understood the danger. Giving the promise might cost him everything. Yet he did not withdraw.
Vāmana grew into Trivikrama and covered the worlds in two steps. For the third, Bali offered his own head.
This was no longer ordinary giving. Bali released ownership over everything he had gained — kingdom, power, wealth, and finally himself.
His story shows the deeper direction of karma-phala-tyāga. What we call “mine” is gradually recognized as belonging to Bhagavān.
3. Story of the Fruit Seller and Kṛṣṇa (Bhāgavata Purāṇa)
One day in Vṛndāvana, a fruit seller came calling through the streets. Little Kṛṣṇa heard her and came running with tiny hands full of grain to exchange for fruit.
But as He ran, most of the grains slipped through His fingers and fell to the ground.
When He reached her, only a few grains remained.
The fruit seller saw the child’s face and her heart melted. She filled His hands with fruits anyway.
When she later looked into her basket, it was filled with jewels.
The outer exchange was small. But the inner offering was complete.
This story shows that Bhagavān does not measure the material value of what is offered. When the heart offers, the result becomes prasāda.
4. Story of Purandara Dāsa
Purandara Dāsa was once known as Śrīnivāsa Nāyaka, a wealthy but miserly man. His life was centered on possession and calculation.
Through a divine incident, his heart was shaken open. He saw the emptiness of hoarding and the higher value of devotion.
He gave up his pride in wealth and dedicated his life to Bhagavān.
From then on, his songs became offerings. His talent, time, voice, and life were no longer held as personal property. They became service to Bhagavān.
His transformation shows how the attitude toward wealth and results can change. What was once “mine” becomes an offering.
5. Story of Janābāī and Viṭṭhala
Janābāī lived a simple life of service. Her daily work included grinding grain, cleaning, and performing household tasks.
But inwardly, she did not see these as ordinary burdens. She remembered Viṭṭhala while working.
In the stories of her devotion, Viṭṭhala Himself is said to have joined her in daily chores, helping her grind grain and complete household work.
The meaning is beautiful: when work is connected to Bhagavān, even simple labor becomes worship.
Janābāī did not need a grand ritual to offer her life. She offered the result of daily work through remembrance and devotion.
Her life shows that karma-phala-tyāga can begin in the kitchen, courtyard, workplace, and every ordinary corner of life.

rich_grammar.padacchedah:
अथ एतत् अपि अशक्तः असि कर्तुम् मद्योगम् आश्रितः ।
सर्वकर्मफलत्यागम् ततः कुरु यतात्मवान् ॥
rich_grammar.pratipadarthah: अथ | (atha) = if / now / then एतत् | (etat) = this अपि | (api) = even अशक्तः | (aśaktaḥ) = unable असि | (asi) = you are कर्तुम् | (kartum) = to do मद्योगम् | (madyogam) = My yoga / My worship आश्रितः | (āśritaḥ) = having taken refuge in सर्वकर्मफलत्यागम् | (sarvakarmaphalatyāgam) = renunciation of the results of all actions ततः | (tataḥ) = then कुरु | (kuru) = do यतात्मवान् | (yatātmavān) = with self-mastery / self-restraint
rich_grammar.anvayah: अथ एतत् अपि कर्तुम् अशक्तः असि, मद्योगम् आश्रितः ततः यतात्मवान् सर्वकर्मफलत्यागम् कुरु ।
rich_grammar.sandhi: अथ + एतत् = अथैतत् | Sandhi name: वृद्धिसन्धिः; final अ combines with initial ए to form ऐ. | एतत् + अपि = एतदपि | Sandhi name: व्यञ्जनसन्धिः / जश्त्वसन्धिः; final त् becomes द् before the following vowel. | अपि + अशक्तः = अप्यशक्तः | Sandhi name: यण् सन्धिः; final इ becomes य् before the following vowel अ. | अशक्तः + असि = अशक्तोऽसि | Sandhi name: विसर्गसन्धिः; final अः becomes ओ before अ, with अवग्रहः marking the elided अ. | कर्तुम् + मद्योगम् = कर्तुं मद्योगम् | Sandhi name: अनुस्वारादेशः; final म् is represented as अनुस्वारः before the following consonant म. | मद्योगम् + आश्रितः = मद्योगमाश्रितः | Sandhi name: संहितायाम् पदसन्धिः; final म् joins directly with the following vowel आ without special change. | सर्वकर्मफलत्यागम् + ततः = सर्वकर्मफलत्यागं ततः | Sandhi name: अनुस्वारादेशः; final म् is represented as अनुस्वारः before the following consonant त. | ततः + कुरु = ततः कुरु | Sandhi name: विसर्गसन्धिः; visarga remains before the hard consonant क.
rich_grammar.samasa: मद्योगम् = षष्ठी तत्पुरुष समासः | Breakdown: मम योगः — My yoga / My worship. | सर्वकर्मफलत्यागम् = षष्ठी तत्पुरुष समासः | Breakdown: सर्वेषां कर्मणां फलानां त्यागः — renunciation of the fruits of all actions. | यतात्मवान् = बहुव्रीहि sense | Breakdown: यतः आत्मा यस्य सः — one whose self/mind is restrained.
rich_grammar.other: कर्तुम् is a तुमुनन्त-कृदन्तम् from कृ धातुः, meaning “to do.” | आश्रितः is a क्तप्रत्ययान्त-कृदन्तम् from आ-श्रि धातुः, meaning “having taken refuge in.” | अशक्तः is a क्तप्रत्ययान्त form with negative prefix अ-, meaning “unable.” | कुरु is लोट्-लकारः, मध्यमपुरुषः, एकवचनम् from कृ धातुः, used as an instruction. | यतात्मवान् carries the sense of self-restraint and discipline.

key_verses.final_takeaway: Even when desires remain, spiritual life is possible. Pursue legitimate goals through dharmic means, offer the results to Bhagavān first, and receive them back as prasāda. This simple shift in attitude begins the transformation.

key_verses.title: The Ladder of Spiritual Growth
key_verses.sanskrit:
श्रेयो हि ज्ञानमभ्यासाज्(त्) ज्ञानाद्ध्यानं(वँ) विशिष्यते ।
ध्यानात्कर्मफलत्यागः(स्) त्यागाच्छान्तिरनन्तरम् ॥
key_verses.transliteration:
śreyo hi jñānamabhyāsāj(t) jñānāddhyānaṁ(v̐) viśiṣyate ।
dhyānātkarmaphalatyāgaḥ(s) tyāgācchāntiranantaram ॥
key_verses.one_line_meaning: Bhagavān Kṛṣṇa concludes the discussion of sādhana by saying that knowledge is better than mechanical practice, meditation with knowledge is better than mere knowledge, and karma-phala-tyāga is specially praised because renunciation immediately brings peace.
key_verses.concise_journey: Bhagavān Kṛṣṇa has now completed the teaching of the five stages of bhakti-yoga. Bhakti-yoga is not one separate practice; it includes the whole range of spiritual disciplines — karma-yoga, upāsanā-yoga, and jñāna-yoga. In the previous verses, these were presented as a ladder suited to different levels of maturity.
key_verses.meaning_detail:
Bhagavān Kṛṣṇa has now completed the teaching of the five stages of bhakti-yoga. Bhakti-yoga is not one separate practice; it includes the whole range of spiritual disciplines — karma-yoga, upāsanā-yoga, and jñāna-yoga. In the previous verses, these were presented as a ladder suited to different levels of maturity.
In this verse, Kṛṣṇa gives a concluding comparison using four terms: abhyāsa, jñānam, dhyānam, and karma-phala-tyāga. Here abhyāsa means meditation or practice done mechanically, without clear understanding. It is still better than doing nothing, but it is the lowest in this particular list because the person may not know the meaning or significance of what is being practiced.
jñānam is better than such mechanical practice because it gives understanding. Here it means knowledge without meditation — one may understand what Bhagavān is through śāstra, but may not yet dwell upon that understanding. dhyānam is better than mere knowledge because it is knowledge combined with meditation; after understanding Bhagavān properly, we dwell upon that understanding and internalize it.
Then Kṛṣṇa says dhyānāt karma-phala-tyāgaḥ — karma-phala-tyāga is better than meditation. This can seem confusing because earlier karma-phala-tyāga was presented as the lowest step in the ladder. The point here is not to overturn the whole ladder. This is a deliberate praise of karma-phala-tyāga to encourage those who must begin there. Even at the beginning level, when we offer the result to Bhagavān and receive it as prasāda, peace comes immediately.
The last statement is the key: tyāgāt śāntir anantaram — after renunciation, peace follows immediately. When we hold results with egoistic ownership, the mind becomes anxious, proud, disappointed, or bitter. When we offer the result to Bhagavān and receive it as prasāda, the burden reduces, and peace begins.
key_verses.story:
In the Mahābhārata, there is a famous story of Kauśika and Dharma-vyādha.
Kauśika was a brāhmaṇa who had performed austerities and gained great spiritual pride. One day, while he was sitting under a tree, a bird disturbed him. In anger, he looked at it, and through the power of his tapas, the bird was burned.
Kauśika became aware of his power and felt inwardly proud.
Later, he went to a house seeking bhikṣā. The lady of the house was busy serving her husband and household duties, so she did not come out immediately. Kauśika became angry.
When she came out, she calmly said, “Do not look at me as you looked at the bird.”
Kauśika was shocked. How did she know?
She explained that true spiritual maturity is not mere power or outer austerity. She had gained inner clarity by doing her duties with dedication, self-control, and freedom from selfish agitation. She then directed him to Dharma-vyādha, a butcher in Mithilā.
Kauśika was surprised. How could a butcher teach dharma?
But when he met Dharma-vyādha, he found a person of great wisdom, humility, and inner peace. Dharma-vyādha performed his inherited duty without arrogance, served his parents with devotion, lived truthfully, and did his work without inner selfishness.
Kauśika learned that spiritual growth is not measured merely by external practice. Mechanical austerity without understanding and inner transformation is incomplete. Knowledge is important. Meditation upon that knowledge is important. And action done with the right attitude — free from egoistic clinging — brings peace.
This story beautifully reflects the teaching of 12.12. Practice must mature into understanding, understanding must be internalized, and action must become free from grasping.
key_verses.real_life_example:
Many of us practice things mechanically. We may chant, pray, attend classes, perform rituals, or repeat habits without understanding their meaning. Such practice is not useless, but it may not transform us deeply if it remains mechanical.
On the other side, some of us collect knowledge. We listen to talks, read books, and understand concepts intellectually. But if that understanding is not meditated upon and assimilated, it remains in the head and does not change how we live.
This verse asks us to see the progression clearly. Practice should gain understanding. Understanding should become reflection and meditation. And even in ordinary life, we should reduce our clinging to results.
In daily life, peace is often lost because we hold tightly to outcomes — marks, promotions, appreciation, success, status, children’s achievements, health reports, or social recognition. Karma-phala-tyāga teaches us to act sincerely, offer the result to Bhagavān, receive it as prasāda, and reduce the mental agitation around outcomes.
key_verses.reflection:
Am I doing any spiritual practice mechanically without understanding its meaning?
Can I act sincerely while reducing my anxiety and ownership over the result?

kids_content.explanation_script:
“Suppose you sing a song without knowing its meaning. That is okay, but it becomes better when you understand the meaning.
Then, if you sing it again and again while thinking about the meaning, it becomes even better.
Kṛṣṇa is saying something like that. Practice is good. Understanding is better. Thinking deeply about what we understand is even better. And when we stop worrying too much about the result and offer it to Bhagavān, we become peaceful.”
kids_content.story:
A child was learning a prayer.
At first, the child repeated the words because everyone else was saying them. The child did not know what the words meant.
One day, the parent explained the meaning.
The next time the child prayed, it felt different. The words were no longer just sounds.
Then the child began to think about the meaning while praying. The prayer became calm and beautiful.
After praying, the child had a test at school. The child studied well and said, “I will do my best and offer the result to Bhagavān.”
The child felt peaceful.
The work was done. The result was left to Bhagavān.
kids_content.reflection:
Do you know the meaning of the prayers you say?
Can you do your best and then stay calm about the result?

key_verses.detailed_meaning:
Step 1: Practice Without Understanding
Phrase: abhyāsāt = than practice / repeated practice
Here abhyāsa means mechanical practice or meditation without clear understanding. It is better than no practice, but it is still limited when it is only repetition without knowledge.
Step 2: Knowledge Is Better Than Mechanical Practice
Phrase: śreyo hi jñānamabhyāsāt = knowledge is indeed better than practice
Understanding Bhagavān through śāstra is superior to merely repeating a practice without knowing its meaning. Knowledge gives direction and removes mechanical dullness.
Step 3: Meditation Is Better Than Mere Knowledge
Phrase: jñānāddhyānaṁ viśiṣyate = meditation is superior to knowledge
Here jñānam means knowledge without meditation. Dhyānam means knowledge combined with meditation. When we understand and then dwell upon that understanding, the teaching is internalized.
Step 4: Karma-phala-tyāga Is Specially Praised
Phrase: dhyānātkarmaphalatyāgaḥ = renunciation of the results of actions is superior to meditation
This statement is a deliberate glorification of karma-phala-tyāga to encourage those who must begin at this level. It should not be taken as overturning the earlier ladder where karma-phala-tyāga is the entry step. The point is that even this beginning practice is powerful and should not be dismissed.
Step 5: What Karma-phala-tyāga Means
Phrase: karmaphalatyāgaḥ = renunciation of the fruits of action
This means dedicating all karma-phala to Bhagavān and then receiving it back as īśvara-prasāda. We do not throw away the result. We give up egoistic ownership and enjoy or use the result with gratitude and balance.
Step 6: Immediate Peace
Phrase: tyāgācchāntiranantaram = from renunciation, peace comes immediately
When the result is held as “mine,” the mind becomes restless. When the result is offered to Bhagavān and received as prasāda, there is immediate peace. The burden of ownership is reduced.
key_verses.more_stories:
1. Story of Satyakāma Jābāla (Chāndogya Upaniṣad)
Satyakāma Jābāla once approached Gautama Ṛṣi with the desire to learn brahmavidyā.
The teacher asked him about his family lineage. Satyakāma did not know. He could have invented an impressive answer to gain admission, but he did not. He returned to his mother, Jābālā, and asked her the truth.
She told him honestly that she did not know his father’s lineage. Satyakāma went back to Gautama and repeated the truth exactly.
Gautama immediately recognized his qualification. “Only a true brāhmaṇa would speak with such honesty,” he said, and accepted him as a student.
But the teaching did not begin with a lecture. Gautama gave him cattle and sent him to the forest, asking him to return only when the herd had multiplied.
Satyakāma lived in the forest for a long time, patiently tending the cattle. What looked like ordinary work became discipline. His mind became quiet, simple, and prepared.
Then teachings began to come to him through the very order of life — through the bull, fire, swan, and water bird. Each teaching revealed one aspect of brahman.
When Satyakāma finally returned, Gautama saw the glow of understanding on his face.
This story beautifully shows the movement in 12.12. Practice prepares the mind. Knowledge gives understanding. That knowledge must be assimilated. Spiritual growth is not mechanical repetition; it is a maturation from discipline to understanding to contemplative assimilation.
2. Story of Śravaṇa Kumāra
Śravaṇa Kumāra’s parents were blind and elderly. They longed to go on pilgrimage, but they could not travel on their own.
Śravaṇa did not treat them as a burden.
He placed them in two baskets, tied them to a pole, and carried them on his shoulders. Through forests, dusty paths, and difficult terrain, he walked with patience and devotion.
There was no audience applauding him. No one was offering him reward. No one was recording his sacrifice.
His service was quiet.
When his parents were thirsty, he went to fetch water. That simple act of service became the final act of his life.
Śravaṇa’s story shows action done without demand for personal reward. The peace of such action does not come from applause. It comes from the purity of the offering itself.
This reflects the spirit of karma-phala-tyāga. When action is done as duty and offering, and not as a bargain for recognition, the mind becomes peaceful.
3. Story of Queen Madālasā (Mārkaṇḍeya Purāṇa)
Queen Madālasā was not an ordinary mother. When her children were born, she did not merely think, “My child should become powerful, wealthy, and successful.”
She saw a deeper possibility.
As she rocked her infant child, she sang a lullaby — but it was not a lullaby of worldly ambition. She sang the truth of the Self.
She reminded the child, “You are pure. You are not merely this body. You are not bound by the identities people place upon you.”
To others, it may have looked like a mother singing to a baby. But her words carried knowledge.
This is the difference between mechanical action and action guided by understanding. Anyone can sing to a child. But Madālasā’s song became sacred because it carried knowledge.
Her story reflects the teaching that jñānam is superior to mere abhyāsa. Repetition becomes powerful when it is filled with meaning. Even a lullaby becomes a vehicle of wisdom when the intellect understands the truth.
4. Story of Prahlāda Teaching the Asura Boys (Bhāgavata Purāṇa)
Prahlāda was born in the house of Hiraṇyakaśipu, surrounded by an atmosphere hostile to devotion. His teachers tried to train him in politics, power, and worldly ambition.
But Prahlāda’s heart was absorbed in Bhagavān.
One day, when the teachers were away, the other asura boys gathered around him. They were young, playful, and not used to spiritual instruction. Prahlāda could have joined their games or stayed silent to avoid trouble.
Instead, he gently began to teach.
He told them that life should not be wasted only in sense pleasures and power struggles. Even in childhood, one should turn toward Bhagavān. He spoke not as someone repeating memorized words, but as one who had deeply assimilated devotion.
The boys listened.
Prahlāda’s teaching shows the difference between information and internalized knowledge. He did not merely know about Bhagavān. He lived in that understanding. His knowledge had become contemplation and devotion.
This reflects jñānād dhyānaṁ viśiṣyate — meditation and assimilation are superior to knowledge that remains only in the head.
5. Story of the Devotee Offering the First Harvest
In a traditional village, a farmer worked hard through the season. He ploughed the field, sowed seeds, waited for rain, protected the crop, and harvested with effort.
When the first grain was gathered, his children expected the family to cook and eat immediately.
But the farmer said, “First, we offer.”
He took a portion of the first harvest to the temple and placed it before Bhagavān with gratitude. Only after that did the family cook and eat.
The children asked, “Did Bhagavān take the grain?”
The farmer smiled and said, “Bhagavān gave us the land, rain, sun, strength, and time. We offer first so that we do not forget.”
The grain came back as prasāda.
The family still ate. The result was not wasted. But the attitude changed. The harvest was no longer only “our achievement.” It was received as grace.
This story shows tyāgācchāntiranantaram. When the result is offered and received as prasāda, peace comes immediately.

rich_grammar.padacchedah:
श्रेयः हि ज्ञानम् अभ्यासात् ज्ञानात् ध्यानम् विशिष्यते ।
ध्यानात् कर्मफलत्यागः त्यागात् शान्तिः अनन्तरम् ॥
rich_grammar.pratipadarthah: श्रेयः | (śreyaḥ) = better / superior हि | (hi) = indeed ज्ञानम् | (jñānam) = knowledge अभ्यासात् | (abhyāsāt) = than practice / from practice ज्ञानात् | (jñānāt) = than knowledge ध्यानम् | (dhyānam) = meditation विशिष्यते | (viśiṣyate) = is superior / is distinguished ध्यानात् | (dhyānāt) = than meditation कर्मफलत्यागः | (karmaphalatyāgaḥ) = renunciation of the results of actions त्यागात् | (tyāgāt) = from renunciation शान्तिः | (śāntiḥ) = peace अनन्तरम् | (anantaram) = immediately / without delay
rich_grammar.anvayah: अभ्यासात् ज्ञानम् हि श्रेयः । ज्ञानात् ध्यानम् विशिष्यते । ध्यानात् कर्मफलत्यागः । त्यागात् अनन्तरम् शान्तिः ।
rich_grammar.sandhi: ज्ञानम् + अभ्यासात् = ज्ञानमभ्यासात् | Sandhi name: संहितायाम् पदसन्धिः; final म् joins directly with the following vowel अ without special change. | अभ्यासात् + ज्ञानात् = अभ्यासाज्ज्ञानात् | Sandhi name: व्यञ्जनसन्धिः / जश्त्वसन्धिः; final त् becomes ज् before the soft consonant ज्. | ज्ञानात् + ध्यानम् = ज्ञानाद्ध्यानम् | Sandhi name: व्यञ्जनसन्धिः / जश्त्वसन्धिः; final त् becomes द् before ध्, and the consonants combine as द्ध्. | ध्यानात् + कर्मफलत्यागः = ध्यानात्कर्मफलत्यागः | Sandhi name: व्यञ्जनसन्धिः; final त् joins with क् without change. | कर्मफलत्यागः + त्यागात् = कर्मफलत्यागस्त्यागात् | Sandhi name: विसर्गसन्धिः; final visarga becomes स् before the dental consonant त्. | त्यागात् + शान्तिः = त्यागाच्छान्तिः | Sandhi name: व्यञ्जनसन्धिः; final त् before श् changes to च्, and ś becomes छ् in the combined form. | शान्तिः + अनन्तरम् = शान्तिरनन्तरम् | Sandhi name: विसर्गसन्धिः; final visarga after इ changes to र् before the vowel अ.
rich_grammar.samasa: कर्मफलत्यागः = षष्ठी तत्पुरुष समासः | Breakdown: कर्मणां फलानां त्यागः — renunciation of the results of actions.
rich_grammar.other: विशिष्यते is लट्-लकारः, प्रथमपुरुषः, एकवचनम्, आत्मनेपदम् / कर्मणि प्रयोगः from वि-शिष् धातुः, meaning “is superior” or “is distinguished.” | अभ्यासात्, ज्ञानात्, ध्यानात्, and त्यागात् are पञ्चमी-विभक्ति forms used for comparison or source. | अनन्तरम् is used adverbially; this is an अव्ययीभाववत् प्रयोगः in the sense of “immediately” or “without interval.” | कर्मफलत्यागः is the subject of the implied comparison in the third pāda. | शान्तिः is प्रथमा-एकवचनम् and is the result stated in the final pāda.

key_verses.final_takeaway: Practice gains depth through understanding, understanding through meditation, and every level benefits from offering results to Bhagavān. When we stop clinging to outcomes and receive life as prasāda, peace follows immediately.

key_verses.title: Qualities of the Beloved Devotee
key_verses.sanskrit:
अद्वेष्टा सर्वभूतानां(म्) मैत्रः(क्) करुण एव च ।
निर्ममो निरहङ्कारः(स्) समदुःखसुखः क्षमी ॥
key_verses.transliteration:
adveṣṭā sarvabhūtānāṁ(m) maitraḥ(k) karuṇa eva ca ।
nirmamo nirahaṅkāraḥ(s) samaduḥkhasukhaḥ kṣamī ॥
key_verses.one_line_meaning: Bhagavān Kṛṣṇa begins describing the parā-bhakta as one who has no hatred toward any being, is friendly and compassionate, free from possessiveness and ego, balanced in pleasure and pain, and forgiving.
key_verses.concise_journey:
From this verse onward, Bhagavān Kṛṣṇa describes the nature of the mature devotee — the parā-bhakta, the one who has gone through the spiritual disciplines and discovered the highest truth. This is no longer a description of an initial practice, but the character of one whose mind has been transformed.
The first quality is adveṣṭā sarvabhūtānām — one who has no hatred toward any being.
key_verses.meaning_detail:
From this verse onward, Bhagavān Kṛṣṇa describes the nature of the mature devotee — the parā-bhakta, the one who has gone through the spiritual disciplines and discovered the highest truth. This is no longer a description of an initial practice, but the character of one whose mind has been transformed.
The first quality is adveṣṭā sarvabhūtānām — one who has no hatred toward any being. This does not mean we should passively tolerate wrong actions. If correction is required, appropriate action may be taken. But the action should not be motivated by hatred, revenge, or the desire to hurt. Misbehavior may require correction, but the person does not require hatred. Even punishment, when unavoidable, should be guided by clarity and concern, not hatred.
The next qualities are maitraḥ and karuṇaḥ — friendly and compassionate. A mature devotee does not live with a hostile vision of the world. There is a basic goodwill toward all beings. When others suffer, compassion naturally arises. This friendliness and compassion are not selective; they flow from the understanding that all beings are included in the same total order of Bhagavān.
Then Bhagavān says nirmamaḥ and nirahaṅkāraḥ. Nirmamaḥ means free from possessiveness — the constant “mine-making” tendency. Nirahaṅkāraḥ means free from egoistic self-importance. When “I” and “mine” loosen, the mind becomes lighter and less defensive.
Finally, such a devotee is samaduḥkhasukhaḥ and kṣamī — balanced in sorrow and joy, and forgiving. Since the mind is not centered on ego and possessiveness, pleasure and pain do not throw it violently up and down. And because there is no hatred, forgiveness becomes natural.
key_verses.story:
In the Mahābhārata, there is a powerful moment after the war that shows the spirit of adveṣṭā sarvabhūtānām.
After the terrible war of Kurukṣetra, the Pāṇḍavas had lost almost everything dear to them. Sons, relatives, teachers, friends, and warriors had fallen. The victory was heavy with grief.
Then came one more wound. Aśvatthāman, in anger and revenge, killed the sleeping sons of Draupadī. These children were innocent. Draupadī’s grief was unbearable.
Aśvatthāman was captured and brought before her. Everyone expected Draupadī to demand punishment. She had every reason to burn with hatred. Her children had been taken away unjustly.
But in that moment of great sorrow, Draupadī saw beyond her own pain.
She remembered that Aśvatthāman was the son of Droṇa. If he were killed, his mother Kṛpī would suffer the same agony that Draupadī herself was suffering. Draupadī did not approve of his action. She did not say that he had done no wrong. But she refused to let hatred become her response.
Her heart, even in grief, made room for another mother’s sorrow.
This is not weakness. It is greatness.
Draupadī’s response shows how one may condemn adharma without hatred toward the person. Pain was there. Injustice was there. Appropriate action was needed. But hatred did not rule her heart.
This beautifully reflects adveṣṭā, karuṇaḥ, and kṣamī.
key_verses.real_life_example:
In modern life, hatred has become very easy to justify. We may hate people because of politics, religion, family conflict, workplace rivalry, social media arguments, or personal hurt. Sometimes we think hatred gives us strength, but it usually burns our own mind first.
This verse teaches a more mature response. We do not have to approve of wrong behavior. We do not have to become passive. We can correct, oppose, protect, punish, or set boundaries when needed. But hatred need not be the inner fuel.
This is especially important in parenting, leadership, teaching, and social life. A parent may correct a child. A manager may correct an employee. A society may punish a criminal. But if the action is driven by revenge, anger, and ego, it creates more damage. If action is guided by clarity, fairness, and concern for correction, it becomes dhārmic.
The verse also challenges our possessiveness and ego. Many emotional reactions come from “my people,” “my success,” “my insult,” “my opinion,” “my side.” When this grip loosens, the mind becomes more balanced and forgiving.
key_verses.reflection:
Can I correct wrong behavior without hatred toward the person?
Where do “I” and “mine” make me defensive, possessive, or unforgiving?

kids_content.explanation_script:
“Suppose someone makes a mistake or behaves badly. We may have to tell them it is wrong. But we do not have to hate them.
Kṛṣṇa is saying that a good devotee does not keep hatred in the heart. Such a person is friendly, kind, and forgiving. That does not mean they allow wrong things. It means they correct with care, not with hatred.”
kids_content.story:
A child had a friend who broke a favorite pencil.
At first, the child became very angry and thought, “I will never talk to him again!”
Then the friend came and said, “I am sorry. I did not mean to break it.”
The child still felt sad, but slowly said, “You should be careful next time. But I forgive you.”
The friend felt relieved.
The pencil was broken, but the friendship was not broken.
Kṛṣṇa is teaching us to correct what is wrong, but not fill our heart with hatred.
kids_content.reflection:
Have you ever forgiven someone who made a mistake?
Can you say something is wrong without hating the person?

key_verses.detailed_meaning:
Step 1: The Mature Devotee
Phrase: adveṣṭā = one who is free from hatred
Bhagavān Kṛṣṇa now begins describing the parā-bhakta. The first quality is absence of hatred. This does not mean absence of discrimination or absence of correction. It means hatred is not the inner attitude.
Step 2: Universal Scope
Phrase: sarvabhūtānām = toward all beings
No being should be kept in a private list of hatred. The scope is universal. We may respond differently to different behaviors, but hatred toward the being is not retained.
Step 3: Positive Goodwill
Phrase: maitraḥ = friendly
The absence of hatred is not cold indifference. It becomes a positive attitude of friendliness and goodwill. The mature devotee is not inwardly hostile to the world.
Step 4: Compassion
Phrase: karuṇa eva ca = and compassionate indeed
Compassion means sensitivity to the pain and struggle of others. Even when correction is required, the attitude is not retaliation but concern for what is right and healing.
Step 5: Freedom from Possessiveness and Ego
Phrase: nirmamo nirahaṅkāraḥ = free from “mine” and free from ego
Possessiveness and ego are major causes of hurt and hatred. When “mine” and “I am the doer / I am superior” are weakened, the mind becomes less reactive and more open.
Step 6: Balance and Forgiveness
Phrase: samaduḥkhasukhaḥ kṣamī = balanced in sorrow and joy, forgiving
Such a person is not violently tossed between pleasure and pain. Because the ego is light and hatred is absent, forgiveness becomes possible. The person can respond to life without being inwardly poisoned.
key_verses.more_stories:
1. Story of Draupadī and Aśvatthāman (Mahābhārata)
After the war, Draupadī had already suffered beyond measure. She had seen humiliation, exile, war, and loss. Then came the unbearable news that her sons had been killed while asleep by Aśvatthāman.
Her grief was deep and raw.
When Aśvatthāman was captured and brought before her, the moment was charged with pain. Everyone knew what he had done. Everyone knew that Draupadī had the right to be angry.
But Draupadī looked beyond revenge.
She remembered Kṛpī, the mother of Aśvatthāman. If he were killed, another mother would weep as she was weeping. Draupadī did not excuse the crime, but she refused to let hatred become her response.
This is extraordinary compassion in the middle of suffering.
Her heart had room for justice, grief, and mercy at the same time. This is adveṣṭā, karuṇaḥ, and kṣamī in a living form.
2. Story of Vidura’s Compassionate Counsel (Mahābhārata)
Vidura lived in the Kuru court, surrounded by ambition, jealousy, and political blindness. He saw the growing adharma of Duryodhana and repeatedly warned Dhṛtarāṣṭra.
His words were often sharp, but never hateful.
He did not flatter wrongdoing. He did not remain silent out of fear. Again and again he spoke what was right, trying to protect the kingdom from destruction.
Yet he was not motivated by anger or revenge. His counsel came from concern — for the Pāṇḍavas, for the Kauravas, for Dhṛtarāṣṭra, and for dharma.
This is the subtlety of maitraḥ and karuṇaḥ. Friendliness does not always mean pleasant words. Compassion sometimes speaks firmly. But the inner motive is welfare, not hatred.
3. Story of Bhagavān Buddha and Aṅgulimāla
Aṅgulimāla was feared by everyone. He had become a violent robber, and people trembled at his name.
One day, Bhagavān Buddha walked calmly into the forest where Aṅgulimāla lived. The robber ran toward him, but strangely, he could not catch up, though Buddha appeared to be walking slowly.
Aṅgulimāla shouted, “Stop!”
Buddha replied, “I have stopped. You stop.”
Those words pierced him.
Buddha did not look at him with hatred. He saw the suffering and ignorance behind the violence. Through compassion and wisdom, he transformed Aṅgulimāla’s life.
Aṅgulimāla became a monk.
This story shows that hatred does not transform. Compassion guided by wisdom can transform even a violent person.
4. Story of Mahāvīra’s Forgiveness
Bhagavān Mahāvīra is remembered for extraordinary ahiṁsā and forgiveness. Many times he faced insult, hardship, and physical suffering, but he did not respond with hatred.
His discipline was not weakness. It was mastery over reaction.
When people misunderstood him or caused pain, he did not allow resentment to take root. His vision was that beings act under ignorance, fear, and bondage. Therefore, the response should not be hatred, but compassion and firmness in non-injury.
This reflects kṣamī — forgiveness and forbearance.
Forgiveness here does not mean denying wrong. It means refusing to poison oneself with hatred.
5. Story of Sant Eknāth and the Spitting Man
Sant Eknāth was once walking after taking a sacred bath. A man, wanting to provoke him, spat on him.
Eknāth did not become angry. He calmly went back, bathed again, and returned.
The man spat again.
This happened repeatedly. Each time, Eknāth remained calm and went back to bathe.
Finally, the man broke down. He could not understand such patience. He asked forgiveness.
Eknāth did not carry hatred toward him. His patience exposed the other person’s anger and transformed it.
This story shows samaduḥkhasukhaḥ and kṣamī. The insult did not make him lose balance. His forgiveness became a teaching.

rich_grammar.padacchedah:
अद्वेष्टा सर्वभूतानाम् मैत्रः करुणः एव च ।
निर्ममः निरहङ्कारः समदुःखसुखः क्षमी ॥
rich_grammar.pratipadarthah: अद्वेष्टा | (adveṣṭā) = one who does not hate सर्वभूतानाम् | (sarvabhūtānām) = toward all beings / of all beings मैत्रः | (maitraḥ) = friendly करुणः | (karuṇaḥ) = compassionate एव | (eva) = indeed च | (ca) = and निर्ममः | (nirmamaḥ) = free from possessiveness निरहङ्कारः | (nirahaṅkāraḥ) = free from egoism समदुःखसुखः | (samaduḥkhasukhaḥ) = equal in sorrow and joy क्षमी | (kṣamī) = forgiving
rich_grammar.anvayah: सः अद्वेष्टा सर्वभूतानाम्, मैत्रः, करुणः एव च, निर्ममः, निरहङ्कारः, समदुःखसुखः, क्षमी (भवति) ।
rich_grammar.sandhi: करुणः + एव = करुण एव | Sandhi name: विसर्गलोपः / विसर्गसन्धिः; final visarga is dropped before the following vowel ए. | निर्ममः + निरहङ्कारः = निर्ममो निरहङ्कारः | Sandhi name: विसर्गसन्धिः; final अः becomes ओ before the following soft consonant न. | निरहङ्कारः + समदुःखसुखः = निरहङ्कारः समदुःखसुखः | Sandhi name: विसर्गसन्धिः; visarga remains before the following hard sibilant स्. | समदुःखसुखः + क्षमी = समदुःखसुखः क्षमी | Sandhi name: विसर्गसन्धिः; visarga remains before the following hard consonant क्ष्.
rich_grammar.samasa: सर्वभूतानाम् = षष्ठी तत्पुरुष समासः | Breakdown: सर्वेषां भूतानाम् — of all beings. | निरहङ्कारः = नञ् तत्पुरुष समासः | Breakdown: न अहङ्कारः यस्य / अहङ्काररहितः — one who is free from egoism. | समदुःखसुखः = द्वन्द्वगर्भित बहुव्रीहि समासः | Breakdown: दुःखं च सुखं च = दुःखसुखे; तयोः समः यः सः — one who is equal in sorrow and joy. | निर्ममः = नञ् तत्पुरुष / बहुव्रीहि sense | Breakdown: ममकाररहितः — one who is free from possessiveness.
rich_grammar.other: अद्वेष्टा is a तृच्-प्रत्ययान्त-कर्तृवाचक-नामपदम् from द्विष् धातुः, with negative prefix अ-, meaning “one who does not hate.” | क्षमी is formed with the इनि/इन् sense, meaning “one endowed with forgiveness” or “forbearing.” | All descriptive words in this verse are प्रथमा-एकवचनम्, qualifying the implied devotee. | भवति is understood elliptically in the anvaya.

key_verses.final_takeaway: The mature devotee is free from hatred, friendly, compassionate, without possessiveness or ego, balanced in joy and sorrow, and forgiving. These qualities flow naturally from a heart purified by devotion and knowledge.

key_verses.title: Contentment and Firm Conviction
key_verses.sanskrit:
सन्तुष्टः(स्) सततं(य्ँ) योगी यतात्मा दृढनिश्चयः ।
मय्यर्पितमनोबुद्धिः(र्) र्यो मद्भक्तः(स्) स मे प्रियः ॥
key_verses.transliteration:
santuṣṭaḥ(s) satataṁ(y̐) yogī yatātmā dṛḍhaniścayaḥ ।
mayyarpitamanobuddhiḥ(r) yo madbhaktaḥ(s) sa me priyaḥ ॥
key_verses.one_line_meaning: Bhagavān Kṛṣṇa says that the devotee who is ever contented, always a yogī, self-disciplined, firm in conviction, and whose mind and intellect are offered to Him, is dear to Him.
key_verses.concise_journey: Bhagavān Kṛṣṇa continues describing the parā-bhakta — the highest devotee, who has gone through all the stages of bhakti-yoga and has reached jñānam. Here, yogī does not mean merely a person with mystical powers or extraordinary abilities. A real yogī is a jñānī — one who has clear knowledge.
key_verses.meaning_detail:
Bhagavān Kṛṣṇa continues describing the parā-bhakta — the highest devotee, who has gone through all the stages of bhakti-yoga and has reached jñānam. Here, yogī does not mean merely a person with mystical powers or extraordinary abilities. A real yogī is a jñānī — one who has clear knowledge. Siddhis may or may not be present; they are not the measure of liberation. What matters is jñānam.
Such a person is santuṣṭaḥ — contented. This contentment is not laziness or lack of effort. It is inner fullness born of understanding. Since the person is not inwardly dependent on external arrangements for completion, the mind is not constantly chasing something to become whole.
The devotee is also yatātmā — self-disciplined and integrated. Discipline is not required only before Vedānta; it is required during śravaṇam, during mananam, and during nididhyāsanam. Without integration, even listening steadily becomes difficult. A distracted, undisciplined mind cannot receive, retain, or assimilate the teaching properly.
Then comes dṛḍhaniścayaḥ — firm conviction. This conviction comes through systematic śravaṇam and mananam. The intellect naturally raises questions; until those questions are resolved, the teaching will not be fully assimilated. When doubts are removed, knowledge becomes firm.
Finally, Bhagavān says mayyarpitamanobuddhiḥ — the mind and intellect are offered to Me. The emotional personality and the rational, questioning intellect both rest in Bhagavān. Emotionally, the devotee can relate to Bhagavān as iṣṭa-devatā. Cosmically, the devotee can appreciate Bhagavān as Viśvarūpa. Intellectually, the devotee understands Bhagavān as arūpa, nirguṇa brahman, the substratum of all forms. These levels do not destroy one another; they are integrated. Such a devotee is not merely dear to Bhagavān — in the highest sense, there is no distance between Bhagavān and that jñānī.
key_verses.story:
Sant Jñāneśvar’s life beautifully reflects this verse.
Jñāneśvar was born into a family that faced severe social rejection. From a young age, he and his siblings went through humiliation and hardship. Yet his life did not become bitter. Instead, his mind turned deeply toward Bhagavān and toward the truth of Vedānta.
Still very young, Jñāneśvar composed the Jñāneśvarī, a profound commentary on the Bhagavad Gītā in Marathi. What is striking is not merely that he wrote a great text, but the spirit in which he taught. His heart was full of devotion, and his intellect was full of clarity.
He did not treat knowledge and devotion as separate compartments. For him, the wisdom of the Gītā flowed as devotion, poetry, compassion, and spiritual insight. His mind was offered to Bhagavān; his buddhi was also offered to Bhagavān.
At the end of the Jñāneśvarī, he composed the famous Pasāyadāna, a universal prayer asking not for private benefit, but for the welfare, wisdom, and upliftment of all beings.
This shows mayyarpitamanobuddhiḥ in a beautiful way. The heart is with Bhagavān. The intellect is illumined by Bhagavān. The life becomes a blessing to the world.
Jñāneśvar also shows dṛḍhaniścayaḥ. His conviction did not collapse under social difficulty. His contentment did not depend on outward approval. His knowledge and devotion remained steady.
Such a devotee is indeed dear to Bhagavān.
key_verses.real_life_example:
Many people today divide life into emotion and intellect. Some are devotional but become uncomfortable when questions arise. Others are intellectual but feel devotion is childish. This verse shows that the mature devotee integrates both.
Our mind needs a devotional connection — something that softens the heart, gives love, trust, and surrender. Our intellect needs clarity — it must understand why Bhagavān is not merely one being somewhere, but the truth of all forms and the substratum of everything.
This verse also speaks to modern restlessness. We often believe we will be content only after the next achievement, salary increase, relationship improvement, health report, or social recognition. But the parā-bhakta’s contentment comes from inner clarity and spiritual maturity.
Self-discipline is equally relevant. In an age of short attention spans, constant notifications, and scattered priorities, yatātmā becomes very important. Without discipline, even good intentions do not mature into wisdom.
key_verses.reflection:
Is my devotion supported by clear understanding, or do my emotions and intellect move in different directions?
Do I seek contentment from outer arrangements alone, or am I growing toward inner fullness?

kids_content.explanation_script:
“Sometimes we love someone with our heart, and sometimes we understand something with our thinking.
Kṛṣṇa is saying that a great devotee gives both the heart and the thinking to Bhagavān.
That means we love Bhagavān, and we also try to understand Bhagavān properly. Such a person becomes calm, steady, and content.”
kids_content.story:
A child loved visiting the temple.
The child liked the lamps, flowers, bells, and songs. One day, the child asked, “Is Bhagavān only in the temple?”
The parent smiled and said, “The temple helps your heart love Bhagavān. But your understanding should also grow. Bhagavān is not only in one place. Bhagavān is present everywhere.”
The child thought about this.
The next day, the child saw a tree, a river, a teacher, and a friend. The child remembered, “Bhagavān is connected to all of this.”
Now the child’s heart loved Bhagavān, and the child’s mind also began to understand.
kids_content.reflection:
Can you love Bhagavān and also try to understand Bhagavān?
Can you think of one place outside the temple where you can remember Bhagavān?

key_verses.detailed_meaning:
Step 1: Contentment
Phrase: santuṣṭaḥ = contented
The parā-bhakta is contented because the person has discovered inner fullness. This is not carelessness or inactivity. It is freedom from the constant feeling that something outside must complete me.
Step 2: Constant Abidance
Phrase: satataṁ yogī = always a yogī
Here yogī means jñānī. The word does not refer primarily to someone with siddhis or miraculous powers. A person may have powers and still remain a saṁsārī if knowledge is absent. The real yogī is the one who has jñānam.
Step 3: Self-Integration
Phrase: yatātmā = self-disciplined / self-integrated
The devotee is integrated at the level of body, senses, mind, and intellect. This discipline is required before Vedānta, during Vedānta śravaṇam, and after śravaṇam for assimilation. Without self-integration, the mind cannot remain available for knowledge.
Step 4: Firm Conviction
Phrase: dṛḍhaniścayaḥ = one with firm conviction
This means firm knowledge — sthira-prajñā. Such conviction is born through śravaṇam and mananam. First we listen systematically, giving the teaching time to unfold. Then, if doubts remain, we remove them through mananam. When the intellect is convinced, the knowledge becomes steady.
Step 5: Mind and Intellect Offered to Bhagavān
Phrase: mayyarpitamanobuddhiḥ = one whose mind and intellect are offered to Me
The mind represents the emotional personality, and the buddhi represents the rational, questioning intellect. Both are placed in Bhagavān. Emotionally, the devotee can relate to saguṇa Bhagavān. Intellectually, the devotee understands arūpa, nirguṇa brahman as the substratum of all forms.
Step 6: Dear to Bhagavān
Phrase: yo madbhaktaḥ sa me priyaḥ = that devotee of Mine is dear to Me
This devotee has integrated devotion, discipline, knowledge, conviction, mind, and intellect. Such a devotee is dear to Bhagavān. At the highest level, the jñānī does not stand apart from Bhagavān; the distance caused by ignorance has been removed.
key_verses.more_stories:
1. Story of Śrī Rāmānuja and the Sacred Mantra
Śrī Rāmānuja once longed to receive a sacred mantra from his teacher. This was not treated casually. Such a mantra was considered precious, and the student had to receive it with reverence, discipline, and readiness.
After much preparation, the teacher finally gave him the mantra. But along with it came a strict instruction: “Do not share this openly. It is sacred. If you reveal it carelessly, there will be serious consequences for you.”
Śrī Rāmānuja received the mantra with devotion. But as he walked away, he began thinking of ordinary people — people who struggled, suffered, and longed for Bhagavān but had no access to such teaching.
His heart could not keep quiet.
He climbed the temple tower and called out to the people below. Men, women, elders, and children gathered, wondering what he was about to say.
Then, with great compassion, Śrī Rāmānuja openly shared the mantra so that everyone could receive its blessing.
When his teacher heard this, he was deeply upset. “Do you realize what you have done? Do you know what consequence you may face?”
Śrī Rāmānuja replied, “If by my suffering many people can reach Bhagavān, I am willing to accept it.”
This was not impulsiveness. It came from a heart fully offered to Bhagavān and an intellect firmly convinced that Bhagavān’s grace should not be hoarded.
His mind was not centered on self-protection. His buddhi was not confused by fear. He had dṛḍha-niścayaḥ — firm conviction — and mayyarpita-mano-buddhiḥ — a mind and intellect dedicated to Bhagavān.
2. Story of Tirunāvukkarasar Appar and the Stone
Tirunāvukkarasar, known as Appar, lived with deep devotion to Bhagavān Śiva. But his devotion was not accepted by everyone. There were people who opposed him strongly and wanted to test or destroy him.
In one severe incident, Appar was tied to a heavy stone and thrown into the sea.
For an ordinary person, this would have been a moment of terror. The waves were vast. The stone was heavy. The body had no power against the sea.
But Appar’s mind did not run toward fear. His heart turned to Bhagavān Śiva.
Instead of panic, prayer arose. Instead of despair, devotion arose. He sang to Bhagavān with full trust.
Then the impossible happened: the stone floated.
Appar was carried safely by the very stone that was meant to drown him.
Whether we take the story as miracle, symbol, or devotional memory, its message is clear. Appar’s mind was not scattered by danger. His conviction did not collapse when life became threatening.
This is yatātmā and dṛḍha-niścayaḥ. The senses, mind, and emotions were gathered in Bhagavān. His mind and intellect were not handed over to fear; they were offered to Bhagavān.
3. Story of Akkā Mahādevī Leaving Worldly Security
Akkā Mahādevī was deeply devoted to Bhagavān Śiva, whom she loved as Cenna Mallikārjuna. Her devotion was not a small part of her life; it was the center of her being.
She lived in a world that expected her to accept comfort, status, and social approval. But her heart did not belong to these things. She saw clearly that worldly respect and security could not give the fullness she sought.
When pressure increased for her to live according to worldly expectations, she chose Bhagavān over comfort.
This was not a small choice. To walk away from protection, position, and approval requires extraordinary courage. People misunderstood her. Some judged her. Some thought her life was extreme.
But Akkā Mahādevī’s conviction was firm.
Her songs reveal a heart that had given itself completely to Bhagavān. She did not want half-devotion — Bhagavān on one side and worldly dependence on the other. She wanted only Cenna Mallikārjuna.
This shows santuṣṭaḥ and dṛḍha-niścayaḥ. Her contentment did not come from possessions or social approval. Her mind and intellect had been offered to Bhagavān, and that gave her the strength to stand apart from ordinary expectations.
4. Story of Kūrattāzhvān Protecting His Ācārya
Kūrattāzhvān was a close disciple of Śrī Rāmānuja. He was known not only for learning, but for humility, loyalty, and deep devotion.
At one point, danger came to Śrī Rāmānuja because of religious and political hostility. The situation was serious. It was not merely a debate; real harm could come.
Kūrattāzhvān understood the danger and stepped forward. Instead of protecting his own safety first, he chose to protect his guru and the sampradāya.
In the traditional accounts, he accepted great suffering, including the loss of his eyesight, rather than betray dharma or abandon his commitment.
What is striking is not only the sacrifice, but the inner steadiness after the sacrifice. He did not become bitter. He did not say, “Why did this happen to me?” He did not turn his suffering into complaint.
His devotion remained luminous.
His mind was offered to Bhagavān. His intellect was firm in its conviction. His body suffered, but his inner commitment did not break.
This is yatātmā, dṛḍha-niścayaḥ, and mayyarpita-mano-buddhiḥ in action.
5. Story of Sant Ravīdās and the Simple Offering
Sant Ravīdās lived as a cobbler. His work was simple, and society did not give him high status. But his heart was absorbed in Bhagavān.
One day, devotees were going for sacred worship and invited him to come. Ravīdās could not leave his work immediately. He had duties to complete, and he did not treat his work carelessly.
Yet his mind was with Bhagavān.
There is a well-known saying associated with him: if the mind is pure, even the small vessel at home becomes Gaṅgā. The meaning is not to dismiss sacred pilgrimage, but to show that purity of heart matters most.
Ravīdās did not think, “My life is too ordinary for Bhagavān.” He did not wait for outer status to become spiritual. His work, his songs, his devotion, and his humility all became offerings.
His contentment did not depend on society’s approval. His devotion did not depend on outer grandeur.
This is santuṣṭaḥ and mayyarpita-mano-buddhiḥ. The mind rests in Bhagavān, the intellect understands what truly matters, and even simple daily life becomes sacred.

rich_grammar.padacchedah:
सन्तुष्टः सततम् योगी यतात्मा दृढनिश्चयः ।
मयि अर्पितमनोबुद्धिः यः मद्भक्तः सः मे प्रियः ॥
rich_grammar.pratipadarthah: सन्तुष्टः | (santuṣṭaḥ) = contented सततम् | (satatam) = always / constantly योगी | (yogī) = yogī / jñānī यतात्मा | (yatātmā) = self-disciplined / self-integrated दृढनिश्चयः | (dṛḍhaniścayaḥ) = firm in conviction मयि | (mayi) = in Me अर्पितमनोबुद्धिः | (arpitamanobuddhiḥ) = one whose mind and intellect are offered यः | (yaḥ) = who मद्भक्तः | (madbhaktaḥ) = My devotee सः | (saḥ) = he मे | (me) = to Me / for Me प्रियः | (priyaḥ) = dear
rich_grammar.anvayah: यः सन्तुष्टः, सततम् योगी, यतात्मा, दृढनिश्चयः, मयि अर्पितमनोबुद्धिः, मद्भक्तः, सः मे प्रियः ।
rich_grammar.sandhi: सततम् + योगी = सततं योगी | Sandhi name: अनुस्वारादेशः; final म् is represented as अनुस्वारः before the following consonant य्. | मयि + अर्पित = मय्यर्पित | Sandhi name: यण् सन्धिः; final इ becomes य् before the following vowel अ. | बुद्धिः + यः = बुद्धिर्यः | Sandhi name: विसर्गसन्धिः; final visarga after इ changes to र् before the semivowel य्. | बुद्धिर्यः + मद्भक्तः = बुद्धिर्यो मद्भक्तः | Sandhi name: विसर्गसन्धिः within यः; यः before the soft consonant म् becomes यो. | मत् + भक्तः = मद्भक्तः | Sandhi name: व्यञ्जनसन्धिः / जश्त्वसन्धिः; final त् becomes द् before the voiced consonant भ्. | सः + मे = स मे | Sandhi name: विसर्गलोपः; final visarga is dropped before the following consonant म्.
rich_grammar.samasa: यतात्मा = बहुव्रीहि समासः | Breakdown: यतः आत्मा यस्य सः — one whose self/mind is disciplined. | दृढनिश्चयः = बहुव्रीहि समासः | Breakdown: दृढः निश्चयः यस्य सः — one whose conviction is firm. | अर्पितमनोबुद्धिः = बहुव्रीहि समासः | Breakdown: अर्पिते मनः बुद्धिः च यस्य सः — one whose mind and intellect are offered. | मद्भक्तः = षष्ठी तत्पुरुष समासः | Breakdown: मम भक्तः — My devotee.
rich_grammar.other: सन्तुष्टः is a क्तप्रत्ययान्त-कृदन्तम् from सम्-तुष् धातुः, meaning “contented” or “satisfied.” | अर्पित is a क्तप्रत्ययान्त-कृदन्तम् from अर्प् धातुः, meaning “offered” or “dedicated.” | भक्तः is a क्तप्रत्ययान्त form from भज् धातुः, meaning “devotee” or “one devoted.” | योगी is derived from योगिन् and appears here in प्रथमा-एकवचनम्. | All the descriptive expressions qualify the implied devotee, who is finally identified as madbhaktaḥ.

key_verses.final_takeaway: The devotee who integrates contentment, self-discipline, firm conviction, and both emotional and intellectual dedication to Bhagavān represents the highest expression of spiritual maturity. Such a person is truly dear to Bhagavān.

key_verses.title: Neither Disturbing nor Disturbed
key_verses.sanskrit:
यस्मान्नोद्विजते लोको(कः) लोकान्नोद्विजते च यः ।
हर्षामर्षभयोद्वेगैः(र्) र्मुक्तो यः(स्) स च मे प्रियः ॥
key_verses.transliteration:
yasmānnodvijate loko(ḥ) lokānnodvijate ca yaḥ ।
harṣāmarṣabhayodvegaiḥ(r) mukto yaḥ(s) sa ca me priyaḥ ॥
key_verses.one_line_meaning: Bhagavān Kṛṣṇa says that the devotee by whom the world is not disturbed, who is not disturbed by the world, and who is free from elation, intolerance, fear, and anxiety, is dear to Him.
key_verses.concise_journey: Bhagavān Kṛṣṇa continues describing the parā-bhakta, the highest devotee, who has gone through the stages of bhakti-yoga and has gained inner freedom. This freedom is not about escaping the world physically. It is inner freedom from the regular emotional disturbances that make the mind a burden.
key_verses.meaning_detail:
Bhagavān Kṛṣṇa continues describing the parā-bhakta, the highest devotee, who has gone through the stages of bhakti-yoga and has gained inner freedom. This freedom is not about escaping the world physically. It is inner freedom from the regular emotional disturbances that make the mind a burden.
The first mark of such a devotee is that the world is not disturbed by him. This means he is sensitive, considerate, and aware of the feelings of others. He does not deliberately hurt people. He is ahiṁsaka in attitude. Unknowingly, someone may still feel hurt, but his intention and way of living are not harmful.
The second mark is that he is not disturbed by the world. People may criticize, insult, misunderstand, or behave unfairly. Still, he does not collapse inwardly. His heart is tender like a flower when dealing with others, but firm like a diamond when receiving difficult experiences.
Because he does not hurt others, he is free from guilt. Because he does not get hurt by others, he is free from inner wound. Hurt and guilt are two powerful sources of mental disturbance, and the parā-bhakta is free from both.
Bhagavān then mentions four inner disturbances: harṣa, amarṣa, bhaya, and udvega. Harṣa is over-excitement or elation that clouds discrimination. Amarṣa is intolerance, impatience, or envy when others excel. Bhaya is fear born of dependence on insecure things. Udvega is mental disturbance, anxiety, or sorrow caused by these inner reactions. The parā-bhakta is free from these; therefore he is dear to Bhagavān.
key_verses.story:
In the life of Mahātmā Gāndhī, we see a powerful example of not disturbing the world and not being disturbed by the world.
Gāndhī faced criticism, insult, imprisonment, violence, and repeated misunderstanding. Many people opposed him strongly. Some mocked him, some attacked him, and some dismissed his methods as weakness.
Yet his central commitment was ahiṁsā. He did not want his freedom struggle to become a movement of hatred. He insisted that even when resisting injustice, one should not hate the person who commits injustice.
This was not passivity. Gāndhī opposed wrong firmly. He organized movements, accepted imprisonment, challenged unjust systems, and awakened millions. But he repeatedly taught that the method must not poison the heart. The struggle should not be driven by revenge.
When violence broke out among his own people, he was deeply pained. He fasted, prayed, and tried to awaken their conscience. He wanted freedom, but not at the cost of inner degradation.
This reflects the teaching of 12.15. The mature person does not disturb the world through hatred, aggression, or careless action. At the same time, he does not become inwardly shattered by insult, opposition, or suffering.
His heart is soft when dealing with others, but firm when receiving the blows of life.
key_verses.real_life_example:
In modern life, we often disturb others without noticing it — through harsh words, impatience, online comments, anger at home, workplace pressure, sarcasm, or careless behavior. This verse asks us to become sensitive to the effect we have on people around us.
At the same time, we are easily disturbed by the world. A message, a criticism, a social media comment, a delay, a comparison, or a small disrespect can upset us for hours. Our mind becomes dependent on how others behave.
This verse gives a two-sided discipline. First, let me not become a source of disturbance to others. Second, let me not allow every external situation to disturb me.
It also warns us about emotional extremes. Happiness is natural, but over-excitement can cloud judgment. Disagreement is natural, but intolerance becomes poisonous. Caution is natural, but fear becomes bondage when we lean psychologically on insecure things. Anxiety becomes constant when the mind depends on what is unstable.
The teaching is not to become cold or emotionless. It is to become inwardly free, alert, considerate, and steady.
key_verses.reflection:
Do others feel safe and undisturbed in my presence, or do my words and moods unsettle them?
Am I inwardly steady, or does every criticism, comparison, fear, and success disturb my mind?

kids_content.explanation_script:
“Suppose you are holding a glass of water. If someone bumps you, the water spills only if the glass is shaky.
Our mind is also like that. If the mind is very shaky, small things disturb us.
Kṛṣṇa is saying that a good devotee does not disturb others and also does not get disturbed easily. Such a person is kind to others and strong inside.”
kids_content.story:
A child was building a tower with blocks.
Another child accidentally bumped the table, and the tower fell.
At first, the child became angry and wanted to shout.
Then the teacher said, “Pause. Was it done on purpose?”
The child looked and saw that the other child was already sorry.
So the child took a deep breath and said, “It’s okay. Let’s build it again.”
This time, both children built the tower together.
The first child did not hurt the other child with angry words. And the child also did not stay upset for a long time.
That is what Kṛṣṇa is teaching — do not disturb others, and do not let every small thing disturb you.
kids_content.reflection:
Can you speak kindly even when something goes wrong?
What helps you calm down when you feel upset?

key_verses.detailed_meaning:
Step 1: The World Is Not Disturbed by Him
Phrase: yasmānnodvijate lokaḥ = by whom the world is not disturbed
The parā-bhakta is sensitive and considerate. He does not deliberately hurt others. He is ahiṁsaka in his attitude and conduct. People around him are not disturbed because of his presence, speech, or behavior.
Step 2: He Is Not Disturbed by the World
Phrase: lokānnodvijate ca yaḥ = and who is not disturbed by the world
The world may bring criticism, insult, difficulty, misunderstanding, or unpleasant experiences. Still, he does not get inwardly shaken. When handling others, he is soft like a flower; when receiving experiences, he is firm like a diamond.
Step 3: Freedom from Elation
Phrase: harṣa-muktaḥ = free from elation
Harṣa means over-excitement or elation. Happiness is not a problem, but when happiness becomes so intense that it clouds discrimination, it becomes harmful. The parā-bhakta does not allow even joy to rob him of viveka.
Step 4: Freedom from Amarṣa
Phrase: amarṣa-muktaḥ = free from intolerance / envy
Amarṣa means intolerance, impatience, or envy. It is the inability to appreciate another person’s excellence. The mature devotee is able to recognize good qualities in others without feeling diminished.
Step 5: Freedom from Fear
Phrase: bhaya-muktaḥ = free from fear
Bhaya comes when we psychologically lean on insecure things. Possessions, status, body, reputation, power, and relationships can all become sources of fear when we depend on them for inner security. The jñānī does not lean on them for self-worth.
Step 6: Freedom from Mental Disturbance
Phrase: udvega-muktaḥ = free from anxiety and disturbance
Udvega means mental disturbance, sorrow, agitation, or anxiety caused by these inner reactions. Since the parā-bhakta is free from harṣa, amarṣa, and bhaya, he is also free from the udvega they create.
Step 7: Dear to Bhagavān
Phrase: yaḥ sa ca me priyaḥ = such a one is dear to Me
Such a devotee is dear to Bhagavān because the mind is free from the disturbances of saṁsāra. This is the mark of parā-bhakti, advaita-bhakti, and jñāna-niṣṭhā.
key_verses.more_stories:
1. Story of Śrī Ramana Maharṣi and the Cow Lakṣmī
At the āśrama of Śrī Ramana Maharṣi, the cow Lakṣmī was not treated as an ordinary animal wandering near the premises.
She would come near Bhagavān with affection and trust. Devotees noticed that he responded to her with the same quiet attention he gave to human visitors.
One day, Lakṣmī approached him with a kind of familiarity that surprised newcomers. She stood close, and he gently received her presence. There was no irritation, no dismissal, no sense that an animal was an interruption.
Around him, squirrels, dogs, monkeys, birds, and cows moved with unusual ease. They seemed safe.
Human beings too came in many states — grieving, argumentative, confused, proud, or desperate. He did not create fear in them. His presence did not disturb.
And when his own body later suffered illness and pain, he did not turn that suffering into agitation for others. He remained inwardly steady.
This story reflects both sides of 12.15: the world was not disturbed by him, and he was not disturbed by the world.
2. Story of Sant Kabīr and the Angry Listeners
Sant Kabīr often sang in language that cut through religious pride. He did not flatter people’s identities. He challenged hollow ritual, empty scholarship, and social arrogance.
Because of this, some people became angry.
Once, while Kabīr sang in a public space, people from different communities objected to his words. Some felt he was criticizing their customs. Some felt exposed. Voices rose. The atmosphere became tense.
Kabīr did not respond with fear. Nor did he soften the truth merely to please the crowd.
But he also did not speak from hatred.
His purpose was not to insult. His purpose was to awaken. His words were sharp, but his heart was not violent.
He continued singing of the one Bhagavān beyond labels, beyond pride, beyond outer divisions.
This is a subtle form of lokānnodvijate. The world’s criticism did not shake him. At the same time, he did not disturb the world through personal hatred. If people were disturbed, it was because truth challenged their ego — not because Kabīr wished to hurt them.
3. Story of Samartha Rāmadās and the Insult
Samartha Rāmadās, the great devotee of Bhagavān Rāma, travelled widely, taught devotion, and encouraged strength rooted in dharma.
During his travels, not everyone received him with respect. Some tested him. Some mocked ascetics. Some spoke harshly.
In one remembered incident, when insult came his way, Rāmadās did not collapse into hurt pride. He did not respond with an outburst to defend his status. Instead, he remained composed and used the moment as teaching.
For him, insult was not a personal wound to be nursed. It was an opportunity to see whether the mind was leaning on public respect.
He continued his work.
This reflects freedom from udvega. When the mind does not depend on praise for its stability, insult loses much of its power. Such a person may still correct what is wrong, but does not become inwardly shattered.
4. Story of Sant Sakhūbāī Remembering Viṭṭhala
Sant Sakhūbāī lived in a difficult household. Her devotion to Viṭṭhala was deep, but those around her did not always understand it.
There were days when she longed to go to Paṇḍharpur and join the devotees of Viṭṭhala. But household burdens and harsh treatment stood in her way.
One day, the longing became intense. Her heart cried for Viṭṭhala.
In the devotional tradition, Bhagavān Himself is said to have responded to her suffering, taking care of her duties so that she could go for darśana.
Whether read as miracle or bhakti-poetry, the heart of the story is clear: Sakhūbāī did not let bitterness become her identity. The world around her was harsh, but her mind found refuge in Bhagavān.
She did not disturb others through retaliation, and she did not let suffering destroy her devotion.
This reflects the flower-and-diamond quality: soft in heart, strong in endurance.
5. Story of Śrī Śāradā Devī and the Difficult Devotee
Many kinds of people came to Śrī Śāradā Devī — gentle devotees, troubled seekers, confused householders, and people carrying guilt or emotional pain.
Once, a devotee came in a disturbed state, speaking restlessly and seeking comfort. An ordinary person might have become impatient or judgmental.
Śrī Śāradā Devī received the person with motherly calm.
She listened. She did not frighten the person with harshness. She did not make them feel rejected because of their weakness. Her presence itself gave safety.
Yet she was not inwardly shaken by the person’s distress. She did not become agitated. She did not absorb the disturbance as her own. She remained steady, compassionate, and clear.
This is the quality praised in 12.15. Others were not disturbed by her presence; they felt protected. And she was not disturbed by the pain and confusion others brought to her.

rich_grammar.padacchedah:
यस्मात् न उद्विजते लोकः लोकात् न उद्विजते च यः ।
हर्षामर्षभयोद्वेगैः मुक्तः यः सः च मे प्रियः ॥
rich_grammar.pratipadarthah: यस्मात् | (yasmāt) = from whom / because of whom न | (na) = not उद्विजते | (udvijate) = is disturbed / agitated लोकः | (lokaḥ) = the world / people लोकात् | (lokāt) = from the world / by the world न | (na) = not उद्विजते | (udvijate) = is disturbed च | (ca) = and यः | (yaḥ) = who हर्षामर्षभयोद्वेगैः | (harṣāmarṣabhayodvegaiḥ) = by elation, intolerance/envy, fear, and anxiety मुक्तः | (muktaḥ) = free यः | (yaḥ) = who सः | (saḥ) = he च | (ca) = and मे | (me) = to Me / for Me प्रियः | (priyaḥ) = dear
rich_grammar.anvayah: यस्मात् लोकः न उद्विजते, यः च लोकात् न उद्विजते, यः हर्षामर्षभयोद्वेगैः मुक्तः, सः च मे प्रियः ।
rich_grammar.sandhi: यस्मात् + न = यस्मान् | Sandhi name: व्यञ्जनसन्धिः / अनुनासिकादेशः; final त् changes to न् before the following न्. | न + उद्विजते = नोद्विजते | Sandhi name: गुणसन्धिः; अ + उ becomes ओ. | यस्मान् + नोद्विजते = यस्मान्नोद्विजते | Sandhi name: संयोगसन्धिः; न् + न् becomes doubled न्न in continuous pronunciation. | लोकः + लोकात् = लोको लोकात् | Sandhi name: विसर्गसन्धिः; final अः becomes ओ before the following soft consonant ल्. | लोकात् + न = लोकान् | Sandhi name: व्यञ्जनसन्धिः / अनुनासिकादेशः; final त् changes to न् before न्. | न + उद्विजते = नोद्विजते | Sandhi name: गुणसन्धिः; अ + उ becomes ओ. | लोकान् + नोद्विजते = लोकान्नोद्विजते | Sandhi name: संयोगसन्धिः; न् + न् becomes doubled न्न in continuous pronunciation. | हर्ष + अमर्ष = हर्षामर्ष | Sandhi name: सवर्णदीर्घसन्धिः; अ + अ becomes आ. | भय + उद्वेग = भयोद्वेग | Sandhi name: गुणसन्धिः; अ + उ becomes ओ. | हर्षामर्षभयोद्वेगैः + मुक्तः = हर्षामर्षभयोद्वेगैर्मुक्तः | Sandhi name: विसर्गसन्धिः; final visarga changes to र् before the following soft consonant म्. | सः + च = स च | Sandhi name: विसर्गलोपः; final visarga is dropped before च्.
rich_grammar.samasa: हर्षामर्षभयोद्वेगैः = द्वन्द्व समासः | Breakdown: हर्षः च अमर्षः च भयम् च उद्वेगः च — by elation, intolerance/envy, fear, and anxiety.
rich_grammar.other: उद्विजते is लट्-लकारः, आत्मनेपदम्, from उद्-विज् धातुः, meaning “is disturbed” or “is agitated.” | मुक्तः is a क्तप्रत्ययान्त-कृदन्तम् from मुच् धातुः, meaning “freed” or “released.” | यस्मात् and लोकात् are पञ्चमी-विभक्ति forms indicating “from whom” and “from the world.” | हर्षामर्षभयोद्वेगैः is तृतीया-बहुवचनम्, showing the factors from which the devotee is free. | The implied subject throughout is the parā-bhakta described in the sequence of verses.

key_verses.final_takeaway: The mature devotee neither disturbs others nor is disturbed by the world — soft like a flower when dealing with people, firm like a diamond when facing life's difficulties. Freedom from elation, intolerance, fear, and anxiety marks true inner liberation.
